Satya： Yeah, I mean, I think this is where, again, how we think about education—recently I met with the founders of Alpha School and learned a lot about what they were going about, and it's fascinating to listen to how to even rethink what is education really look like. Because I think it's actually very important. And I'm not saying anything traditionally being done is less important, right? I was even looking at—it's fascinating to see, I forget which Stanford class it was, the Asian guidelines for CS something—because you still need people to learn. Like, it was an interesting AI class that they were making sure people were learning how to apply softmax appropriately versus saying, "hey, fix my training run."

So I think learning concepts is important, it's going to be critical. But the way we create the incentives, what are the credentials, how we value those credentials, what is the employment opportunity for those credentials—so I think there's a complete change that has to happen, given the way to get to information, the way to educate yourself, the way to continuously keep yourself updated has changed so much.

So I think, interestingly enough, maybe the next big startup and success story could be someone who builds a new university, or a new pedagogy even, of how to get someone to go through a curriculum and find economic opportunity. That's highly valuable.

主持人： Well, that has felt perhaps impossible for a long time, but it's a great note to end on and something that might be possible. Yeah. Thank you, Satya.

Satya： Thank you so much. Thank you. We appreciate it. Thank you all.

节目结束语

主持人： Find us on Twitter at @NoPriorsPod. Subscribe to our YouTube channel if you want to see our faces. Follow the show on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or wherever you listen. That way, you get a new episode every week. And sign up for emails or find transcripts for every episode at no-priors.com.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的洞察：Satya 正在偷偷地把"资产负债表"重新发明
在所有访谈表层讨论中，最容易被忽略的一句话是："**company veteran agent ... should in fact go onto the balance sheet**（公司老员工式的 agent 实际上应该进资产负债表）"。
这句话表面像是一句修辞，但它实际上是在宣布一场**会计学革命**。过去 200 年，人力资本因为"无法被持有、无法被估值、会自己走人"，从来无法被记入资产端——只能作为费用流过利润表。但如果一家公司能用 trace + private eval 把一个员工的隐性知识"蒸馏"进一个 agent，这个 agent： - 不会离职 - 可以被复制 - 有边际成本为零的服务能力 - 拥有可被审计的"工作历史"
它在会计意义上突然满足了"可控制 + 未来经济利益 + 可计量"这三个资产定义的全部要件。Sarah 顺势开的那句玩笑"SEC 要为 token expertise 制定会计准则"其实并不是玩笑——这是 Satya 在为下一个十年的**企业估值模型**埋伏笔。一旦市

item.title: [Lex Clips] Anti-matter & nuclear weapons: Why technology is always a double-edge sword | Don Lincoln
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的悖论：人类越追求"清洁能源"，越在制造"更危险的火"

视频中 Don Lincoln 把核能与火并列，这是一个看似平淡却极具颠覆性的类比。表面上这是在为科学辩护——"火也会烧房子"——但真正的洞见藏在他没说出口的那一层：人类文明史上每一次"能量密度"的跃升，都同时是"破坏密度"的跃升。

  • 木柴的能量密度 → 烧毁村庄
  • 煤炭 → 工业革命与帝国战争
  • 石油 → 全球供应链与全球性轰炸
  • 裂变 → 城市级的瞬时蒸发
  • 反物质 → 理论上 1 克可释放约 43 千吨 TNT 当量

这意味着：我们正在追求的"终极清洁能源"，本质上是"终极武器"的另一面孔。这不是技术伦理问题，而是一个热力学层面的几何规律——能量越集中，越自由，也越无法被任何单一文明"驯化"。Lex 说"我们要走那条线"，但事实上，那条线本身正在以能量密度的平方加速变窄。

二、跨领域连接：核物理与"语言模型"的同构性——都是"对称性破缺"的产物

这是一个出人意料的连接：反物质与人工智能，在结构上是同一种东西。

宇宙之所以存在物质（而非物质-反物质完全湮灭后空无一物），是因为早期宇宙发生了一种"对称性破缺"（CP violation）——某种微小的偏倚让物质赢了反物质一筹，于是有了星系、有了你我。

而大语言模型的"智能"也来自同一种几何动作：在高维概率空间中，模型必须打破"所有 token 等可能"的对称，引入一个微小但持续的偏倚，才能让"意义"从噪声中浮现。

所以 Don 说"理解反物质可能解锁新能源"，真正深层的暗示是：任何能够"操控对称性破缺"的技术——无论是反物质引擎、还是 AGI——都不是在"产生"能量或智能，而是在收割宇宙最初那次不对称的剩余红利。这两条技术路径在哲学上将不可避免地汇合。

三、最反直觉的观察：科学家其实并不"创造"危险，他们只是揭露宇宙早已埋好的地雷

视频里 Don 有一句被低估的话："science is …… finding power that nature has presented to us."

请注意这个动词——finding，而不是 making。

这暗含一个令人不寒而栗的世界观：

核武器、反物质炸弹、未来的真空衰变武器……它们在宇宙诞生那一刻就已经"在那里"了。科学家不是发明者，而是考古学家——在挖掘一座 138 亿年前就被埋好的、布满陷阱的遗迹。

这把"科技伦理"的讨论从"我们该不该造"，彻底翻转为"我们迟早会发现，问题只是谁先发现"。

由此可以推出一个更尖锐的预测：

  • 文明的真正过滤器（Great Filter），不是核战争本身，
  • 而是文明的"发现速度"超过"社会消化速度"的那个临界差值。

Don 说"这必须是全社会的对话"——但他没说的是：人类社会的认知更新速度，在过去 200 年里几乎没变（仍是"一代人 ≈ 25 年"），而能量发现速度每 30 年翻一个数量级。这条剪刀差，才是费米悖论真正的答案候选之一。

换句话说：我们最该害怕的，不是反物质，而是我们理解反物质的速度比理解自己快得多。

📝 转录文本

一、近期前景：核裂变与核聚变

It's very clear that nuclear fusion and nuclear fission will unlock huge amount of energy that's required for a civilization to flourish, but that's almost like near-term.

Mhm.

二、远期前景：暗能量与反物质

Longer-term, you can think about things like — we'll talk about dark energy crisis and antimatter. Maybe if you figure out some of the mysteries around antimatter, that too would lead to energy sources, how to produce energy. That too might lead to counterintuitive propulsion systems —

Mhm.

— for us humans to travel through through the universe.

Now, right now it seems far-fetched, too expensive, too complicated, too difficult, but breakthroughs in the fundamental theoretical physics might lead us to unlock some incredible energy sources, incredible technologies that uh will uh allow humans to explore the universe.

三、技术的双刃剑

And of course, we should also mention that as always with technologies, it's a double-edged sword. It will most likely lead to the development of more dangerous weapons or other sources of harm. And then we uh as a civilization kind of have to walk that uh line and hope we figure out how to do more good than bad with the technologies we build.

Right.

四、火的类比：科学只是"发现"自然的力量

But but we have to really remember — while people worry about nuclear weapons, which are admittedly very dangerous, and even nuclear power, which has waste that has to be dealt with, what science is doing is working out, finding power that nature has presented to us.

This is not new. Fire is like that, too. Fire can burn down your house, or it can cook your steak. Power is like that, and that's just something that we have to understand as humanity.

五、科学是全社会的对话

And that's why this needs to be a — you know, when we talk about science, it has to be a broad conversation by all of society. Because what scientists can do is figure out how the world works. Society has to figure out how we wish to apply that or not apply that.

六、解开宇宙之谜本身就很"awesome"

Also, solving the mysteries and the puzzles of the universe in itself is effing awesome.

It is. It is.

So, I mean, that's the thing that makes us human — in part — is looking at a thing and saying, "How does this work?"

Sure — together uh a bunch of apes get together, like poke the thing, kind of shake the thing, and then over time you have rockets going out into space. You build roads and bridges. You build the internet.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Mercatus Center] Glory Liu on Adam Smith's America
item.dc:creator: Mercatus Center
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最意外的观察：斯密的"看不见的手"其实是一种"政治反讽"，而非市场颂歌

最颠覆直觉的事实是——"看不见的手"出现在《国富论》第四卷批判重商主义的章节里，而不是出现在歌颂市场的章节。换句话说，斯密这只"手"原本是写给被国家俘获的政治经济体系的解药，而非一句赞美自由市场的口号。Glory Liu 的精彩之处在于点出：她在课堂上几乎"懒得讲它"——因为这个短语的伟大不在文本本身，而在 250 年里被层层涂抹的反射光。

这里藏着一个更深的悖论：当代许多自称"斯密派"的人最反对"系统设计者"（the man of system），却在不自觉中把"看不见的手"本身变成了一种新的 system thinking。他们用斯密反对的方式来捍卫斯密。这是一种近乎佛家式的"以指为月"——指头被供奉，月亮却被遗忘。

二、跨领域连接：斯密的"商人–议员合谋"模型 ≈ 信息论中的"非对称信道" ≈ 互联网时代的算法俘获

斯密在 4.1.10 段的句子值得反复咀嚼："商人完全知道这政策如何使他们自己致富，但如何使国家致富并不是他们的本职。" 这句话如果用今天的语言重写，它就是一个信息不对称的博弈论模型，甚至可以视作 20 世纪 70 年代 Stigler 与 Peltzman "管制俘获理论"（regulatory capture）的鼻祖——比公共选择学派早了整整两个世纪。

更出人意料的跨域连接：这个结构在今天算法时代被完美复刻。平台公司之于监管者，正如东印度公司之于英国议会——一方掌握技术细节、收益机制与话语权，另一方掌握合法性印章却"知道自己什么都不知道"。斯密所说的"部分坚实、部分诡辩"（partly solid, partly sophistical）的论证形态，恰恰是今天 AI 监管、加密货币游说、平台反垄断辩论的元结构。斯密提供的不是经济学，而是一套关于"何种谬误最容易通过政治系统"的认识论。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：纪念活动本身就是一种"政治经济现象"

Glory 揭示了一个被严重低估的元层级真相：我们对斯密的纪念方式本身，就是斯密所说的那种"被结构所塑造的注意力分配"。

1976 年（200 周年）的纪念由经济学家主导，焦点是《国富论》——那是冷战后期、芝加哥学派如日中天的时刻。 2023–2026 年（300 周年/250 周年）的纪念由政治理论家、历史学家、文学学者主导，焦点转向《道德情操论》——这恰好是"新自由主义"被反思、国家–市场关系被重审的时刻。

换句话说，每个时代选择性地"激活"斯密的某一面，作为它自我理解的镜子。这与 Warren Samuels 1977 年的"选择性过滤"洞察形成跨越半世纪的回响。

这里有一个让人惊讶的推论：如果你想预测未来十年的政治经济思潮转向，观察学界正在重新挖掘斯密的哪一卷可能比读任何宏观预测都更准。今天人们重新关注《国富论》第五卷（教育、司法、税收、公共品）——这暗示着一场关于"国家能力"与"公共基础设施"的悄然回归。Glory 与 Kristen 没有明说，但这正是她们对话的潜文本：斯密研究的潮汐，是政治经济秩序焦虑的灵敏地震仪。

四、彩蛋洞察：你对"看不见的手"觉得无聊，恰恰是你成熟的标志

Glory 和 Kristen 都坦承："读这一段觉得有点 boring。" 这看似自嘲，其实是一个深刻的智识信号：当一个思想从"启示"退化为"常识"，它就完成了它的历史使命，并悄悄变成了新的盲点。 真正活着的斯密，藏在那些让你皱眉、让你不确定、让你想反驳的段落里——比如他对议员遭受"暴力威胁"的描述，比如他对道德情操腐化的那一章。无聊感是一种思想化石化的味道。 你应该警惕你不再被冒犯的经典。

📝 转录文本

开场介绍

Welcome to Virtual Sentiments. I'm Kristen Collins, political theorist and senior fellow at the FA Hive program for advanced study in philosophy, politics, and economics at the Mercatus Center at George Mason University.

On today's episode, I talked to Dr. Glory Liu. Glory is an assistant professor in the department of government at Georgetown University, specializing in the history of political thought, intellectual history, and political economy. She is the author of Adam Smith's America: How a Scottish Philosopher Became an Icon of American Capitalism, from Princeton University Press, published in 2022.

As I mentioned in our season premiere with another Smith scholar, Maria Pia Paganelli, this year we are commemorating the 250th anniversary of Smith's Wealth of Nations. This conversation with Glory complements my earlier interview with Maria, while also touching on the second big 250th anniversary this year — the Declaration of Independence and the American Founding.

Glory and I situate Smith's concept of the invisible hand within his larger critique of what he termed the mercantile system of Great Britain. We talk about how much this part of Smith stands out given the problems that today's liberal democracies face with respect to state capture, which was a common theme of this season so far, with episodes with Sam Bagg and Henry Farrell.

But given Glory's expertise in reception history — that is to say, how readers of Smith in different times and places interpreted his work in conjunction with their own questions and concerns — Glory helps us think critically and deeply about what we do when we seek to draw lessons from past thinkers who are living in a wildly different context than we are. She helps us think about what it means to commemorate a historical figure or text. How do we do justice to the distinctiveness of that person, of their writing, and the context in which they were working, while acknowledging the impact that they or their work has had on our past and on ourselves? How can we engage with history on its own terms while exploring how we might learn from it to confront the challenges of our own day?

Glory helps us explore these questions and much more. So, let's get to it.

嘉宾欢迎与缘起

On today's episode of Virtual Sentiments, we're talking to Dr. Glory Liu. She's an assistant professor in the department of government at Georgetown University, specializing in the history of political thought, intellectual history, and political economy. You may also know her already as the author of Adam Smith's America: How a Scottish Philosopher Became an Icon of American Capitalism. Thanks so much for joining us, Glory.

Thanks so much for having me, Kristen.

As you know, I am so excited to talk to you. And one of the many reasons why is not just that you are such an expert on Smith, but you're an expert in his reception, especially in America. And so today, in this year with Virtual Sentiments, we are somewhat informally celebrating and commemorating the anniversary of Smith's Wealth of Nations. It is 250 years old as of this March, and a similar anniversary of the American Declaration of Independence. So I thought, who better to guide us through commemoration of these two anniversaries? What does it mean for us to commemorate Smith, and also what is Smith teaching through his work, and how has it been received over time as we continue to read him today for our own reflections and insights on political economy? So thanks so much for talking with us today about those issues.

Yeah, thanks for having me. I'll just start by saying, you know, when I first started the project that then became the book, I was like, "Oh, three people are going to read this. What a weird thing to be writing about, right?" Like, the reception of Smith's ideas in America. And when the book was finally published, it was right before the 300th anniversary of Smith's birth — so the tercentenary that happened in 2023. And I was asked to do all this stuff for the tercentenary and talk about Smith and his legacy, kind of reception. And I was like, "Okay, now I'm finally done with that. I can move on. I can move on from Adam Smith." And they're like, "Oh, but 2026 is the 250th anniversary not only of the Wealth of Nations, but also of, you know, American independence." And I'm like, "Oh god, I will never be done with this."

And I don't say that as a kind of full complaint, or complaint only, but more that I really didn't realize how long-lasting and perennial these questions about the entanglement of Smith's ideas and American capitalism have been and would be. So yeah, I'm always game to talk about Smith and American capitalism.

Well, good. I'm glad. Although I totally appreciate, you know, you have a lot to say, so feel free to bring in other work that you do and other insights and interests you have too. So I don't want you to feel like you have to be limited, but I also think it is a sign of how much of an expert you are on these issues, on Smith. And I think especially today — I mean, not to... we're going to, I think, stick more to history, to the text, but also the history of reception, and also guiding, I think, our audience and exploring with each other about the idea of: what do we do when we read these historical texts and do so from a place of our own kind of political interests and questions and puzzles and prejudices? What does that do for how people tend to understand the text, the legacy that we have? So I think that you are really a perfect person to have this kind of conversation. But we'll kind of go, I think throughout the conversation, from trying to understand the text on its own terms, but then also recognizing that as we do that, we have to reckon with this broader legacy.

You already framed this conversation quite well, but I wondered if you could just start a little bit more with how you approached your own work — giving us a reception history in your book — and sort of an overview, so that people have a familiarity with what you produced.

什么是接受史？

Sure. So the way I see it, a reception history is about the distance between the initial conception of the text, the ideas, the author's original intentions, and what became of that text. So I say this in the book that it's the line between intention and impact, or intention and influence.

And sometimes we think about the impact of a book as being inherent in the text itself, right? The impact of the Wealth of Nations is that it is the founding book of economics, that Smith kind of brought to life these ideas, and that the ideas themselves contain the meaning. The point of view that reception history takes says that it's not just the ideas themselves, but what readers and subsequent readers, engagers, debaters do with those ideas. What do readers make of those ideas? And people make meaning out of ideas in ways that often wildly diverge from the author's original intentions. And Smith's Wealth of Nations, and the rest of his corpus, is really kind of a perfect case study for that distance between authorial intention and what subsequently was made up of Smith's ideas.

So that's the perspective of reception history, right? That we're trying to make sense of what the demands and the needs and interests of Smith's subsequent readers were in their time, and try not to judge them — whether they got Smith right or wrong, whether their ideas and their readings were good or bad — but just to understand them in their world. Why were they drawn to Smith in the first place? And what did they do with Smith, and what consequences did that have for subsequent generations? That's the perspective and that's the method that I adopt as somebody doing reception history.

That perspective is often in tension with how we as political theorists or historians of political thought want to read or teach Smith, right? Which is, when we present Smith to our students and we say, okay, what was Smith doing in his time? What is the meaning of the invisible hand? What is the meaning of self-interest? What did he mean by sympathy? And why is that so important? And that's not a wrong perspective by any means. You know, to be totally transparent, I do that when I teach Smith to my students.

But I also want to make it super clear that I am teaching Smith as somebody who created these ideas in a particular context, was responding to certain questions in his time — whether they were questions about the nature of morality or kind of problems of commercial modernity and trade, you know, however you want to frame the questions in Smith's time. I make it very apparent to my students: this is how I'm going to teach you these texts using this approach. But I also want to make it clear that this is not how a lot of people have read Smith over time. So I like to plant that idea, that texts and ideas take on a different life of their own outside of the context in which they were originally written, so that students are kind of attuned to the way that ideas take on different lives and have different meaning for different people. There rarely is a single authoritative version of a single text and idea. And again, I think Smith is kind of an exemplary thinker in that regard.

如何教授斯密：《道德情操论》

Yeah. Excellent. And so I thought maybe one way in is if you could give us more of how you might teach Smith in terms of the historical context. We're not going to do a full class session here by any means, but feel free to maybe just give what you think are some important details for us to have a grounding in Wealth of Nations especially — whether it's particular parts of the argument, the context in which he was responding to (what kind of conversation was he a part of), or things like the invisible hand that people are familiar with, and how you would teach that in a class.

Oh gosh, that's a really good question. And I'll just say upfront that I've taught Smith in a bunch of different contexts. I've taught a semester-long class exclusively on Smith. I've done one-off lectures where I get one hour to teach the Theory of Moral Sentiments and the Wealth of Nations. I've done it over two weeks. There's no perfect way to do this. And it's going to be very context dependent. I teach Smith differently in the course that I was teaching at Hopkins, which is a course on social theories of the economy, than I would if I were giving a guest lecture in a kind of history of political thought class. So take all of that with a grain of salt.

I'm going to split this up into kind of two chunks. I'll talk a bit about how I frame the Theory of Moral Sentiments and how I frame the Wealth of Nations.

So, typically when I teach the Theory of Moral Sentiments, I start with a title — the Theory of Moral Sentiments. What kind of theory is it? I say, well, it's kind of explanatory or phenomenological theory, right? It's not necessarily prescriptive theory, though there are aspects of it that seem very, very normative. And in Smith's time, it was very common to mix the kind of normative with descriptive language. So I want students to be attuned to how different it is as a theoretical text and also as an empirical text.

Then I'll say, okay, what does moral mean? Okay, moral is not just ethical. It means something closer to psychological or social. Right? Moral is used in contrast to the physical or the natural. Sentiments — okay, why sentiments? In contrast to reason. Smith is engaging in this debate that stretches back to the 16th, 17th century about the nature of morality. Is it grounded in reason or is it sentiment?

Okay, well, so the title already signals that he's trying to do something pretty expansive here. He's trying to offer a broad phenomenological theory of where our ideas of morality come from, and we have some indication that they're grounded in sentiment. But as we work through the text, we'll see that it's a little bit more complicated than that.

So I like to frame it that way because (a) it helps students just in really basic terms get a hold of what this text is up to, and (b) it situates Smith in this longer debate about human nature and the nature of morality.

And then I usually only have time to cover things like sympathy. So we'll read the very first passage out loud, and we kind of break down: what is Smith doing? Why is he saying that sympathy is this mechanism by which we project our imagination into somebody else's shoes, and that's how we kind of fill in the gaps of what people might be feeling and how we make sense of our own feelings? So I make sure to talk a lot about sympathy, the imagination, the impartial spectator.

And then as a little teaser — and this might be controversial because of debates in the Smith scholarship — but I love leaving students with this open question of the chapter in, gosh, 1.3.11, the section on the corruption of our moral sentiments. And again, because it has been at the center of so many scholarly debates about corruption in commercial society, that's precisely why I want to introduce it to students. Not because I want them to settle a scholarly dispute, but because it's such a great launchpad for talking about the relationship between morality and what we can call commercial society, market society. What is Smith doing? And the number of times that students have been spot-on in anticipating scholarly debates about, like, "well, no, no, he's actually criticizing the accumulation of wealth and our admiration for the wealthy," and then other people are like, "No, I think he's just describing a weird quirk of human psychology" — right? Those are the debates that I like to kind of foster in class, to get students to engage with a text and to realize that there is ambiguity and that there are differences in interpretations, but also to start thinking about connections across Smith's works.

That's kind of my template for how I introduce the Theory of Moral Sentiments. There are a ton of ways to do this. I mean, you could spend weeks on like Part Six, on the character of virtue. I don't go too deeply into that only because I feel like I would have to really dig into, like, virtue ethics and classical versus Christian morality and ethics and stuff. And because I typically teach the Theory of Moral Sentiments followed by the Wealth of Nations, I find that the segue with that kind of section on the corruption of moral sentiments just works better as a teaching device.

如何教授斯密：《国富论》的总体架构

Okay. And I'll try to be quick. Again with the Wealth of Nations, I try to give people a really clear architecture, because it's so easy to read the Wealth of Nations piecemeal, and if you only have one session, you can't teach all, like, 1000 pages. But I am super insistent on giving students excerpts from every single book. So that's quite different than if you were to take this in a history of economics class — you're probably really only going to read excerpts from Book One and Two, maybe Book Five, maybe Book Four, but I do all five books.

So it tends to be a lot of reading. But I tell students, "Okay, the goal of this class is to see how everything hangs together. We're not just going to focus on the invisible hand. We're not just going to focus on mercantilism. We're not just going to focus on the weird bits about education. I want you to see the architecture of Smith's work."

So I try to lay that out for them. It's an Inquiry, not a manifesto, into the Nature and Causes — right? What is the nature of wealth? He's got to define what wealth is. And that's also one of the radical moves that he makes: he's offering a different definition of where wealth comes from. What causes it to increase or decrease? There's another claim that he's making. And it's also the wealth of nations, not the wealth of individuals, and not optimized choice-making under constraints. To really clearly signal to students that what we think of as economics is not at all what Smith was doing at the time.

And the reason why he's offering a different definition — not just definition, but a different conception of wealth — is because at the time, the definition of wealth was the mercantilist one. And that gets us into the context that helps us better understand why he has to start off with this long description of the pin factory and the woolen cloak and stuff. Just so that, right, wealth is generated through the advancement of the division of labor.

And then by the time we get to Book Four, we get the critique of the mercantile system. We better understand: oh right, he's writing in this time where this different understanding of wealth as kind of gold and silver and the balance of trade was firmly entrenched in the political system. How is he going to overturn that? How does he explain the durability of that system? And how does he overturn it?

Then that helps us set up Book Five, which is usually kind of this weird tacked-on assemblage of stuff — education, militias, taxes, justice, and stuff. But I think once you teach the arc of the Wealth of Nations in this way — the argument and theory builds — you better understand what Smith is doing in Book Five. And I have a whole paper on that that I won't go into, but that's how I would say I frame it. And that's kind of my motivation for teaching the Wealth of Nations in the way that I do.

重商主义体系：斯密的真正靶子

No, excellent. I think that makes sense. And so let's then start with understanding that architecture of the text a little bit more. And I just want to dig in maybe specifically to the idea of him constructing alternatives to, and an argument against, what he saw as the dominant system — the mercantile system. So you did kind of lay out the details, but I want to make sure that we're giving the language to people who are going to be listening, of different academic disciplines or different levels of experience in this literature in general. So would you mind giving us more of what that meant? The balance of trade, for example.

Yeah. So Book Four of the Wealth of Nations is called "Of Systems of Political Economy." And here he kind of gives an overview of what he calls the commercial system of Great Britain — or the mercantile system, sometimes called mercantilism, but he uses the phrase mercantile system.

And what he's doing there is basically outlining what was the dominant political and economic order in Great Britain — which is this view that wealth consists of gold and silver coin in the treasury, and that the more gold and silver coin the treasury hoarded, the wealthier you were. And the best way to do this was to maintain a favorable balance of trade, which is to export more than you import, which often meant creating policies that privileged companies in the business of exporting slash importing — trading. So that a certain company would have exclusive privileges to, say, import and export tea between India and the British colonies in North America, which is exactly what happened with the British East India Company.

So this is the policy for well over a century. I'm going to get my economic history wrong here, so please don't quote me on that. But this is the dominant political and economic order in which Smith is writing.

细读文本：货币即财富的"流行观念"

And in this chapter on the mercantile system — I'm going to pull up my book here. So I'm using the standard edition, so to speak, the Liberty Fund edition. And I'm on page 429, Book Four, Chapter One, "Of the Principle of the Commercial or Mercantile System." So this is the first paragraph where he says:

"That wealth consists in money, or in gold and silver, is a popular notion which naturally arises from the double function of money, as the instrument of commerce and the measure of value."

So this idea that money is wealth as well as the kind of medium of exchange — it's a popular notion, why not? But then he kind of goes on and is like, okay, well, you know, we say that a guy is rich because he's got a lot of money. Do we also say a nation is rich because we have a lot of money? Sure, this seems plausible, right? And what he's doing here is he's reconstructing how it is the logic of money as national wealth becomes the dominant logic and the political and economic order of the time.

部分坚实，部分诡辩

So by the time we get to page 433, he's basically saying: okay, well, how did the arguments for mercantilism actually take into effect? It's like, okay, this is pretty… it seems like pretty sound logic, right? National wealth consists in money, export more than import, seems right. He says:

"These arguments were partly solid and partly sophistical. Partly solid, so far as they asserted that the exportation of gold and silver in trade might frequently be advantageous to the country."

He's like, "Yeah, that makes sense. That seems fairly solid." And then he says:

"But they were sophistical, in supposing that either to preserve or to augment the quantity of these metals required more the attention of government than to preserve or to augment the quantity of any other useful commodities, which the freedom of trade, without any such attention, never fails to supply in the proper quantity."

So he's saying, okay, but these arguments also had this flawed logic in thinking that in order to augment trade, you needed the protection of government — these special policies. And this is where I think he does such a great job in saying: it's not surprising that this theory of wealth became this ideology and then a political order, because it's partly solid and partly sophistical. Things that are kind of part-sound, part-unsound tend to be the arguments that work.

关键之处：论证作用于"谁"

And my favorite part is what he does in the next paragraph. So 4.1.10, page 434, says:

"Such as they were, however, those arguments convinced the people to whom they were addressed."

And this, I think, is the critical part. The point is not about the soundness of the argument, it's that they worked on the people to whom they were supposed to work on.

"They were addressed by merchants to parliaments and to the councils of princes, to nobles and to country gentlemen; by those who were supposed to understand trade, to those who were conscious to themselves that they knew nothing about the matter."

So again, it's like — I like going slowly here to point out to students — it's not just about the logic of the argument. It's about who's presenting the argument to whom. People who are kind of in this position of structural advantage, the merchants, who can make these convincing arguments about how this is supposed to augment national wealth to people who are supposed to know about this but not really. And they're supposed to make decisions.

"That foreign trade enriched the country, experience demonstrated to the nobles and country gentlemen, as well as to the merchants; but in what manner it enriched it, was a matter of some dispute. The merchants knew perfectly in what manner it enriched themselves. It was their business to know it. But to know in what manner it enriched the country, was no part of their business."

So that's the other thing that I underscore: merchants know exactly how this is going to benefit themselves at the expense of the public. It is no part of their business to know how this policy is going to benefit the public.

商人与公共利益的根本对立

And then I tie that back to something that Smith says in Book One, where he says that, look, in these three great orders of men — people who live by wages, people who live off of profits (the merchants), and people who live off of rents (the landowners) — anytime there's a policy being debated that has to do with commerce or trade, whatever the merchants say, it is almost certainly going to be against the public interest. Because he knows that merchants — they're not evil, but their interests are directly opposite of that of the public. And that's the thing that he underscores in this chapter, and I think it's so easy to miss if you don't pick up on this thread.

国家俘获、权力不对称与结构思维

No, I think that's exactly right, and I agree completely. And I think one reason why this matters is because often there was, you know, very long time, a legacy of reading Smith and focusing on the invisible hand, when the invisible hand passage itself occurs in this book — in the book on mercantilism, in the sections talking about mercantilism and talking about restrictions on trade. And so here, by setting this up, we show that the political system itself, restrictions on trade, were themselves — what the language that we would use — of state capture, of merchants being able to influence policy. And part of that being because of a lack of knowledge on the part of those who are directing policy and politicians. And it's not necessarily that either party is especially evil or especially vicious.

It's also not that legislators are just kind of derpy, right?

Yeah.

There's this asymmetry of knowledge and interests, and merchants are working with legislators, and legislators are very happily going along with what merchants are saying, trusting them, saying like, "Yeah, sure. If you say that this is going to increase national wealth, sure, let's do it." So it is a form of state capture that is subversive in outcome, but not necessarily with this malicious, devious intention right from the get-go.

And he's quite critical, and so I think in a lot of passages he comes across quite critical. And that's why, in fact, this is actually — I'll set aside, but there are moments throughout where he'll, in the chapters on the British East India Company, he specifically talks about the structure of having a monopolistic company and how that shapes behavior. And he specifically is saying it's not just individuals, but it's the system.

Yeah, exactly.

And so that kind of systems thinking and structural thinking is so crucial. And I think even though he is willing to — even though he's so measured so often in his writing — though he has these moments of strong critique. And so if you focus too much on that, then you might miss that ultimately he's a very structural thinker.

Yes. And he cares about power. I think that's the other thing that I emphasize. And this is where the kind of political theorist in me comes out a little bit: I draw my students' attention to Smith's attention to power and power asymmetry. Like, who doesn't have power? The ordinary worker here. Legislation is stacked against them. Because if ordinary workers try to combine, then the law comes slamming down on them. But it's very easy for masters and employers to combine, and to ensure that labor movements and agitations don't make it anywhere, and to have passed legislation in favor of the employer.

So power and power asymmetry play a huge role in how Smith sees economic life. And that's the other thing I underscore: political economy is an attentiveness to power and how that shapes economic relations.

Yeah, that's right. And it's always a feedback kind of dynamic, because institutions and laws partly contribute to why workers can't combine as easily and agitate as easily. But then it's a vicious cycle, by which because their employers have more political power, they can entrench that system more easily too.

斯密反对的不是政府，而是"被俘获的政府"

Yeah. And there's one more thing that I wanted to say, because you brought up the invisible hand thing. Which is that, you know, this section of the Wealth of Nations — this whole book, Book Four of the Wealth of Nations — is often seen as, like, this is Smith at his most anti-government. You know, in his most anti-government mode ever. And the invisible hand is often used in contrast to — right, it's the invisible hand of the market as opposed to the heavy hand of government.

And, you know, what we've just talked about here — Smith's outline of the commercial system of Great Britain and kind of mercantile collusion — it is not Smith saying "government sucks, down with the government." He's saying, no, this particular alignment of wealth and power, and using the law to rig the economy, if you will, that is the problem. It's not "government intervention in the economy is bad." It is the specific alignment of a social class that uses wealth in order to capture legislation in order to rig the economy. That is the target.

一段炽热的修辞：自由贸易与垄断者的怒火

I completely agree. And if you will indulge me, I will read the — I think you brought us into the more measured. I'm going to read some of that more inflammatory rhetoric.

Oh yeah, I love inflammatory Smith.

The passage — I know — the passage is Book Four, Part Two, paragraph 43, on page 471 of the Liberty Fund. So here he's talking about freedom of trade, which is what he seems to be arguing for, but then he's also acknowledging just how hard that is. So:

"To expect, indeed, that the freedom of trade should ever be entirely restored in Great Britain, is as absurd as to expect that an Oceana or Utopia should ever be established in it. Not only the prejudices of the public, but what is much more unconquerable, the private interests of many individuals, irresistibly oppose it."

Then he compares two merchants to a standing army in the way in which the army officers might animate their soldiers. So does the employers animate, inflame their workmen to attack with violence and outrage the proposals of any regulation that would lead towards freedom of trade.

And then he says:

"The member of parliament who supports every proposal for strengthening this monopoly is sure to acquire not only the reputation of understanding trade, but great popularity and influence with an order of men whose numbers and wealth render them of great importance. If he opposes them, on the contrary, and still more if he has authority enough to be able to thwart them, neither the most acknowledged probity, nor the highest rank, nor the greatest public service, can protect him from the most infamous abuse and detraction, from personal insults, nor sometimes from real danger, arising from the insolent outrage of furious and disappointed monopolists."

Yeah, I love that passage because it shows how Smith is basically saying there are like no incentives in the political system to do otherwise. Because if a legislator — the legislator is basically so incentivized to go along with the monopolists, because he gets all this praise and public approbation and stuff. But doing otherwise subjects you to, like, violence, basically.

Actual danger, like — that is a very scary image, where it's both the, yeah, like you said, it's both the kind of carrot of the veneer of intellectual authority and of being associated with — you know, he has other passages about the way people admire the rich, like you said, including that additional chapter in Theory of Moral Sentiments. But it's also in general the sort of aesthetic authority he has other passages on. But then also the actual fear, the actual danger. This is a description of a society that's heavily, I would say even you could even say to some degree politicized. I mean, that's not — I'm not saying that in a normative sense of, like — it's just to say that like there is no world without politics. There is no world where this sort of economics can rule all. But instead, often these economic systems are both a product of and shape the political system too.

Yes. Yeah, 100%.

"看不见的手"：被放大的隐喻

And so I just wanted — yeah. So why don't we — well, because the invisible hand kind of does come out here, and I kind of always feel like, if we're going to be talking Smith and Wealth of Nations, we have to invisible hand. So when we have these passages and we're putting it in this context, like, how do you understand it? Like how do you bring that in?

Yeah. Let's see. There are two ways that I've done this. And I will say that the more common way is to barely mention it.

Yeah.

I know. I'm going to alienate so many listeners of this podcast to be like, "I don't talk about it that much."

It's hard because it's something that has cache.

Yeah. Part of that is just because, again, from our perspective as a reception historian, I'm like, I just don't think it's that important. Like, it was made important. That's something that I want to bring out. Like, why did the invisible hand take on so much meaning? Why did it become this political stance to say, "I believe in the invisible hand, not the state intervention"?

But, like, look. I do say this is the setup for when Smith talks about the invisible hand. Keep in mind, he mentions the invisible hand once in the Theory of Moral Sentiments before this. But when we get to the invisible hand passage in Book Four, he's already set up the commercial system of Great Britain. And he does this kind of walkthrough of the logic of what would happen if a person was given the choice to invest their capital close to home or abroad. And he's like, "Well, you know, let's walk through this. Person with capital is probably going to invest closer to home because they know the culture more. They have a better sense of credit and reputation, and it's less risky than to send their capital abroad. So what happens? Well, he ends up kind of generating a little bit of economic growth. That was no part of his intention."

And so I kind of honestly, I kind of downplay it, and I make it seem really mundane, merely to illustrate that Smith has an underlying logic of socially beneficial unintended consequences. And that when it comes to talking about the choice of foreign versus local investment, that happened to be the example that took off.

Now again, I am positioning myself as somebody who is just kind of "meh" about the invisible hand. But again, I am a reception historian, and so I see it taking on meaning much later. And I know that other people will have lots of different things to say. As a counterpoint, I have also spent an entire class talking about just the invisible hand passage, and I made my students go through this incredibly tedious exercise of literally reading the entire chapter in class, marking it up by themselves, reconstructing the logic on the chalkboard. And the payoff was, like, fine. They were like, "Yeah, this makes sense. Cool."

So yeah, I think again, my sensibilities are to basically illustrate the core logic, but don't make a big deal out of it. To the extent that I want to make a big deal out of it, I say that it was only much later that the phrase the invisible hand became what we associate it with today.

Well, I think that — no, I mean, and to be clear, the reason why I ask is because I share your same — it's not something that's particularly important to me. Or it's also just a little bit boring, and I'm like, "Yeah, this seems logical."

It seems that. And I say that in a little bit of a self-deprecating way too, because to some degree, I think we're attracted to things that maybe are more puzzling, they may take issue with. But it also just has such a legacy. It's so often the way that people kind of summarize Smith, or kind of the bumper-sticker Smith, that then once you actually sit down and read the text, it has this big shadow looming over it. And then when you see everything else, like the earlier sections you were talking about, about where he says, "Well, not every order, not every part of society, has their interest so perfectly aligned with the public interest, including merchants" — and that's how the mercantile, and that explains this mercantile system that he's critiquing — is crucial. And it seems to be at odds, but I think it's not entirely. But if you only think of the invisible hand, and that's all you think about, it'll make it harder to see the rest of the text.

当代意义：新自由主义与"以斯密反斯密"

And I also think that my main — actually, I will say, and this will get us — I want to talk more about your work in the reception history, and the idea, the kind of politics of commemorating. Because the other thing that I — and I'm going to frame it in this way, just to be transparent about some of my concerns — is that I think, you know, right now in our political moment, we're kind of faced with — you know, people talk about this sort of realignment or a real reckoning with what's referred to as neoliberalism on both the right and left. And people kind of also offering alternatives, and then often in those ways, maybe sometimes reading Smith in different ways.

So that sort of continual conversation, it goes on. But then there's always this concern I have, which on two levels: which is, one, the invisible hand itself could become a kind of system. I mean, so much of what people who emphasize the invisible hand like to argue against is — or what they also like from Smith — is when he criticizes the man of system, the man who wants to kind of reform politics according to this kind of perfect plan. And the invisible hand is this nice... and it can often serve as, like, intellectual humility, to remind us that control won't work and that we don't necessarily need control. But then there is also this risk where if you take this kind of metaphor too far, or you treasure it too much, then you might end up even unintentionally becoming a kind of man of system, or imposing a sort of system approach and not thinking with more political nuance or tact, or kind of what are the different conditions required to maintain liberal democratic institutions.

Yeah, 100%. And what I loved that you just did there was basically use Smith on Smith. Like, that you were being really consistent with Smith's own view about system thinking, and the dangers of taking kind of an ideal in the abstract and designing a whole political order, a whole social order, around it, thinking that you, the designer, have agency to, like, move the pieces on the chessboard, as he says. And so even what we think of, or what subsequent readers made of Smith's, like, the Smithian system of the invisible hand, is itself liable to becoming a type of system thinking. And that would be the most Smithian way of thinking about the possibilities and limitations of whatever you want to project onto the invisible hand.

Yeah. And then the second part of that, though, then too, is that as we reckon with that kind of legacy, and that kind of image, or that kind of risk — there is then also, because that's what a lot of other people might associate Smith with — they don't recognize everything he has to say about power, everything he has to say that the skepticism of the mercantile system comes from this deep concern with power dynamics, with how institutions enable the entrenchment of dominant orders of society, of employers, of the wealthy in various ways.

And so having to kind of go back to the text — I don't... the other thing is that on the one hand, I want to kind of show, look, this still might actually hold some insights for us. But we don't want our engagement with the text to be simple appeals to authority. We also have to be kind of conscious of what exactly is it that we're doing when we're going back to Smith.

Yeah, couldn't agree more.

纪念斯密的政治：从1876到2026

So I was wondering then if you could walk us through a little bit more on your thoughts on what we're doing when we're going back to Smith now. What are some of your favorite stories from the reception of Smith? Maybe if we're right now thinking about the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence — how was Smith received in the early decades of America?

Yeah. So you asked about kind of what it is that we're doing today when we're commemorating or celebrating Smith, and to what extent our celebrations today are different from the way Smith was celebrated, in vogue, debated, or whatever, in the longer reception of Smith. So thank you for asking that question. I've actually written a really short reflection piece on this that's coming out in a volume for the celebration of the 250th.

So, you know, to be clear, I think there are all kinds of versions of celebrating Smith or commemorating Smith today that we've seen. From the kind of merely decorative or ornamental — we just like to drop some quotes and say, "Oh, look, wasn't it so divinely ordained that the Wealth of Nations and the Declaration of Independence were both written in 1776, right? Like it's destiny that America and the origin text of capitalism came out in the same year." There are those kinds of things, and those are all over the place. And look, I've been conscripted into writing pieces for the Wall Street Journal on this, because they're doing this big retrospective on 250 years of American capitalism.

And, you know, again, I think that part of it is American exceptionalism — a perennial obsession with what, if anything, makes American capitalism exceptional or special. There's a critical version of that too: what, if anything, makes American capitalism kind of like the worst version of capitalism? Some people might ask. And I also think that there's something with Smith that's entangled — I wrote a whole book on this — with how America has viewed itself, how it's positioned itself against the old world.

19世纪的斯密：从教育者到南北战争

This was true in the early 1800s reception of Smith, in these American textbook writers and educators trying to say, "Look, we have all these resources on political economy, but Ricardo and Malthus are writing for this dire situation in England, and we've got green pastures, so we don't need Ricardo and Malthus. We need Smith."

But then there are also people debating whether Smith was really appropriate for a particular sectoral interest. During the Civil War, there was this big divide between free traders in the South — who really leaned into Smith as the founder of science of political economy, to say that free trade was founded in the science that Smith gave us, and so free trade should be the kind of national political order — versus people in the North, some of whom were anti-slavery, but many of whom were kind of more pro-manufacturing interests.

So again, Smith has always been this resource and this device to critically reflect on what is the constitution of American political economy. So that I think is kind of just the big takeaway — why Smith and our idea of America are always going to be kind of tangled with one another.

"Kitschy" 斯密：邀请、奖项、领带

You know, and then there's, like, the silly stuff and the kitschy stuff. Like I write about this in the book, like the 100th anniversary of the Wealth of Nations in 1876, and all these free traders getting together at a fancy restaurant and toasting the Wealth of Nations and making speeches, economics graduate students associations dressing up and giving out the Invisible Hand Award. There's the kitschy and the kind of cringy stuff that's going to happen no matter what. I find that stuff cute and fascinating. And there are deeper stories underneath it that have to do with the kind of trajectory of the discipline of economics and how it relates to politics and stuff.

关键转变：从《国富论》的斯密到《道德情操论》的斯密

I do think, getting to the meat of the question — which is, like, what is going on now? Is there anything different going on now? — I do think that the way in which we're talking about Smith, the way that we're commemorating Smith, celebrating Smith, debating Smith, is very different. And I don't want to say better or worse.

I think there's a tendency to be like, "Well, we have the most knowledge now. We are doing better than the Chicago School, and we're doing better than the people in 1926, and we're doing better than the people in 1876." It's like, okay, by what metrics? We have different resources now. We have different textual apparatuses available to us. But our knowledge is also the product of disciplinary evolution, the prerogatives and the directives of our professional organizations, disciplinary norms and all of that. So I hesitate to make claims about better or worse.

I do think the position on Smith has really shifted dramatically. I mean, if you look at the commemorations at the bicentenary in 1976, what was happening — it was dominated by economists, and it was happening at the University of Glasgow. This is the commemoration event, whatever you want to call it, that debuted the Glasgow editions, which are now the standard editions of the Wealth of Nations and the Theory of Moral Sentiments and kind of every text afterward. But the essays that came out of that were still mostly focused on the Wealth of Nations, still mostly written by economists, and the kind of revisionism hadn't quite taken off yet. We're still, like, just a couple years away from that. I mean, it's happening, but it is still Smith the Economist and Smith of the Wealth of Nations that dominates.

Now look at where we are now. 50 years later, and it's almost a complete reversal. It's, like, Smith of the Theory of Moral Sentiments. It's Smith the author of the Theory of Moral Sentiments that I think people want to reach for first, to be like, "No, no, no, no, first and foremost he's a moral philosopher, and it's our understanding of moral philosophy and the Enlightenment that should color the way we understand the Wealth of Nations and everything else."

Now, say what you want about that perspective. I do think that that is a marked difference, and I also think it shows attention and attentiveness to context, intertextual relations, and a much subtler and acknowledgment of the complexity of Smith as a humanist and also a human being.

You know, I was at the tercentenary celebrations in Glasgow, and the thing that I really walked away with was just like, we're celebrating Smith the man, not Smith the author of the Wealth of Nations. And I can see that carrying through. And it's not just in the scholarship that's being produced and where it's being produced and the scholars who are doing it. I mean, it's across all these different fields. You and I are political theorists, so we know that there's a huge contingent of political theorists leading this charge. But it's also historians, English, comparative literature, economics, heterodox economics — it's across all these different fields. And I think that that's super exciting. That we have different approaches to think with Smith, and that he's not just somebody you reach for for intellectual legitimation or proof of a theory, but that Smith is this kind of living resource where we're having a conversation with him about things that were present in his time and things that were present in our time.

I noticed that the themes for the 250th anniversary are exactly things like state capture, justice and political economy, revolutions. These are all things that we're talking about now. They had different meanings in Smith's time, but they were at the forefront of his mind. And so I can't emphasize enough that I do think that we've come to a more holistic appreciation of Smith as a thinker, and that just in the 50-year span between now and the last major celebrations, there's just been this incredibly marked shift in how we're approaching Smith.

一个梦想：注解版《国富论》与斯密周边

No, I think that's excellent. I mean, that's a great summation, and I think it is very exciting. And I guess I wonder — yeah, I mean, I think it's interesting to think about, though, how much of our current moment then owes itself to some degree to — I mean, I think things like the Glasgow edition of the collected works, and the Liberty Fund books especially, that people have come to rely on, our Bible so to speak, that have made that more holistic approach to Smith possible. So then you also kind of — I mean, not to be — I guess have forward thinking — but it's interesting to think about, like, what will be the legacy of these commemorations in the future.

I threw out this idea the last time I saw Craig Smith, and I was like, "You know what we need? We need, like, the annotated Wealth of Nations." Or not just the annotated — because there are annotations all over the Glasgow editions. But, like, I don't know if you have, like, the Landmark Thucydides or the Landmark Herodotus. But there are little inset maps, and there are all these references to all the people that are named, and dates, and all this other stuff. And I'm like, "Oh yeah, I would love, like, an interactive annotated Wealth of Nations where when he mentions this obscure land tax in France in the 1600s, like, I just want a little pop-up to tell me what that thing is. Or again, you know, he mentions 'Mr. Lock' or like 'Mr. Cantillon' — oh, you should have a little bit more than just like author, date of birth and date of death in there." But yeah, just a little bit more fleshed out, interactive. Maps are good.

I feel like, you know, anybody who takes on that project just be working on it until they die. So I remember telling this to Craig Smith and he was like, "Oh, oh yeah, I could see that." And I was like, "I volunteer to do one page."

Well, it's a collaborative... I mean, that's a great collaborative division of labor.

Yeah, exactly, exactly.

I mean, and that I agree completely, because there are even — you know, as well-informed as someone might be, there are still so many little details. And I think this goes for a lot of thinkers, where there were things that they took for granted that their audience at the time would know that they aren't going to make explicit.

Yeah, like, I don't know, like Dutch banking. You know, a former colleague of yours, Rossberg, who is this graduate student in Georgetown, like, wrote his entire dissertation on the influence of Dutch political economy on Adam Smith. And that was something that I kind of vaguely knew was out there but didn't dig into. And again, I was focusing more on the reception, but I'm like, "Oh yeah, that probably deserves a little bit more visibility than somebody just digging through all the insane footnotes and references in Smith's library." So that you get a sense of, like, what were the resources and guides that Smith used to construct his view of the world.

Yes, no, exactly. Well, and that is a very good aspiration. The idea of an annotated, more thoroughly annotated Wealth of Nations I love and is very highbrow and intellectual.

My lowbrow — my confession of what my lowbrow wish is: I would love — your book has this really great photo of Stigler's "Adam Smith's Best Friend."

Oh yeah.

I want that shirt. I want that shirt.

More of those.

I want — I want a crop top that says "Adam Smith's Best Friend."

I support that. I'm a big fan of the Adam Smith swag from the last celebrations. And this is 2023 — I walked out of there with, like, a handful of the lapel pins. I have those on a bunch of my blazers. There's some good pens. There's a bespoke Adam Smith tartan scarf that I have.

Wow.

Yeah.

That sounds great.

我们究竟在向斯密"要"什么？

Well, and so it's interesting. So, let me ask you then just another question about this, about how we use and engage with history when we're also thinking about our own time.

And so, I think to some degree you concluded the book with some reference — I'm not weird to quote you to you, but yeah, the final line, I feel like, is a little bit of a Quentin Skinner reference. If we can answer these questions — so you say, "What are our conditions of possibility that are drawing us to Smith now? What are we asking Smith to do for our political, moral, and economic thinking in the 21st century? If we can answer these questions and do our thinking for ourselves, perhaps we will be worthy of learning from Adam Smith after all."

And so I was wondering if you were thinking of Skinner, and if you were thinking about, you know, what does it mean for us to do our thinking for ourselves? I would say that, like, when I think about what we're doing in these commemorations — but also what we're doing when we engage with Smith — is he's like a really intelligent interlocutor. He's someone through which I can develop my own judgments, but that doesn't mean I don't think — I want to be wary of the sort of idea of using him to legitimate my own views. I don't think that's — I think that's dangerous. But there is — but I also think there's something to be said for being candid about our own kind of political motivations to some degree too.

Yes. Yeah. And I also want to reiterate, like, I don't think scholars are just going to Smith to legitimate their own views. That's not usually the reason why people are turning to Smith. There are a whole other reasons, and I think one of them is, we have questions that we're interested in and we want to see what Smith's answers to those questions are. Whether it's about state capture or state capacity or inequality or the relationship between ethics and economics. These are questions that we're asking, and in some cases they might have been questions that Smith was asking.

But I err on the side of — and again, I'm kind of bracketing myself off here as maybe, I don't know if "Skinnerian" is the right word, but — I tend to err on the side of: our questions might not be the questions that Smith was asking and might not be the reasons he was writing. So we have to be really careful when we ask Smith to give us answers to things that he wasn't necessarily providing answers for.

That's, I think, the intention of that last paragraph there. That we often reach for Smith because we think that he might offer some guidance, you know, sometimes in a very scholarly, robust way, but in ways that help us understand ourselves better rather than understanding Smith better. Right? And those are two very different things. We might turn to Smith because we want to understand Smith better in Smith's time, or we're turning to Smith because we think he might have something to offer us for now. And there's gray area in between. But I really wanted to differentiate those two things, because I think they could be very easily confused, because we often aren't totally aware of those differences.

I mean, especially as we were talking on the state capture stuff — like, I, for me, that is one of the primary concerns that I have about our contemporary moments. And I'm like, ah, I just see state capture everywhere. I see the conspiracy of the merchants everywhere. So am I probably letting that color my reading of Smith? 100%. But is, you know, at least I'm transparent about it slightly better? I don't know. But I think I just want readers — especially the kind of newer readers to Smith — to be aware that there is a difference between reading Smith to understand what Smith had to say in his time, versus reading Smith because we think that he has answers for our time.

与正典对话：选择性过滤与学科塑形

Yes. Yeah. And I think I do think that when one is doing — at least for me, I can tell I know when I'm doing a project that is much more historical, a little bit more, what we say, Cambridge School, a little bit more Skinner-influenced, and more about trying to really think in context, versus the work that I'm doing — and I guess for me, I will, I mean, I put it in terms of a little bit of intellectual history versus political theory. And I'm not saying that the Cambridge School is not political theory, but I just mean for my own work, I'm approaching it with different goals.

But either way, always having to be aware of, like, where do I begin? And where does the text end? And I think part of working with a thinker like Smith, working with canonical thinkers especially, these kind of big figures, is that you have this huge legacy and reception history. And so to some degree, whenever you write a work or make an argument about a thinker, to some degree you're arguing with the readers of the thinker who are doing the same project that you're doing, more than you are in a vacuum just trying to take care of the text, as it were, and like, kind of hermetically seal it from further contention and interpretation.

Yeah. I want to read this quote. This is one of my favorite quotes, and it didn't make it into the book, but I've used it in subsequent lectures. And this is from Warren J. Samuels, who was like an amazing economist and historian of economic thought. And he has this quote in a 1977 paper called "The Political Economy of Adam Smith." And it gets exactly to that point, right, that one of the difficulties with working with canonical thinkers like Smith is that, consciously or not — usually unconsciously — our mode of interpretation is the product of many decades, if not centuries, of previous interpretations, and responding to those interpretations.

And he says:

"The interpretation of Smith has been influenced by a selective filtration process which has permitted certain views to remain viable and others not. A process deeply channeled by ideology, power, and whatever governs professional or disciplinary concerns, including the felt needs between orthodoxy and heterodoxy economics, each of which has had its own dogmas and preconceptions about the past and present. Ideological and other filtration has permitted most economists and non-economists to have only an aphoristic appreciation of Smith's view of the economic world."

That was in 1977. He's just so prescient in observing how the ways in which we go about interpreting, even if we think we're doing the kind of best revisionist work possible, is nevertheless kind of part of this selective filtration process. And he doesn't necessarily mean that pejoratively. It's not just polemical or intellectual legitimation. But, like, he says the felt needs of disciplinary orthodoxy — how does your discipline go about reading Smith, rationally reconstructing his views, responding to different interpretations? That looks very different in economics than it does in political theory or in history.

So I love that quote. That we have to think of our own approach — like, we have to historicize our own approach.

Yes, yeah. No, that's actually — that's a really great way to put it. And an excellent quote too, and I think really gets at that thoughtfulness.

新重商主义？把斯密放回他的时代

The thing that I wanted to kind of — I did want, if you're willing to go along with me — in terms of, people are talking about kind of mercantilism, neo-mercantilism, and this sort of — so what would be your kind of, like, do you have any advice for people who might turn to Smith in that? And it doesn't mean that you have to give your interpretation, but it's like, what are the challenges of doing that in our time? And, you know, as somebody who is attracted to, like, I see the state capture everywhere — and you and me both — I mean, I — they have more passages, I would just — Jacob would have to, like, shut us down. You'd have to leave the room and I would just be reading still. So…

Yeah. Yeah. So this is going to sound kind of weird, maybe, but I would say: read Smith, and then read other people besides Smith too. I think that kind of over-reliance — and again, that impulse to be like, "Oh, what's going on now? Is it state capture? Okay, let's read Smith as a theorist of state capture." And it's like, okay, well then, it's like a Rorschach test. You're going to get that view of Smith happens to align with my view, like, take it or leave it.

But I also think — I mean, there's so much literature on this — like neo-mercantilism, gosh, there's so many other names — like a kind of neo-patronage system, patrimonial system. There's so many ways in which you can slice and dice the space. You know, like, I don't know, Marx has a lot to say about the moment too. And I don't mean to say that as a kind of political statement by any means, like, "oh, if you think you like Smith, you should probably read more Marx." I just think that's a generally good intellectual thing to do anyway. But just being extremely aware of the limits of saying there's this thing that's happening out in the world, Smith must have a category or an accurate diagnosis for this — be wary of that impulse.

Read Smith carefully, and see what he brings to light, and then ask: okay, well, what was Smith actually responding to in his time? In what ways was the British East India Company similar to or different from whatever it is that you're seeing out in the world right now? In what ways are networks of global commerce similar or different to the things that we're seeing now?

三大根本差异：民主、工业、资本

And, you know, the other thing that I would tell listeners and readers who kind of share that impulse — again, I'm like, yeah, I'm on team state capture with you — but at the same time, right, Smith — there is no mass democracy in Great Britain at this point. That is the other key difference. And it is also not industrial capitalism. Like, if you want to take little-c capitalism as, like, rule in favor of the capital class, like, sure, you can say that there was a version of capitalism. I think plenty of people would probably say it's, like, "oh, it's like a crony capitalism." He calls it the commercial system of Great Britain.

But it's clear that what he means is that this commercial system, this political order, is oriented in a way that disproportionately favors people of the capital class — people that have capital and live off of capital. Okay. In what ways is that different or similar to capital today? Well, we have financial capital. Like, it is a very, very different historical form that capitalism takes. And so, again, I completely sympathize with that initial impulse, and at the same time, all the little alarm bells should tell you, like, okay, but what else was different? What else is going on in our time or in Smith's time that we should be attuned to?

It has to do with the prevalence of mass democracy or not, the nature of capital, the organization of capital, and the extent of the organization of democratic politics. Those are the things that kind of go off in my head.

Yeah, no, 100%. And I think that's crucial too, because when you look at our moment too, there is plenty of, you know, need to — I mean, I would say I am a fervent believer of democracy, and so —

Good, good.

Yeah, cleared that bar. But, you know, Smith isn't going to be the best resource for that. As you said, that's not really the world that he was living in. And so I'm going to need to have other — if I'm thinking about all this — like, political theory as a conversation, you're going to need other interlocutors, both old and new. And someone can be welcome to be a part of the conversation, but doesn't have to end there. So that's essential too.

I do think that there is — what I find exciting about it though, is I do think there's a — because this is always an ongoing conversation — because he's a thinker with such a big legacy — is that the new things that we're facing may also shape the way we can better appreciate aspects of his arguments that maybe we didn't appreciate in the past, or that weren't front and center for people.

Yeah, yeah, for sure. And again, that's part of the view that informs my approach to reception history. That there are certain things that are making up our mental and moral and intellectual and political worlds that are shaping what we bring to a text that we're reading, whether it's Smith or Machiavelli or Hobbes or whatever, Mary Wollstonecraft, right? That the demands that we bring now are possibly very different from a reader in 1855. And so we have to reconstruct what life was like in 1855 and understand everything from, "Oh well, guess what? American democracy looked a little different back then, and so did American higher education, and the economy was very different." I mean, to speak of a national political economy would have been very strange in 1855. And so people are going to have a very different view of Smith and how they're reading him and how they're teaching him, and that informs what they get out of the text and why they're reaching for Smith.

Well, this has been an excellent conversation, Glory. Thank you so much for taking the time to chat. And I could talk to you for hours, so this was fantastic.

I'll just say, like, thanks again for having me on the show. It was a really fun conversation.

Great. Awesome. Of course. Anytime.

主持人结语：纪念、对话与不孤独

I'm so grateful that Glory took the time to chat with us. There are a few points I just wanted to reiterate and expand on as we wrap this episode up.

I loved how Glory discussed what it means to think deeply about the historical reception of the work of someone like Adam Smith. On the one hand, it is important to try to understand Smith on his own terms — understanding the context in which he was writing, and how that shaped the questions that he was asking, the methods he used, and what he could have possibly been arguing. At the same time, there are many Smiths that we can see through the interpretations of his readers throughout history. Even the American Founding Fathers drew on Smith for their own arguments in distinct ways. And members of the Chicago School of Economics interpreted Smith in a way that's different than how Glory or I do.

But the point is not to say that any of these interpretations are better or worse. Instead, it's worthwhile to be cognizant of the circumstance in which we find ourselves and how they shape what stands out to us about the text, and even motivate us to turn to Smith in the first place, just like all those who came before us. One way that my Georgetown professor Bruce Douglass put it is that we turn to these old texts again and again, just in the same way that we benefit from returning to a favorite song over the years. I know there are songs that I loved in high school, but it wasn't really until I was older and had different life experiences that I could feel and understand and appreciate the song in a new way.

国家俘获的当代回响

We also discussed Smith's insights that might be relevant for today's conversations about state capture. So Smith was discussing a very different political economic system. But within Smith's context, merchants had their own distinct interests via their profit-seeking practices, where they could then attempt to shape state policies for their own benefit, which would end up advantaging themselves more than the public, including their poorer workers. Further, their business interests incentivized them to understand how they might maximize their interests, whereas politicians were not as incentivized to understand the actual economic theories that those who were lobbying them for tariffs, subsidies, other policies were perpetuating. Instead, they would benefit from just being associated with these wealthy, socially powerful individuals and seek to get their approval, as well as even avoid severe dangers.

Smith wasn't really all about vilifying either the merchants or the politicians at the level of their individual moral character. It is more about how the political economic structure encouraged certain behaviors, enabled people to develop knowledge and expertise and rhetoric and strategies that could advantage themselves. And in the end, the public lost out.

不把斯密当权威：诚实的研究伦理

I appreciate Glory's generosity in being clear that to question and critique interpretations of Smith's isn't to say that these interpreters were just using Smith to legitimate their own thoughts, or polemically exploiting his arguments for their own political actions. I certainly wouldn't say that the people she studied in her book were doing that. But it is something I think a lot about when I do my own work. Am I making an argument from a genuinely restrained, scholarly, and inquisitive perspective? Or am I attempting to borrow the authority of someone like Smith for my own views?

It's really important to me to avoid the latter. And I think I'm not alone in that. I think the vast majority of people working on these thinkers do the same. But I do think it's important to keep in mind this risk of weaponizing a thinker as an appeal to authority.

一位学生在办公时间的困惑

I think there's a lot of value in returning to these texts. Like I said, it's like returning to a favorite song and seeing how its meaning changes over time. But I think a lot about this moment I had when I was a teaching assistant in the early years of my PhD. One of my undergraduate students came to office hours because she was really struggling with a thinker. In this case, it was Rousseau, but it could have been Smith. It could have been anyone. Specifically, she was confused because she was somehow under the impression that she was supposed to defer to his authority. She was worried that she was meant to just take these lessons from his text as the right way to think about politics, including today.

I was taken aback, but so grateful — because this is exactly why you hope your students come to office hours. You want to make sure you can clarify these types of misunderstandings, no matter how much you tried to avoid them. So I know the professor and I worked to make sure that these texts were not taken as ready-made answers to approach politics.

As I told her, it's always about facilitating conversations in the classroom and outside the classroom and across time and place. In fact, I also think we empower students by introducing them to these primary sources, allowing them to develop their own interpretations, such that when someone else does make an appeal to authority to a thinker, they have the power to question and offer an alternative understanding, as well as their own perspective.

颂扬不同意见：作为方法的政治理论

Ideally, our classes create a space where students step outside of the world that they've taken for granted and look at it from different perspectives — think about all these other political and economic and social possibilities, some better and some worse, and to see that these texts are often in tension and contradiction and disagreement with each other. And indeed, that the value of this disagreement is in allowing us to look at things differently. It really is about championing disagreement, productive disagreement, as opposed to difference. And I think that's really important to model as an instructor and to create that conversational process, both again between students with the instructor, but also just the act of doing political theory, of reading primary texts.

结尾：读古人，让人不再孤独

At the same time, why deny that there are times when we come to a text and are struck by how much it resonates with us? That despite the differences between yourself and the thinker, they have these arguments that bring to the fore the same political, economic, social, personal, and existential questions or concerns that eat at us. I know it's strange, but I have to admit that reading the words of a long-dead 18th-century Scottish philosopher, economist, and I would say political theorist, makes me feel less alone.

But commemoration should not — and cannot — be the same thing as blind idolatry, especially for anyone who truly treasures freedom.

Thank you so much to Glory for having this excellent conversation. Thank you so much to all of you for listening. Till next time.

Heat. Heat.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最意外的观察：斯密的"看不见的手"其实是一种"政治反讽"，而非市场颂歌
最颠覆直觉的事实是——"看不见的手"出现在《国富论》第四卷批判**重商主义**的章节里，而不是出现在歌颂市场的章节。换句话说，斯密这只"手"原本是写给**被国家俘获的政治经济体系**的解药，而非一句赞美自由市场的口号。Glory Liu 的精彩之处在于点出：她在课堂上几乎"懒得讲它"——因为这个短语的伟大不在文本本身，而在 250 年里被层层涂抹的反射光。
这里藏着一个更深的悖论：当代许多自称"斯密派"的人最反对"系统设计者"（the man of system），却在不自觉中**把"看不见的手"本身变成了一种新的 system thinking**。他们用斯密反对的方式来捍卫斯密。这是一种近乎佛家式的"以指为月"——指头被供奉，月亮却被遗忘。
## 二、跨领域连接：斯密的"商人–议员合谋"模型 ≈ 信息论中的"非对称信道" ≈ 互联网时代的算法俘获
斯密在 4.1.10 段的句子值得反复咀嚼："商人完全知道这政策如何使他们自己致富，但如何使国家致富并不是他们的本职。" 这句话如果用今天的语言重写

item.title: [Lex Clips] Why antimatter costs $63 trillion dollars to produce | Don Lincoln and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

反物质的真正瓶颈：不是物理，是"能量密度的几何学"

大多数人讨论反物质时，纠结于"产量太少"或"成本太高"。但 Don Lincoln 在这段对话里其实埋了一个更深的洞见——反物质的稀缺，本质上是一个"空间问题"，而不是"能量问题"。
