And we want to release it to the community for use for free because we think that there are probably other people doing also large-scale Lean operations, and these tools going to make their stuff go a lot more robust and faster and do so at scale.

AXEL 的功能

AXEL is currently I think 14 such tools, starting from verify proof, which is to make sure that there's nothing weird going on — no sort of cheating by Lean code, you don't aim something out, you don't assume weird things. If you ask N plus N equals N, you can prove 2 plus 2 equals 2, which you're for sure not — that's not the right answer.

There are also other generation tools. For example, you can try different repair attempts: broken Lean in and good Lean out. And there are currently other repair methods by LLM. So hopefully this what we provide can be just a lot cheaper and more straightforward. And it's just strong and better engineering can get you to a place that's quite far.

A lot of the people from the Lean community has been using AXEL, even if it's just been a week, to do all sorts of different interesting things. We have seen people from the blockchain community use it as actor, and we have heard from a lot of the people that "Claude plus AXEL" is kind of their go-to setup for now.

We think that these are really interesting tools. I think famously today there's this mathematician who said he formalized the Donald News using Claude to prove a Ramsey result, and then to formalize the Lean proof — and that is also using AXEL tool. So we are really glad to see people kind of already using it.

数学协作的未来与 blueprint

Brandon: I feel like this is a great opportunity for the collaborations that Terence Tao was talking about as well, where once people have access to the common tools and it becomes easy to do — and if you have intuition, even not a strong mathematician like myself, you might be able to participate in an effort to prove a larger theorem or something like that.

Carina: Yeah, I think that's a very interesting view, which is that — if you think about, mathematics has been not as collaborative as software engineering. You don't have hundreds and thousands of people working on something together. I think Polymath was an instance when that happened. That was fantastic.

So if you have a lot of really good sort of setup, indeed commoditized access, and people can all participate — in fact, that's how I think some of the large formalization projects have been done. Things are divided into subtasks. But really the blueprint writing process by, say, Terence Tao and Alex Kontorovich — of assigning the task to different people and how things kind of fit together — that blueprint writing part is extremely important.

And there has been, I think, result about sphere packing, I think by one of the other companies out there. And the blueprint part for the 8 dimension is still pretty much built on what the sphere packing community, the Lean community, the humans, blueprint. And similar with some of their other results as well — the blueprint part has still been human-generated. And I think autogenerated blueprint is going to be a technical bottleneck that many people are trying to solve around the same time.

Brandon: So is there value in me as a Claude Code user trying to attempt some small lemma or whatever, where I don't have a great understanding of the math, maybe I have a high-level understanding?

Carina: Depends. Are you trying to formalize or are you trying to prove?

Brandon: Uh, to prove new things.

Carina: That's a good point. Yeah, so for formalization, you would obviously probably start with formalization, you know the proof and you just can't get — nobody has been able to get the formalization correct.

I have actually seen people use Lean in formalization, and they try to do it by hand, not using AI, as a way to learn mathematics. No, it's auto-formalization, you don't have that process.

It's interesting because I think a lot of my friends who started working on Lean and Mathlib was because they are in PhD and this problem's really hard. We get stuck all the time and we want to review some of the undergrad classes a time where we still understand what the math was about. And we do so by doing Lean. And I think that's very beautiful — the material.

But if you have access to Axiom Prover that also can formalize, you don't have — you lose that part of the learning process.

But I do think that for you and I we can set up AXEL and try to see what results we might be able to prove. And I think that's quite interesting. And thanks to AXEL sort of making the speed a lot faster, you don't have to wait very long.

Putnam 当天的实战

I remember the Putnam exam day. We were all in the war room. It was a Saturday. We're all really excited and we just got the exam paper from the official organization, the proctor of the Putnam exam. We just were looking at how much workout AXEL is getting. And without it we couldn't have solved with — I think the 8 problems within the time limit, definitely not within the time limit.

And I think one thing about these tools is, it's very interesting in that potentially you can have interesting reward for RL as well.

Brandon: What do you mean by that?

Carina: So for example, verify proof can be a reward for just basically a proof is completely correct and validated.

Brandon: I see.

Carina: I think formal verification tooling can be interesting direction to pursue with RL.

Brandon: So you mean, for example, you auto-formalize the informal proof and then verify what, and then use that as a reward?

Carina: No, as in like you pass Lean programs in these formal tools, and you will have some sort of score.

Brandon: Okay.

Carina: I think if I were to build O1 or something, I would have in my mind I would have used what I just described.

与前沿实验室合作的价值主张

Brandon: But you're saying just to learn how to do Lean.

Carina: So the value proposition which is interesting about frontier lab is: suppose you are a B2C business, then sure you can just not do what we are doing. And we have, since for example DeepSeek, originally having a formal team and then later dissolve that team because of strategic direction change — that's all completely reasonable.

Now suppose you are focused on coding, and you have talent who want to work on what we're doing — it makes a lot more sense for you to do code generation, further your strength and moat.

You can partner with Axiom. Just like how, for example, frontier labs partner with startups that work on search, such as Exa and Parallel — right, just call Exa API for search. And potentially, if you're frontier lab, I think you should call Axiom API for verification.

Better proposition. But spinning up your own — it doesn't make sense. The talent, the finickiness of Lean, the sort of data, code — there's no reason to.

Brandon: I mean, it took me five minutes to set up.

创业起点：法学院 + 数学痴迷

Brandon: Why did you decide to start? You were a grad student at Stanford in math. So what made you decide to —

Carina: I wasn't in math for a while at all. Almost as soon as I started the PhD, I just started fundraising. So it wasn't like —

Brandon: Oh really?

Carina: Yeah.

Brandon: Was that the plan, or did you start there and you almost immediately realized that this is —

Carina: Right. The year of law school was very interesting to me on an intellectual level. But it's also the first year where I had no science, technology, math whatsoever in my life. It's a weird year. I'm reading a lot. I'm practicing — well, I'm learning how to write, I'm learning how to read.

But I'm just kind of — I want to be obsessed about something in technology. That was also what's going on that year. The year of law school. And it was very interesting to me because — okay, I just need to be obsessed with a technical thing because otherwise I get — I don't think I'm bored because I really love everything about law. I really really loved it. It was something that's incredibly interesting to study.

But I've been very excited about the progress of reasoning. I was looking at a lot of the post-training papers. I was learning all of these by myself. And then at one point it got to a point where I'm like, I think this is for sure happening. And like, I think talking to Shubo at Verve every weekend also didn't help soothing this thought.

So I got more and more obsessed. And at a point I'm like — okay, if I'm doing this literally every minute, and I can't think about something else, I need to do something about it. I mean, it's like, I fall madly in love with the idea that AI is going to do math. And like, okay, now do I do math? It's really really crazy. At a time, the obsession was quite — I just couldn't get out of it.

And then I went to this Ni-Hennessy event — Scholar Dining House like host all sorts of free lunch events. And those are great because you get free food and you get interesting intellectual exposure to things. And I remember Julie Drow, who was I think a Facebook, first Facebook PM, came to speak. And then after that I just basically walked up to her and I said, "what do you do if you want to do a startup and you really wanted to do academia because you love math?"

And then she's like, "well, what's your time spent on these two different things?" And I'm like, "100% / 0%." And then she's like, "well, you kind of have to follow your energy."

I mean, if you are completely obsessed with it. I was completely obsessed with it. But I thought it's going to be big. And I thought it just has to be a for-profit startup because it's so much broader than making mathematical breakthroughs.

If you think about recursive self-improvement and like really the more high-level concept of you really want to have this AI AI scientist — the math reasoner is going to be a pretty big part of it. And now trying — I think the sort of belief by Cursor and Claude and other folks is, okay, like just like math transfer to coding, coding transfer to math as well. I think that's true. It's just that, why not push it directly? I don't get it. You need to push that directly.

三个核心论点：关于 verified AI

And then there's this other thought which is — and maybe kind of going back to the collaboration point — verification has traditionally been thought of as: okay, well, there are some industry where there's a lot of guardrails. So if you're working in defense, military use, okay, you need to basically satisfy a lot of barriers to entry to meet those stringent requirements. So verification is for the industries that are closed.

第一点：开放协作

But it's for the first time now I think verified AI is to open up collaboration. Either it's human-AI collaboration, well before — blueprinting, that's human-human collaboration. And Lean was a grounding, was a verification formal language. And then human-AI collaboration, like we're seeing now, future AI agent-agent-agent like collaboration.

So I think verified AI is for openness. It's not for meeting the requirements of closed industries.

第二点：放大才华，而非消除错误

And I think — just like I think verification should not be about: oh, I remember there's article like "chatbots mixed stuff up, is AI the solution" — sorry, "is math solution to hallucination."

Verification to me is not about lousiness. Verification to me is about scaling brilliance, compounding brilliance. It's like, just kind of going back to the collaboration point. It's about Ramanujan being a much stronger mathematician. He was already a really strong one, but verification helps him extend the brilliance, like both kind of scale up and scale out.

So verification — rigorous verification to me is not about erasing the mistakes, the lousiness. About scaling brilliance.

第三点：性能增益，而非合规负担

And the third point is that verification to me is not about just talking about rigor, it's actually about performance gain. It's not just about the stringent requirements, the hurdles that you need to overcome. It is about, like, actual verified generation is going to make it so much better.

And I think these three points — I think the last point is that a lot of the people think that you work on verification because of your distrust for technology. It sells really well to, I think, the general public, including my parents. Like, "oh, why are we doing verification? Because technology make mistakes." No, we don't think verification is because of the distrust for technology. It's because that's what expected rapid exponential scale up and the deployment and the creation of technology and technological progress is what that compels and demands.

数学如何驱动其他科学

Brandon: It's a very mathematical perspective, right? Because you're saying proofs drive math. A lot of math is about proofs. And math drives a lot of science and innovation in the world. And the innovations in math drive innovation in the world.

Carina: But it doesn't need to even go through. Like, in terms of, the "solve everything," like obviously stands. My point is, transfer learning is about pushing math reasoning.

So there are a couple narratives here. For some people it's like you solve math and then maths are the fundamentals of sciences, so that's actually the — from AI for math like take this radical layer of AI for science. That is narrative we actually believe in. Just like general transfer learning. I think Axiom is on the infrastructure stack.

Brandon: And you think that this is just a first step to basically unlocking capabilities in many domains in science and law, for example.

Carina: Yes. So again, there are multiple beliefs. One belief is that there's math and there is the formal verification. Suppose we actually solve math and have a really strong informal math reasoning engine. We do not expect that TAM to be as large as solving math through the formal way.

Brandon: Why?

Carina: I mean, code is language, but it is indeed on the more structured end. It bridges informal and formal.

What we are doing is — it's not informal versus formal. We're not taking the sort of completely formal of a proof approach. It's bridging between informal and formal. It is bridging between high level and low level. It is a direct sort of direct improvement through reasoning, through transfer learning. And it's also indirect in that, okay, well, math is going to unlock all science. And that is really what we're seeing.

Brandon: So you think that it enables transfer learning.

Carina: Yeah. I think that is pretty much a consensus.

I think it is a consensus, and this is a bet that has been pretty much overlooked by others because math sounds pure and it doesn't sound like there's any commercial value. Well, I do obviously understand the opportunity cost if you're like a frontier lab of solving this problem. But I definitely think this is a problem that if you're a well-resourced startup, you should be doing.

Brandon: That's an interesting perspective. Did you get everything out that you wanted?

Carina: Yeah, I think the question of "is math, or is verification?" — the DNA of the company is math. We think verification is the best first market.

And we think that sort of solving math, and especially formal math, is going to help us tackle the really ambitious quest of verified AI. Now when we are done with that, we might have other second markets including AI for science we just talked about. But on the theoretical layer — I think real-world testing is important, and potentially we can stay in the digital world and software stuff, and for other things to be getting reward, like physical world signals.

Brandon: But do you think that the capability of doing really powerful reasoning — once you have that powerful verified reasoning engine, that's the moment when, okay, now we've unlocked that for software verification and hardware, or whatever — but okay, so now what about biology, what about chemistry?

Carina: So that could be one. Then the other one is then like really, how far are you to recursive self-improvement?

Brandon: Okay, so just AGI.

Carina: Yeah, I think there is this sort of question. And different people because of their probably different backgrounds have different — it's really where your energy and your passion leads you. For some people — actually I've heard this with my friends — they want to work on AGI because they believe solve AGI, solve death. There are other people who come from a more medicine background, they really believe they can solve death and they don't solve AGI and then solve death. They just solve AI for science.

Now, which way is correct? I don't know.

Brandon: And so the recursive self-improvement angle — it sounds to me like you're saying that the combination of verification plus the sort of language which is informal, it's that combination that enables really good recursive [self-improvement].

Carina: I think recursive self-improvement is going to happen anyway. We're trying to have formal verification's earnest place. So we like — again, whether formal verification can be welcomed and deployed and become a consensus depends on how well we execute. And I think when you boil down that problem into an execution problem, you should just go for it.

最大瓶颈：碎片化

Brandon: Looking forward, what's the biggest bottleneck that you see in the field for both Axiom and maybe just the field abroad?

Carina: Fragmentation. So I think we're in a market where people like to start a thousand people — they don't join force, they start a thousand things. I think that's actually the biggest bubble indicator.

I think there are categorical bubble, and there are other categories where there are moonshots — it's not bubble, it just looks a little bubbly in the field. If people who are like really legit backgrounds decides to join force and work in a team for the mission rather than for ego, for kind of the status of new lab founder, I think those categories I'm really bullish on. And vice versa.

So I think the bottleneck actually is — it's annoying because, if you believe we are in an age of research, if you believe deep tech are the interesting directions to go after, the market sort of conditions currently is good and bad. Good in that it enables these sort of long-term, long-horizon bets to be funded. Bad because there's too much noise in the market and some other irrational players.

We try to work with really incredible venture firms. They are our partners. They are our intellectual partners, and there's a lot of alignment, and we really bounce very cool ideas, technical and non-technical, each other for long long hours. And we spend a lot of time off work and weekends together to really intensely build the company.

There are also other people who just want to park capital somewhere. And while we don't work with them, these are market conditions that encourage fragmentation. And when things get fragmented, no one gets there.

I think every category, regardless of how right the idea is, it's pretty much in a sort of "earning the right to exist" stage. And if that is the case, then — for example, great deep tech company SpaceX — people do actually join force to work on that dream, and potentially in that case also a very charismatic founder.

I think a really concerning thing for me personally is that for other probably some categories that I'm personally quite bullish about — just looking at things generally, fragmentation is a problem. We see stop pulling professors from university to work on something when it really is a really interesting situation.

Brandon: Maybe this is a naive question, but right now when you were talking about players in let's say AI for math, where Harmonic, and then the big labs — am I missing someone? Is that actually fragmented really?

Carina: I guess fragmentation, I think, is a bottleneck for the entire AI landscape. I think AI for math is a category that is actually not a bubble because it is not fragmented because people who are really amazing talents do like to join force.

So for example, the fact to get Kanu and Francois Chollet on one team — like this is fantastic. You have someone who's a core contributor, Frontier Math tier-4 really great benchmark setter, Francois who's on the AI for math discovery, have proving and discovery, they work together — then you are suddenly a player with both proving capability and construction capability. And that's fantastic.

And I believe, as you said, Harmonic probably also have some really great talents joining force together. I think AI for math is a good category because of the absence of fragmentation.

But even from our perspective, the sort of — for example, RL right? Being — I don't think that's like a category per se — but RL talents currently, it's quite hard to attract and retain for literally everyone. And there are a lot of companies being started then sold like three months later. And just each month where you could have worked on a technical problem and you're instead working on deals — it's a month that is wasted. And I say that with some amount of pain and suffering because of having gone through two fundraises.

AI for Math 的长期瓶颈：短期 vs 长期

Brandon: So what's the biggest bottleneck in AI for math — not Axiom, but just the community? Where is it going? What is the thing that everyone just really wants to break?

Carina: I expect fragmentation to start to happen as Axiom and Harmonic establish category leadership.

So I expect people kind of, you know, that's one thing. But I also think that another bottleneck could be the pressure of short-term versus long-term.

I think that we are doing things in a very sort of fast-paced manner. But that does not mean we can always — or it does not mean it is always correct to do things in the most fast-paced manner. We did things in a fast-paced manner because, well, we were founded on the day of International Math Olympiad, so we couldn't have competed in that anyway.

The next math olympiad is Putnam, and we're quite excited because — it's undergraduate exam, and this year's IMO25 IMO was easy on the MOHS scale, and Putnam could be hard. And in fact, it was harder than the IMO on the MOHS scale if you look at — the AI, how many scores the AI has retained on average and on the max difficulty of the problem. Putnam is harder in both axis.

So we want to try. And so there's only a gap of four months. But it doesn't mean I'm always going to set four-month goals. If I build a company only setting four-month goals, I might build a really short-sighted company.

So there are longer-horizon problems. I think for example market forces could force other players into chip verification. Well, it is possible that code verification is a holy grail. It's possible that if you solve that, then you also naturally solve chip verification with some amount of, like, epsilon caveat of distribution shift. But I strongly believe that a bottleneck could be the pressure.

But I think that Axiom is fortunate that when we are early enough, we are like a team of just incredibly high-agency people that our execution generally surpasses expectation. But I think what I think could be a bottleneck for the entire AI for math field is that potentially trying to prove commercial value is going to distract significantly from the core capability improvement.

结尾

Brandon: Yeah, that makes sense. Cool. Thank you for driving up and coming to see us. I know the traffic was horrible.

Carina: Yeah, thank you.

Brandon: It's been really a pleasure speaking with you, and we look forward to seeing how things develop.

Carina: Yeah, thank you so much. Thank you.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的洞察：验证不是"刹车"，而是"复利引擎"
整段对话最颠覆常识的一点，是 Karina 把"形式化验证"从一个**防御性概念**（防止幻觉、合规、安全）彻底翻转为**进攻性概念**——她用了 Ramanujan 这个例子，简直是神来之笔。
人们习惯把 Ramanujan 想成"靠直觉的天才"，把 Hardy 想成"逼他写证明的导师"。但 Karina 指出：**正是写证明这件"看起来限制创造力"的事情，让 Ramanujan 的直觉真正可以被后世数学家"复利累积"**——他的洞察从此可被引用、被推广、被嵌入更大的体系。
这指向一个反直觉真相：**形式化不是约束智能的天花板，而是让智能可以"被传递、被叠加"的接口标准**。没有协议的智能是孤岛，有协议的智能才能形成网络效应。
## 二、跨领域连接：Anthropic 的"编码赌注" ↔ Axiom 的"数学赌注" ↔ TCP/IP 的胜利
Karina 在不经意间做了一个极其精妙的类比：**2024 年所有人都低估了 Anthropic 押注 coding，认为那只是一个 vertical**。但 coding

item.title: [Lex Clips] Can antimatter be used as rocket fuel? | Don Lincoln and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

反物质经济学：一个被忽视的"能量浓度"哲学

最让我意外的洞察并不在反物质本身，而在 Don Lincoln 那句几乎被一笔带过的话——"瓶颈不是反物质，而是能量浓度"。这句话其实悄悄重写了整个讨论的坐标系：人类文明在能源问题上常常追问"我们有多少能量？"，但反物质把问题翻转成了"我们能把能量挤进多小的空间？" 1.5 千万亿美元的造价，本质上不是支付给"反物质"，而是支付给"把能量压缩到质子尺度"的代价。这是一种空间税，而不是能量税。

跨领域连接：反物质 ↔ 半导体光刻 ↔ 注意力经济

如果把"能量浓度"作为透镜，反物质制造与现代半导体光刻惊人地同构——两者都是在越来越小的体积里完成越来越精确的事件。EUV 光刻为了把图案压进 3 纳米，整个 ASML 机器的成本被推到 4 亿美元一台；粒子加速器为了把能量压进 10⁻¹⁵ 米，造价飙升到 NASA 报告里的天文数字。摩尔定律的尽头和反物质的造价曲线，本质上是同一条曲线：人类每往"更小"前进一步，价格按指数律惩罚你。

更出人意料的跨域连接是：这种"浓度税"也出现在注意力经济里。要把一个观念塞进现代人 8 秒的注意力窗口，营销成本指数级上升——这与把能量塞进质子体积的代价结构惊人相似。"小"在物理、芯片、与认知里，都是最昂贵的奢侈品。

被遗漏的不对称性：反物质的"无声毁灭"

大多数关于反物质的讨论聚焦"它有多强"，但 Lincoln 漏掉了真正诡异的一点，而我想把它挑出来——反物质事故的失败模式没有预警信号。核反应堆有温度上升、辐射读数、压力曲线；化学燃料有泄漏气味、火焰、烟雾。但反物质一旦失去约束，湮灭过程在纳秒尺度完成，没有"正在出问题"的中间状态。

这让反物质成为人类工程史上第一种"二元态"危险源：要么完美，要么彻底消失。与之相比，切尔诺贝利至少给了操作员几十秒的预警。这种"无中间态"的特性，其实和加密货币私钥丢失、量子退相干、雪崩故障属于同一类系统——没有渐变的恢复曲线，只有悬崖。

一个反直觉的结论

Voyager 探测器用着 1977 年的钚电池，至今还在工作。这暴露了一个被星际旅行幻想掩盖的真相：在深空中，缓慢可靠 > 强大易爆。反物质推进听起来浪漫，但 Voyager 用最"原始"的核衰变跑了半个世纪——这暗示人类对"高能量密度即先进"的崇拜可能是一种文化偏见。也许下一个真正的航天突破不在更强的燃料，而在更耐心的能量释放曲线——这与你做内容、做投资、做长期项目的逻辑，是同一件事。

📝 转录文本

NASA 的反物质成本估算

This is a NASA estimate of how much it cost to produce antimatter. So, looking at all the the cost of the accelerator, all everything combined together to do enough for a 1 megaton antimatter bomb, which I should think it would be even possible on the order of 25 g like we mentioned, will cost about uh based on a NASA estimate uh 1.5 quadrillion dollars.

By the way, uh NASA wasn't talking about a bomb. It's just me adding. NASA was talking about the estimate the cost of 62 to 63 trillion dollars per gram of antihydrogen. Actually, is what they're referring to.

[snorts]

与核武器的成本对比

Uh so, compared I was looking at estimates, the current best estimate of how much it takes to produce a 1 megaton nuclear warhead. Everything combined is about 10 to 50 million dollars in the United States. So, you're talking about difference in terms of a weapon with the equal power. 50 million dollars versus 1.5 quadrillion dollars.

从武器到推进系统

To me, what's interesting weapons is just one uh indication of this. One other possibility, and NASA also writes about this, is the use of antimatter in propulsion systems. Right. Uh just like you can use uh nuclear fission and maybe even nuclear fusion down line in the propulsion systems.

I saw that 1 g can help get us to Alpha Centauri star system if we can get to 0.2 times the speed of light in 20 years. Uh meaning it would take us 20 years to get to Alpha Centauri. Is any of this a possible future? The use of antimatter for generation of energy. Because we should mention that it's extremely compact. It has the obvious downsides that it's extremely costly to produce. We don't know how to do that kind of scale. Right. The upside is it's compact.

"这不是物理问题，是工程问题"

So, the short answer is it is not a physics problem, it's an engineering problem. So, I have people for that.

[laughter]

Um okay, but no, no. Yeah. Um the truth is that antimatter, if you are able to uh assemble it and store it, sure, it would be able to take that antimatter, heat up matter, and shoot it out the back of a rocket, and it would you know, do what rockets do. It would make dust go quick, and that would be fine.

最大挑战：约束 (Containment)

I would say mention the thing that you just mentioned is is correct. One of the hugest challenges is the containment because antimatter when it comes in contact with matter uh is a is a problem. Right. So, if you were unable to uh to contain your trip to Alpha Centauri for even a millionth of a second, boom. And that would not be good.

Um you know, it reminds me of the uh the Star Trek where Scotty's saying, "Captain, you know, the antimatter pods are about to look We're losing containment. It's going to blow." And that's exactly what would happen.

So, the short answer is, yes, antimatter as in principle, we could make and use as a a source of energy, but there are probably far less expensive sources of energy. Um you know, it depends on what you need to do. The Voyager probes are still chugging along with plutonium now. They're running out of energy at this point, but we could, you know, presumably do a a somewhat better job if we needed to.

So, I I like the idea of antimatter, you know, but the reality is the danger, not the obvious danger of weapons, but the danger of if you wanted to be in a ship run by antimatter, if it ever got loose, well, you you would never know it.

[snorts]

That would be that.

反物质背后的物理探索价值

The reason I I I find this kind of inspiring. Is antimatter is in the space of physics that has a lot of mysteries. There's a lot of exploration to be done. And so, this kind of connection to energy means that uh if we have a bunch of breakthroughs on the antimatter side, that might lead to a better propulsion system, better energy generation systems. In principle. There's some combination of engineering here, but there's some combination of understanding the fundamental physics.

I mean, we know how to do this. You know, we we know you take energy, you make antimatter. You have to contain it, you have to store it, you have to do all the hard things. But I I would be shocked if there was some like new addition to the theory that made antimatter production easier.

真正的瓶颈：能量浓度

Interesting. So, we know how to produce antimatter with accelerators. You're saying there's not breakthroughs in physics that could lead to different mechanisms for the generation of antimatter. You have to concentrate energy.

Well, that's it. If there's another way to concentrate energy, that would work, too. And our best knowledge of how to concentrate energy is the accelerator. And remember, we're talking concentrating it into um volumes the size of a proton.

I mean, if you concentrate it to the size of your thumb, well, then, you know, it's really the density that matters, the local density. And so, when you smash two protons together, all of that's occurring in a tiny tiny volume. So, it's the local density of energy that matters. If you had a lot of energy in a thimble or something, uh it's you're probably not dense enough. You know, it really has to be in close proximity for that to happen. And then, when it does, it it's it's okay.

So, so if there's another way, we know how to do it to to make that that density thing with with accelerators, if someone has a bright idea on how to make highly dense energy, then yeah, making antimatter is a piece of cake. But that's the crux, concentrated energy.

Yeah, and how to do so in a cost-efficient manner, not trillions of dollars. Well yeah.

观看原视频 →
