十四、回归酒店业：与 Expedia 的圆环

The next leg of hooking this platform up to the entire world is hotels. So interesting that you've gone back to Expedia days. Full circle. Walk us through the the decision logic here. I have a question behind the question, which is like how you decide to do these big things in the first place. Daniel uh who we started the conversation with used to have this bets board at Spotify. There's all these different ways that capital allocators decide what to bet on. I'd like to talk about that strategy as well, but but starting with hotels, why do it in the first place? What was your thinking?

We tend to be pretty data-oriented. And and what what we've seen — and and it's relatively obvious — is that people who travel a lot tend to use Uber a lot, right? We're available in over 70 countries. What's the first thing that you do when you land at an airport often is you open up your Uber app. Just last year, we completed 1 and a half billion trips for people outside of their home city. About 15% of our trips are to and from airports.

So, we could identify travelers. It was a use case that was a headline use case. And we started thinking to ourselves, hey, what more could we do for the travelers? And and what differentiates Uber from some of the other places — again, it's not just about the booking experience but it's about delivering that experience in a reliable way in the real world.

We also we go much deeper into the fulfillment stack than let's say the typical online travel agent. We decided as you're able to target more etc. Let's look at other travel products. We started with trains. We launched it in the UK and Spain and we saw kind of the same thing that we've seen over and over again which is the more you interact with our platform, the more services you have, the more you interact with those services, the more you come back. So more content meant more retention.

And so we said like, well what content can we find in travel? It was very natural for us to go into hotels. Uh it's a high-value decision that you're making. Um, we went out there, talked to the various travel companies to make sure that we had the best supply in the business and we happened to make a deal with Expedia, my my old company, and we're essentially giving the vast majority of the economics of that deal back to Uber One members.

The design spec of hotels is again, how can I make sure that you have a — as a person — your life is more deeply integrated with our services? 10% off hotels, 20% off 10,000 hotels. That's a great deal that you're not going to find elsewhere and it's another reason for you to join Uber One.

端到端的旅行体验

What's the idealized end state of that part of your business like 5 years hence or 10 years hence?

For me, the idealized end state is obviously we want to bring a lot of value to you as it relates to the booking experience, but I want the value to carry into the market. You know, you book a hotel in San Francisco, we offer you uh — we go through your uh email if you open up for us — and we already collect kind of your information on your flight. We pre-book a uh an Uber to the airport. We pre-book an Uber from the airport to uh your hotel because we already know where you're staying.

And you know, ideally when you get to the hotel, we know you're approaching the hotel. Either we give the front desk a heads up that you're going to be there, or Patrick is going to be there and know you, or we allow you to completely bypass that — you know, the front desk, go to your room, maybe you can use your Uber app as a key.

The the magic — I want to bring some in-market magic to you as part of your travel experience. We're already part of your experience in city. The richer we can make that experience, the better. We're starting with a booking. We've got to start someplace. But that's the Uber magic I want to bring.

从"按需"到"预定"的品牌延伸

to the same premortem question, which was — if this doesn't work, like why would that be the case?

We have debated in the past whether or not people's interaction models with our service can change from on-demand to planned. People get very wedded — like our brand has always been known as on demand. You know, like push a button, get get a car, push a button, get your delivery, etc.

And so there was a question mark — could we go from on demand to planned? And we started moving in that direction with actually Uber Reserve. In the olden days, you know, Uber Reserve actually we allowed you to reserve a car but it was kind of a on-demand dispatch to try to get you that car when you expected it to be there.

We have changed the back end to really drive reliability 99 plus%. So we're pre-booking with the driver. The driver accepts. Make sure the driver is there before the reserve. And Reserve now is over $5 billion run rate. Um it didn't exist you know five six years ago.

And clearly we were able to um — if we gave a tangible benefit, in this case higher reliability for higher price — clearly we were able to move both demand and supply drivers won't kind of pre-commit to to your trip. So that gave us the opening to say, wow, this pre-commit thing is a is another angle that we can explore. And travel became uh natural for us.

So I think the question is, you know, is reserving your ride just fundamentally different from thinking about your vacation 2 or 3 months from now. Can we stretch that pre-commit and can we stretch a brand in that temporal direction? I think it's an open question. I think we can. We're giving you great deals to make that plan on Uber, but it's not it's not a slam dunk.

十五、市场营销：从产品本位到故事本位

What have you learned about marketing excellence? Like two sources of the question. One is just marketing Uber in general. Yes. Um but the second is as you do more and more stuff, making sure that even your customers know that it exists. Like I — I before preparing for this, I didn't know that this was a thing that existed. So, uh, it's an interesting question about how you do both the brand staying top of everyone's mind, but also the very tactical specific like we have this cool new thing now that you should try.

Totally. It's, you know, the surface that we have on this phone is very limited. And I've changed my mind on this.

Originally, I had the hypothesis which is the marketing team's job is to get as many people onto the apps as possible and then it's a product team's job to surface the right product to you at the right time. And and a small example of that is, you know, with Reserve now, we offer the ability for you to order hot coffee and the driver goes out and, you know, picks up the coffee wherever and then, you know, it's kind of cool. You have a cup of coffee if you're on the way to the airport.

The goal isn't to get you coffee on that ride. The goal is to make you understand that you can get coffee on Uber and/or other stuff, right? It's not about the ride. It's about the lifetime value, the interaction, the introduction of a new service, not in a way that feels like an upsell to you, but feels delightful. This is really cool. Uber knows me. Why not do do more with them?

All of the different iterations that that you can drive on the app — I have always been much more product. Let's build this from a product standpoint, and and the job of marketing is just to get people uh to to the app.

But my marketing team uh who told me I was an idiot and I loved it. You know, they are building out storylines. Uber Teens — getting your kid from their game home and the human interaction of when your kid comes home either if they did well in that uh in that game or not well in that game. There are very human stories that are part of Uber in every way. Our marketing team are telling the stories about a teens, about a reserve, about grocery delivery and the convenience then. And those stories are helping people realize that Uber isn't just about getting a ride. It's about giving you your time back across a broad array of services on a local basis.

App vs 自然语言：未来的交互形态

What do you think is more likely in seven years? That I do that whatever the next great version of that coffee thing is or that I just type into it into my Uber text like I want a car at this time and I want a coffee and

I think you'll be talking to your to to your apps in seven years.

I I think you're going to have apps because there are certain circumstances — many circumstances — where the communication back to you is much more efficient in the written word or in pictures. It's not compelling to say your Uber is six minutes away — seeing the picture and being able to track the car.

So I think the inbound interaction is going to move more and more away from apps. I think people are going to use apps but the inbound interaction is going to be much more unstructured and AI makes it possible. You historically built UIs for a specific interaction based on optimizing for the overall average. Right? Our booking experience on Uber may not be optimized to you exactly, but it's optimized to overall averages through lots of iteration, lots of testing. And there's only one way in.

Now, we're going to personalize it. So, when you land in a city, Uber is going to be different for you than when you're going to work. But the interaction that agents are going to make possible through AI and through words are going to be incredible.

十六、师承 Barry Diller：追求"源头真相"

And think about the people that have influenced you the most. What did you learn from Barry Diller?

He's incredible. He he's been my business mentor and in many ways a personal mentor as well. I'd say that the thing that I learned from Barry is um the value of getting the truth from the source material.

You know, the the way that he that I first met Barry, I was actually um a analyst at Allen and Company working on the LBO model for Paramount. There was a there was a hostile tender offer by Combine and Paramount. I was building out the LBO, right? Yeah. Exactly. You know, Barry didn't want to talk to the MD or the VP or the associate. He's like, "Who built the model? And I'm going to talk to that guy cuz if I'm going to raise billions of dollars in debt, I want to know I'm good for it."

And I just remember I was like sweating. I was so nervous. I'm like trying to print out this model. And and I took him through it. And and he wanted to hear straight from the source. And and every time I I've witnessed them — and I worked for him for 20-plus years — you know, it's the filtering that gets the edge out of the story or out of the situation. And it's often the edge that gives you an edge. It's not the the the average. Everyone is going to have the same reaction to the average. It's what's that 20%. What's what's that P95 situation, etc.

So Barry always insisted on going to the source, getting unfiltered data. It was sometimes unpleasant. It took more time, but that getting to the ground truth would help his decision-making uh and would allow him to give that med — that edge — to kind of go against the grain.

And I've taken that with me. You know, it it's I've seen in in in larger companies — like most people who run companies, they're smart, they're capable, they're driven. And the failure modes that I see with most companies is that, you know, the higher up your organization often, you get like a very thin layer of what's going on. Yeah. And and everything has been processed for you. Like my schedule today, it's determined by a bunch of well-meaning people who want to get me the best information and have me use my time in the best way, but it's processed.

And so, it's very important for you to cut through that process sometimes and get to that ground truth. You've got to create some randomness in your interactions and your information flow. And Barry often painfully kind of drove to that source.

And it's always for me been a very very important part of how I manage — which is, you know, the best way to to get to the truth is first of all as a leader tell the truth. Yeah. Uh and so I'm I'm brutally frank with my team. What's going on? What's a good? What's a bad? What's okay? Where do I not know the answers — and that helps me then get that truth back from them?

Sometimes requires me to dig and that can be unpleasant both for me and the team. But getting to the ground truth I think is is something that I learned from Barry and and it's always stuck with me.

十七、师承 Allens：押注于人

What did you learn from the Allens both the second and the third?

Oh. Uh-huh. So many. I'd say you know Herbert Allen very very early on he always told me that uh he makes bets on people not companies. And and you see that with Allens — like they're so loyal, they they build friendships and then they stay with those friendships.

And and Herbert always told me like, "Companies, companies can do well during periods or companies can do poorly during periods but great people always stay great. And I'm going to make a bet on people." And I'm like 20-some. I'm like, "Yes sir, Mr. Allen." And that has been, you know, it's a great learning that has kind of soaked into me — like 30 years too late — regardless of the circumstance.

Like I I made a bet on Barry, you know, I was going to stay at a company for all my life, but but there was like he was he was — he was the one guy that I told myself if I have an opportunity to work with that one person. And it was nuts. Like my salary was gone. It went through this like completely entrepreneurial new entity that that that he was building. But I bet on a person and in my life, you know, my bets on people and those personal bets are probably the best ones I've made.

十八、自我观察：透明、随机互动、保护"麻烦制造者"

Daniel A has this thing that he's famous for, which is that he would go shadow, I think he might still do it, go shadow CEOs of companies for long periods of time and watch how they operate and work. So, if I were to go ask the same question I'm asking you about Barry and the Allens, but ask the question about you, what do you think if I were to shadow you for a couple weeks, I would take away from like how you prosecute your life? Like what what do you think the ingredients that I could take away for my own recipe? What do you think I'd write?

I I think number one is the transparency that I talked about — like I want the truth from everybody. I tell the truth. I'm very very open to the company, whatever level that that I talk to. I have a lot of fun working with our product leads and engineers. Um that is the the best part of of my job.

And then the the last that I say is um you see a lot of random interactions — like it — I I don't want to have too structured a day. I don't want to meet with just my direct reports. I build out random interactions within the company so that I get that information uh as well.

It's like I look for the troublemakers in the company. As companies get larger, there's always this incentive for people to get along. You know, you have a single culture. Everyone, you know, is true to the culture of the company. There are processes that that get built. There are certain ways of interacting and certainly the troublemakers often get chased away.

I want to find those troublemakers and I want to bring them in because every company is like an organism, right? And organisms evolve by mutating. And companies that don't mutate, that just sit and with a single process, with a single information flow, those are the companies that die. So I'm looking for those mutations. I'm looking for those troublemakers constantly.

And that requires random interactions. They're less efficient than the structured processes that have been put in front of me. But it's those random interactions that often give me the best signal that you know — sometimes I may not act on it but I'll take it in and I'll at some point — if the signal becomes strong enough, I'll move.

That's a cool idea that the troublemakers are the mutations.

Companies need to change. And by the way, with AI now, the rate of change inside companies, in terms of how information flows, in terms of how we work every single day, the rate of change is has accelerated by 5x. And it's the companies that are comfortable with that change that are going to adapt to this new reality. And the companies that are like, hey, we do things a certain way, they're going to have a hard time.

十九、资本分配：成长 vs 回购的取舍

One of my favorite charts from this era of business has been the the time series of capex of the big technology companies where it all looks like this except for Apple that just looks like this. Like they basically haven't changed. They've decided not to play this specific game.

And the debate around Uber early on was like, well, these guys ever make any money. Like they're losing money on all these rides, blah blah blah. Now you've got $10 billion plus of free cash flow. So you're a capital allocator and you have to make a decision of what to do with this money in this high rate of change environment. How do you how do you prosecute that that decision? You've done big buybacks. You've authorized really big buybacks before. Yeah. Which traditionally have done really good things for shareholder value and things like this through business history, but now you have all these opportunities to spend money to expand demand, to get new supply, to use these new technologies. Like how do you weigh the trade-off between old school stuff like buybacks and free cash flow generation and share reduction and things like that with just like, wow, this is the most interesting investing. I guess I'm asking like are you Amazon or are you Apple?

Uh somewhere in between. And and I'd say you know capital allocation I think is more of an art versus a science. You can read papers on it but but I think you have to use your judgment in real life.

And my view is that my priority is first organic investment and growth. You know Uber Eats when I joined was uh doing under a billion dollars in gross bookings. That's over, you know, over 100 billion now. That's crazy. And that took an enormous amount of organic investment. Now the company's has cash flow beyond that or organic investment.

And you know the math is simple which is you want to make sure that your costs grow slower than your revenue and good things happen over a long period of time. And I think for me because I came from uh banking, I'm very comfortable with the power of compounding. And so the first thing is you just have to get your core metrics right. Starting with the user metrics, get to the financial metrics, make sure your expenses, unless there's a long-term benefit, are growing slower than your revenue. Duh.

And when it comes to excess capital today, our priorities are going to be keep building the services that we're building now. Uh, invest in algorithms, invest in more engineers. uh we are hiring and then really invest in kind of the new realities of AV — whether it's investments in our partners um or it's making commits to tens of thousands of AV vehicles. Those commitments will be financialized — you know, we just announced the deal with Santander for example to finance both EVs and AVs — but we've got to be the ones developing the market so we are going to make those capital uh commitments.

And the fortunate thing in all this is we've got plenty of cash left over for buybacks, but I prioritize growth. I prioritize innovation over buybacks. Uh if you're building the company right, you'll do both.

二十、不安全感与学习的快乐

You strike me as a very like confident but not cocky person and leader. What if anything are you insecure about in the business or as a leader right now?

I'm not insecure so to speak because again, what good does it do? But but but I'm curious — like it — I think again I I'll go back to Barry.

I found in my life that the more successful you are, the more you tend to talk and the less you tend to listen. Right. Totally. Uh and I see kind of executives who — they don't want to be talked back to uh because they view, "Don't you know who I am? I've got authority etc." And authority doesn't mean that you're right. But so many people do.

And and Barry's the opposite. Like if you get into argument with him — and oh my god it's going to be unpleasant. Um but but you bang it out and at the end of that conversation the majority of time — but sometimes it happens — he realizes he's wrong. He's delighted. He lights up because he just learned.

What kind of a world is an interesting world if you're right all the time? Like the magic happens when you learn? And and so for me, it's not that I'm not confident. I'm I'm confident. I'm comfortable in my own skin, but I'm a learning creature. And for me, the delight comes from learning.

And often that learning has to come with pain. And the pain of being wrong, the pain of hearing something that you don't want to hear, the pain of something unexpected happening today or tomorrow or or the next day. So that that's what gets me going. And and if I'm not wrong, if I'm not making mistakes, it's just not very interesting.

Reed Hastings 的启发

Yeah. I I've loved the Barry story so much. Is there anyone else that comes to mind as someone that's just heavily influenced your way of thinking or behaving?

I'm a bit of a student of business. Kind of came from my investment banking background. I I just love how companies uh shape themselves. But I'd say one one CEO who I really admire is um is uh Reed who you know built Netflix into the company that that it is.

He he is so logical. I think I watched like um him talking with you a while ago. He is so logical and structured in his thinking but at the same time he's an engineer — but then once in a while he's a gambler. You know, when and and some of those don't work, like you was talking about some of the gambles that that didn't work.

So I I find it that there's a superpower in being structured in your thinking but then not too structured in that you're always going with a flow — to be able to again listen to the troublemakers etc. and have those wild ideas um outside of the of the conventional wisdom. The those two often go together. You you need kind of the crazy scientist to do so.

You know, Reed is anything but a crazy scientist, but he has innovated in in a very structured, deliberate way, and I love what he did with his company. And as a person, I just love how he thinks.

二十一、收官：最善意的礼物

So, you're a student of businesses, and I think Uber is just such — it's one of the most interesting business stories, you know, by all accounts, the way that Travis willed this thing into existence as just like a break-down-walls entrepreneur and then the way that you've matured it into this behemoth of a business and all these exciting new things. It's so cool to hear about firsthand. I love doing this with you. When I have these conversations, I ask everyone the same traditional closing question. What is the kindest thing that someone's done for you?

I guess it's Sid, my wife, taking me in. You know, we talked about my um, you know, origin, where I came from and having a chip on the shoulder. And and until I met her, I was always kind of living the life that I thought I was supposed to and I was always the person who I thought you wanted me to be.

And when I met her and observed her, she was always the same person — with me, with the kids, with her friends or, you know, the Sun Valley Conference where there are like these unbelievably accomplished billionaires around. She was always the core, the same core person. She never changed who she was.

And meeting her and seeing her operate, I think, finally allowed me to be the person I want to be versus the person I thought I was supposed to be. And that was a gift and a kindness that uh I could never replace.

I have a shockingly similar experience with my wife. It's a beautiful place to end. Thanks so much for your time.

Thanks.

【尾段广告】Ramp / Vanta / Ridgeline / Rogo / WorkOS

Your finance team isn't losing money on big mistakes. It's leaking through a thousand tiny decisions nobody's watching. Ramp puts guardrails on spending before it happens. Real-time limits, automatic rules, zero firefighting. Try it at ramp.com/invest.

As your business grows, Vanta scales with you, automating compliance and giving you a single source of truth for security and risk. Learn more at vanta.com/invest.

Ridgeline is redefining asset management technology as a true partner, not just a software vendor. They've helped firms 5x and scale, enabling faster growth, smarter operations, and a competitive edge. Visit ridgelineapps.com to see what they can unlock for your firm.

Every investment firm is unique and generic AI doesn't understand your process. Rogo does. It's an AI platform built specifically for Wall Street, connected to your data, understanding your process and producing real outputs. Check them out at rogo.ai/invest.

The best AI and software companies — from OpenAI to Cursor to Perplexity — use WorkOS to become enterprise ready overnight, not in months. Visit workos.com to skip the unglamorous infrastructure work and focus on your product.

item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的洞察：Uber 是一家"概率论公司"伪装成"确定论公司"
大多数人把 Uber 理解为"打车软件"，但 Khosrowshahi 在访谈中无意间泄露了一个关键的世界观：**Uber 的业务在数字层是确定性的，在物理层是概率性的**。这是一个被严重低估的认知框架。
更出人意料的是：这种"双层结构"使 Uber 成为**全球最早被迫与 AI 共生的公司之一**——它早在大模型出现之前就在用 ML 处理现实世界的不确定性。换句话说，当 OpenAI 还在做语言模型的时候，Uber 已经在用 AI 处理"司机为什么取消订单"这种最污浊、最非结构化的现实世界数据。这意味着 Uber 不是"AI 时代的受益者"，而是"AI 早就被迫成为它的母语"。
这解释了一个隐藏现象：**为什么 Uber 是少数几家把 AI 预算一个季度烧完整年的公司却没有人惊慌**——因为对他们来说，AI 不是新东西，是变得更便宜的旧东西。
## 二、跨领域连接：Uber 正在变成"运输业的 Netflix + Foxconn + Costco"——而这三种基因互相冲突
Khosrowshahi

item.title: [All-In Podcast] Bill Ackman: Investment Strategy, What the Market is Missing, How AI Breaks Businesses
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：股价上涨本身就在创造价值

Ackman 在访谈里轻轻说了一句几乎被一带而过的话——"股价越高，公司就真的越值钱"。这听起来像循环论证，但其实是现代资本市场最被忽视的暗物质。传统的 DCF 思维把估值当作公司价值的反映，但 Ackman 揭示的是一个正反馈环：高股价 → 低资本成本 → 更便宜的并购弹药、更大的融资余地 → 更强的护城河 → 更高的内在价值。

这不只是金融技巧。它解释了一个看似不公平的事实：Tesla 和 SpaceX 不是因为基本面好才估值高，而是因为估值高才让基本面变得真正好。 Elon 的"信徒军团"不是市场操纵，而是一种降本武器——把"叙事"直接转化为"加权平均资本成本"。Ackman 自己承认 Persing Square 还没学会这一招，这是他罕见地公开承认自己落后于时代的瞬间。

二、跨领域连接：保险浮存金 = AI 时代的注意力浮存金

Ackman 想用 Howard Hughes 复刻 Berkshire 的飞轮：保险公司收保费在前、赔付在后，中间这段"浮存金"可以拿去投资，本质是用别人的钱、零利率、长久期地复利。

这里有个极其隐蔽的跨领域同构——社交媒体的关注度，本质上和保险浮存金是同一种金融工具： - 关注者"先存入"信任 → 你在某个未来时刻"赔付"价值（产品、洞见、号召） - 中间这段时间差，就是你的"注意力浮存金" - 浮存金的关键不是数量，而是久期 + 成本

Elon 把这个浮存金的久期拉到了 20 年（信徒愿意等火星），成本是负的（他们付费购买信仰）。Sam Altman、Ackman 自己、甚至 Wall Street Bets 的散户领袖，都在玩同一个游戏。未来最强的"保险公司"可能不再卖保单，而是经营叙事。 Howard Hughes 真正的战略升级，可能不是把现金投入保险业务，而是 Ackman 已经在无意识地做的事——把他 220 万粉丝的关注度当作另一种浮存金来运营。

三、被遮蔽的真相：Ackman 的"活动家投资"模型正在被 AI 反噬

Ackman 反复强调，他现在最关心的是"不可被颠覆性"（non-disruptibility）。但仔细听他对 SAS 末日的描述，他其实承认了一个让自己难堪的悖论：

他过去 20 年最值钱的能力——"在董事会里改造企业、释放股东价值"——正在被 AI 彻底贬值。 因为他的活动家剧本（拆分、回购、换 CEO、优化资本结构）本质是在成熟、稳态、可预测的护城河里搬运现金流。而 AI 把"成熟稳态"这个前提变成了笑话。两个斯坦福女生 + 一台 GPU 就能让护城河蒸发。

这就是为什么他越来越像 Buffett——不是因为他选择了 Buffett 风格，而是因为活动家这一行正在结构性消失。Howard Hughes 计划的真正含义是：他在为自己造一艘救生艇，一艘不需要 outsmart 任何 CEO、只需要 outlast 所有人的复利机器。

附带一个意外预测：未来 5 年，最强的活动家投资者不会出现在公开市场，而会出现在 AI 公司的董事会里——因为那里才是"两个人的判断可以决定万亿美元命运"的新战场。Ackman 投资 xAI 这个看似"FOMO"的动作，可能比他自己意识到的更具战略性。

📝 转录文本

Bill Ackman 访谈：投资策略、市场遗漏的东西、AI 如何摧毁企业

开场介绍

One of the most provocative and interesting investors in the country, a legendary activist investor, Persing Square CEO and founder, Bill Ackman.

核心观点摘要： - Taking a short position and going public with it is a pretty serious business. - Interestingly, some of the best businesses in the world are trading at the lowest multiples. - We're kind of the rebirth of the closed end investment company universe.

关于 OpenAI 的 CFO Sarah

Q: What did you think of Sarah, the CEO of OpenAI? I'm sorry, CFO. Felt like the CEO.

Ackman: Yeah, I — stop with that stuff. Uh, actually I was super impressed. Uh, made me a lot more bullish on OpenAI and I thought she should be CEO of OpenAI. That's what I thought. I think Sam should be chair. I think he's much better.

There was a question I wanted to ask her that we didn't get time, which was: what's it like working with Sam? I mean that could have been like three hours in a documentary.

一、投资哲学的演变：从行动派到长期持有

Q: It seems like your investment philosophy may be changing — where you've been activist and entered/exited positions, and lately you've talked about more permanent long-term holdings. Is that actually a change?

Ackman: The biggest change over time is an appreciation for the importance of — I call it business quality — long-term durable protected non-disruptible growth. Early days, you're a smaller, more liquid investor. You don't have to think as long term. As you become a bigger concentrated investor, over time you learn the importance of durable growth. That's the most important factor.

I would say I'm as activist as I've ever been, but more of it's on Twitter than in the corporate context.

Wendy's 与 Tim Hortons 的故事

When I started Persing Square, no one knew who we were. One of our first investments was Wendy's International. Wendy's owned Tim Hortons, the Canadian coffee and donut chain. The value of Tim Hortons was more than the entire value of Wendy's.

So we had this very simple idea: buy Wendy's, spin off Tim Hortons, double our money. We bought 10% of the company and I called the CEO — he didn't return my call. I called him again. He didn't return my call.

So we called a friend at Blackstone, and Steve Schwarzman agreed to write a fairness opinion on what Wendy's would be worth if we spun off Tim Hortons. We mailed it in and filed it publicly. Six weeks later, they spun off.

Then the CEO finally called me back. He thanked me — he had gotten fired, but he had a huge exit package and was very happy.

从敲门到被欢迎

So in the beginning, we couldn't get a return phone call. We had to go to conferences, do presentations, go on CNBC. Over time, you join boards of directors, you become known as an investor. I know pretty much every CEO in the S&P 500, either directly or one person removed. Today we buy a stake in a company and sometimes they'll put out a tweet saying "we welcome Persing Square as a shareholder." They open the door for us.

公司治理的价值

The problem of being a public company today is the very short-term nature of markets, analysts, etc. To run a business, a good one is a forever thing. You want to make decisions in the context of decades, sometimes three to five years. How can you do that when someone's asking about the tax rate in the second quarter?

Having a big shareholder on the board where you can test ideas out before exposing them to the public — or the big shareholder can say "I'm supportive of this initiative even though it's going to hurt earnings in the next few quarters" — is a helpful thing.

二、AI 与商业模式质量

Q: Are you an investor in the AI complex, and how do you underwrite business model quality?

Ackman: Yes, effectively we're an investor. We own Microsoft, Meta, Amazon. You're either directly or indirectly invested in AI, or it's a threat. So you have to understand it.

颠覆性风险的剧增

When you're a concentrated long-term investor, the most important and most challenging thing is to determine the risk of disruption. What's the risk of two guys, two women from Stanford in a garage coming up with something? That risk has gone up dramatically.

This is the greatest era in history to build a business: unlimited access to compute, unlimited access to capital, incredible talent. Which means the probability of being disrupted has gone up enormously.

被遗忘的高质量公司

What's interesting about markets is people always bring their eye to the new new thing — chips, semiconductors, energy. What tends to happen is really high-quality things get left behind.

I was there in 2000, in that bubble. This is different — but there's some analogies. People got excited about internet stocks, and Berkshire Hathaway traded at the lowest valuation in its history. People said, "Okay, that's all old stuff."

A similar thing is happening today to Amazon, Meta, Microsoft. These are "old-fashioned" companies in this OpenAI era. They're undervalued in my mind.

SaaS 末日论

Q: What about the SaaS apocalypse? Is it oversold?

Ackman: It's a careful analysis. I worry more about a Salesforce than I do about Microsoft. If you're a software company today, you have to be as AI-enabled as you can. There have been monopolistic-type profit-taking off customers — niche software products charging $30,000 a year. Those companies are really at risk. Microsoft, where the average customer is paying maybe $50 a seat — that platform is worth a lot more, less at risk.

三、COVID 时刻与市场预测

Q: You had an incredibly viral moment on CNBC during COVID — you pounded the table. Where does that desire to be so active come from?

Ackman: My high school yearbook epithet was "most verbose." My friend's quote next to my name said: "A closed mouth gathers no foot." That's kind of what I've lived by.

In March 2020, I was concerned about the country. I felt we needed a two-week pause. I assumed we'd do a short-term shutdown to let the virus cool down. The president hadn't done that yet. That inspired me to go on TV as a way to reach President Trump and say, "Look, we need to shut down the country for two weeks. The virus will blow over. Stocks are at an incredibly cheap valuation. If we handle this correctly, you're going to make a ton of money." And we were buying.

估值如同橡皮筋

Valuation is like a tether on the market. When it gets too high, it's like a rubber band stretching — inevitably it bounces back. It works the other way as well: when stocks get too cheap, the rubber band is actually pulling valuations up.

Q: What happened recently that caused you to call it out?

Ackman: Stocks just got crazy cheap. Really high-quality companies became incredibly cheap on fundamentals — the present value of cash generated over the company's life.

四、私募估值与风险投资模型

Q: Is there any way to underwrite 100x, 50x, 150x revenue companies?

Ackman: I think you underwrite a SpaceX the way you underwrite a venture capital investment.

People, Opportunity, Context, Deal

I had a professor in business school. He said: "People, Opportunity, Context, Deal."

  • People: SpaceX — one of one.
  • Opportunity: One of one.
  • Context: Incredible. Feel bad for Blue Origin, but not harmful to SpaceX — the fact they're way behind helps.
  • Deal: That's the more complicated question. If it's a trillion, $750 billion — think 5 years out. What is Starlink's trajectory? SpaceX has a near-monopoly in low-cost space launch.

Time has become increasingly valuable in the AI era. You delay a model by a month — you lose a couple of months today and it means a lot.

I invested in xAI through an SPV. Ron Baron said, "Bill, you got to invest in SpaceX." So I'm in.

Anthropic、OpenAI、Palantir

These are venture investments — not seed or Series A, but D or E. They've proven they can generate a lot of revenues.

Sarah had a very thoughtful explanation on how they think about committing capital. That's the thing I haven't heard from OpenAI. If I were OpenAI, I'd be getting that message out. From the outside, it's a pretty interesting business model — a company making capital commitments massively in excess of revenues. The degree of difficulty is hard.

五、Fortune 500 CEO 如何思考 AI

Q: How are traditional Fortune 500 CEOs thinking about AI?

Ackman: Every CEO in America today is thinking: How do I use AI? How does it apply to my business? How is it a threat? They've got to find an internal champion, maybe recruit someone from outside. On the hierarchy of things they worry about, it's probably number one as both an opportunity and a threat. Your board is going to ask you the first question every meeting about how you're dealing with the AI threat and opportunity.

早期成功案例罕见

Q: Have you seen much actual success?

Ackman: I haven't seen much success. I'll give you the Persing Square story — we're a tiny little company. How we're using AI today: the first use case is really on the legal side and compliance back-office functionality. I think we're still super, super early in terms of big companies using AI effectively.

六、创始人主导 vs. 职业经理人

Q: Have you looked at founder-led companies vs. non-founder-led — perhaps founders have an inherent advantage in navigating change?

Ackman: I think the answer is exactly what you said. The average life of an S&P 500 CEO is three to four years. You're focused on shorter-term compensation. You don't have a big economic stake.

A founder — this is your entire life, your entire reputation. You're not going to go get another job. You've got to make it work. In the boardroom, you have authority either as a major voting voice or because you've got a huge economic stake.

Look at Mark Zuckerberg — when he bought Instagram or WhatsApp, everyone was shocked at the price. WhatsApp had like 19 employees when he paid a billion. But you make enough of those calls, and you can make the other challenging calls.

Ben Graham 模型的局限

Ben Graham is a really important voice: a stock certificate is an interest in a business, not just a piece of paper. That's one of his most important aphorisms.

But he was investing for the most part in liquidations. In the days of Ben Graham, there was no EDGAR system. To get a 10K filing, you had to go to the headquarters. There were a lot of stocks trading at basically the cash on the balance sheet. His business model was buying these things at stupidly cheap prices. But Ben Graham made most of his money investing in Geico.

七、Howard Hughes 与 Berkshire 2.0

Q: Tell us about Howard Hughes — you've talked about being inspired by Berkshire Hathaway.

Ackman: There's a book — I think it's called the financial history of Berkshire Hathaway. That's for geeks. This guy went back and read every 10-Q, looked at every deal Buffett ever did, followed him over 60 years.

保险浮存金的核心

The vast majority of value Buffett created at Berkshire was through ownership of an insurance operation. Running an insurance company, you have two jobs: 1. Write business — take risk, collect premiums in exchange for the obligation to pay future claims. 2. Invest that money.

The vast majority of insurance companies focus only on the liability side of the balance sheet. Buffett was really the first to focus more on the asset side.

If you manage assets and liabilities well, you can build an enormously profitable, compounding, tax-efficient machine over time.

为什么没人复制？

Why haven't other people done this? If you're really good at investing, you go work for a hedge fund, Fidelity, Wellington — not an insurance company. So insurance companies' ability to recruit investment talent is very limited. Buffett owned half the company. He was really good at investing, which is why it worked.

Howard Hughes 的故事

Buffett started with a crappy textile company, effectively liquidated it, reinvested in insurance, then invested the assets.

Howard Hughes is a really interesting company that Wall Street has not cared about for a long time. We created it out of the bankruptcy of General Growth — a spin-off of all the other assets.

It owns small cities. A lot of you have heard of Summerlin — a lot of the tech community has moved from California to Las Vegas. We own this small city: 26,000 acres of land, all commercial land, all residential land. We sell lots to home builders, build a downtown, build buildings.

It's a bit like the Irvine Company. Don Bren created probably $100 billion of personal wealth managing a small city. Super cool company, but the timeframe is decades, not quarters. So Wall Street has never cared. It's always traded at a huge discount.

战略转型

Buffett bought into a textile business at a discount to liquidation value at $63 a share. You're owning Howard Hughes at a discount to liquidation value.

Instead of reinvesting all the cash into real estate, we're going to reinvest it into insurance. Within the next week or so, we're going to build this into a compounding machine over the next 50 years. It's something I've always wanted to do.

投资策略细节

What Buffett did is he took 100% of the insurance float and put the money in short-term treasuries. So he took no risk on policyholder funds. He took 100% of the surplus — the equity — and invested in common stocks. That's what we're going to do.

We're starting at a very small scale. The company's got a $4 billion market cap. The goal is to build it into a trillion-dollar thing over time. The other thing Buffett did well: he didn't issue any stock for a very long time. He started with a million shares and today has effectively about a million.

为什么必须公开上市？

Q: Why does it have to be public?

Ackman: It doesn't have to be. We got here by accident. The most successful equity investment we ever made was General Growth — we bought the stock of a company going bankrupt. The most contrarian investment you can make.

Stock went from a $20 billion market cap to $100 million. We bought 27% of the company at $200 million market cap, with $27 billion of debt. Strategy: the assets are worth more than the liabilities. We did the first restructuring where the equity kept their investment.

Two years later, we emerged from Chapter 11. The stock went from 34 cents to $34. Part of the restructuring was spinning off Howard Hughes — all the junk that didn't belong in the company. So it was an inadvertent investment. 15 years later, we haven't really created much value with it.

资本成本是关键

The market — a company has to earn a return in excess of its cost of capital for a stock to go up. Elon's done an amazing job keeping the cost of capital of his companies really low. SpaceX going public at $1.75 trillion will probably be the lowest cost of capital equity transaction in the history of the world.

The problem with Howard Hughes — because it's real estate, development, land ownership — the market says the cost of capital is really high. So we're repurposing the real estate assets and transforming the company into a much higher-returning business.

八、名声、社交媒体与市场

Q: How does fame change the way markets work?

Ackman: I don't think markets have changed as a result of anything that's happened with me. But the Ryan Cohen / GameStop story — that is a change. A stock can trade at a valuation well above its value simply on personality and the ability to gather armies of followers. Vibes.

流动性与估值的反身性

The fascinating thing about liquidity and valuation: the higher a stock price goes — this sounds intuitive but isn't — the more valuable the company becomes. The increase in value of the company increases the value of the company. Because it lowers the cost of capital, gives you flexibility, gives you the ability to issue stock, raise capital, acquire other businesses.

Getting back to Elon — he's a better example of this. We've not taken advantage of this at all. Maybe we should. He built an army of believers and followers that enabled Tesla to be built.

九、如何与 Bill Ackman 对齐？

Q: If people want to be maximally aligned with Bill Ackman, is it best to be an LP in Persing Square or go into the market and buy?

Ackman: There are three ways to invest with us:

1. Persing Square (管理公司)

The management company at Persing Square. One of the most intellectually interesting businesses — it's the entity that receives fees on three permanent capital vehicles we manage. It's a royalty on the compounding of investments. No capex. We're going to pay out basically all of our profits and grow as quickly as the underlying assets compound.

If you invested $1 in Persing Square 22 years ago, that became 27 or 28 times net of all fees. Had we charged the fees of this public vehicle, it would have been in the mid-40s.

We now have a public vehicle that charges only a 2% fee. We've got one in London that charges an incentive fee. If we compound at historical rates, we'll have 35 times the assets under management in 22 years — going from $25 billion to approaching $1 trillion. We don't have to hire another person or spend another dollar on overhead.

2. PSUS

If you want to invest with us as an investor, invest in PSUS — you own a portfolio of our best ideas, trading at an 18% discount.

3. Howard Hughes

If you want to believe we can build the next Berkshire Hathaway, you own Howard Hughes.

收尾：Going Direct 的力量

Q: Following you on Twitter — the "going direct" movement — allows you to communicate your vision directly, makes it easier to place the bet.

Ackman: [On the Ronda tweet, which had legal implications, I had my communications guy and a lawyer friend read it — but I only gave him a few minutes because I was so excited. Once I write something I really like, I just want to hit send.]

By the way, it's a very powerful thing to be able to share your view and push a button and reach 2.2 million people.

Why don't we take a picture on stage and I'll send it out.

Hell yeah. Let's do it. Liquidity. Gone all in. Thank you.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：股价上涨本身就在创造价值
Ackman 在访谈里轻轻说了一句几乎被一带而过的话——"股价越高，公司就真的越值钱"。这听起来像循环论证，但其实是现代资本市场最被忽视的暗物质。传统的 DCF 思维把估值当作公司价值的**反映**，但 Ackman 揭示的是一个**正反馈环**：高股价 → 低资本成本 → 更便宜的并购弹药、更大的融资余地 → 更强的护城河 → 更高的内在价值。
这不只是金融技巧。它解释了一个看似不公平的事实：**Tesla 和 SpaceX 不是因为基本面好才估值高，而是因为估值高才让基本面变得真正好。** Elon 的"信徒军团"不是市场操纵，而是一种**降本武器**——把"叙事"直接转化为"加权平均资本成本"。Ackman 自己承认 Persing Square 还没学会这一招，这是他罕见地公开承认自己落后于时代的瞬间。
## 二、跨领域连接：保险浮存金 = AI 时代的注意力浮存金
Ackman 想用 Howard Hughes 复刻 Berkshire 的飞轮：保险公司收保费在前、赔付在后，中间这段"浮存金"可以拿去投资，本质是**

item.title: [Lex Clips] Speed of light explained: Was Einstein's theory correct? | Don Lincoln and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽视的反转：光速不是"限制"，而是"汇率"

大多数人把光速理解为宇宙的"速度上限"——一道不可逾越的墙。但 Don Lincoln 在末尾抛出的那句话其实是一个隐秘的哲学炸弹：光速是穿过"时空"的速度，不是穿过"空间"的速度。

换句话说，宇宙中所有东西其实都在以光速运动——只是分配在时间维度和空间维度上的比例不同。你坐着不动时，几乎所有"运动配额"都花在了时间方向上（这就是为什么你会"变老"）；光子把全部配额都花在空间方向上，所以它根本不经历时间。

这意味着 c 与其说是"速度限制"，不如说是时间和空间之间的汇率——就像 1 美元兑 7 元人民币那样，c 决定了"一秒钟"值多少"米"。爱因斯坦真正发现的，不是宇宙有一堵墙，而是宇宙有一个兑换率。

跨领域连接：粒子物理实验与"信使被信使背叛"的悖论

转录中描述的那个实验其实有一种文学性的诡异：高速运动的母粒子衰变出光子，光子却"拒绝"继承母亲的速度。这在日常直觉里是荒谬的——就像一辆 200 公里时速的卡车扔出一个棒球，棒球却只以棒球本身的速度飞行，完全无视卡车。

这里有一个出人意料的跨领域映射：这与语言学中的"指示语漂移"现象惊人相似。当你转述别人的话时，"我"这个词会改变所指——它不继承说话者的身份。光子也像是宇宙的"引用符号"：它不携带源头的运动状态，它只携带"光速"这个绝对属性。从这个角度看，光在物理学中扮演的角色，与代词在语言中扮演的角色，本质上是同一种"去语境化的信使"。

隐藏的系统性洞察：爱因斯坦的"假设—预测—验证"是一种反向工程

注意 Lincoln 描述科学验证的顺序——这其实揭示了物理学的一个反常识结构：

我们并不是先证明假设正确，再相信它的预测；而是因为它的预测正确，才反向认定假设正确。

这是一种类似贝叶斯逆推的认识论结构，和侦探小说里 Sherlock Holmes 的方法、机器学习中的"反向传播"、以及精神分析中"通过症状反推潜意识"是同构的。物理学最深的真理，往往不是被"看见"的，而是被"它的影子的形状"出卖的。

更耐人寻味的是 Lincoln 的最后一句："一旦我接受了，一切都变得很舒服。" 这其实暴露了一个隐藏的真相——物理学家不是被证据说服的，是被"优雅"说服的。光速恒定不是因为它"显然正确"，而是因为承认它之后，整个宇宙的描述方式变得对称、简洁、可计算。这是审美驱动的科学，而不是经验驱动的科学。这一点，与音乐理论中"为什么我们选择十二平均律"的逻辑惊人地相似——不是因为它最自然，而是因为它最好用。

📝 转录文本

闵可夫斯基与狭义相对论的两个前提

In 1908, Hermann Minkowski really laid it out in the strict space-time. Uh and that also led to the work on special relativity led to the speed limit, the speed of light.

Well, it was a premise. He had two premises.

  • One was that the laws of nature are the same for everybody. So, if you're moving at some speed or if I'm moving at some speed, I can say I'm not moving and saying you're moving at some speed. That's not controversial. That is what we call Galilean relativity. It's from hundreds of years ago.
  • But what Einstein said that was controversial was that everybody measures that the speed of light is the same. Irrespective of how we're moving with respect to each other. You'll measure the speed of light to be a number. I'll measure the speed of light to a number. And that's very, [clears throat] very different from what Newton would have said or Galileo or any of the old guys.

And it was taking those two things together the cause all of the weirdnesses of special relativity.

为什么这个"古怪"假设值得相信

Now, you could then very easily say, "Well, that second premise that everybody measures the speed of light to the same is just dumb." And it that you know, you could test that. So, that's where testing relativity comes in.

And Einstein's equations, which include those two assumptions, it predicts the behavior of everything perfectly well. Now, we've actually measured uh done experiments where we can say that the speed of light is the same for everybody. That's not how that's been in the beginning. It was really that assumption leads to predictions. The predictions are true, so the assumption is true.

粒子物理学家如何测量光速恒定

Now, there is a for for those people for your viewers who want to say, "Well, how do you measure that the speed of light is the same for everyone?" The particle physicists do this. And the way you do this is the following.

There are some subatomic particles that when they decay, they emit light. That's their decay product. And so, you collide two things together, so you know when the particle was created. Then you have surround your collision point by a detector, and you measure how long it takes for light to get to your detector, and my God, it's the speed of light, which it should be.

However, sometimes in these collisions, some of these subatomic particles you make are coming out at very high speed. They might be coming out at 95 or 97 or very large fraction of the speed of light. And then they decay into photons. And so, you measure how long it takes for the photon to get to your detector, and it says it's light travels at the speed of light.

Now, if it were that if Einstein's conjecture was incorrect, you'd have a particle coming out at near the speed of light, it would be decaying into a particle traveling at the speed of light, then that particle should have traveled at, say, two times the speed of light or something like that, so it should have taken half as much time to get to the detector, but it doesn't.

So, this is a hard, serious measurement that shows that something, you know, we we can measure the speed at which light comes out of this stationary created particle, and it's the speed of light. Then we can measure what the speed is of it coming out of something that's moving, and it's still the speed of light. So, that is an actual measurement, but that is not something that was possible in Einstein's day, but it is now.

光速限制究竟有多"古怪"？

Just uh take a small tangent, uh how weird is it in the full ranking of weirdness that is physics, how weird is it that there's that speed limit of the speed of light?

Well, I have to tell you, when I first encountered this, it's pretty freaking weird. It's like pegs the weird meter. But as you become more familiar with it, this you become more more comfortable with the idea.

The thing to remember is the speed of light. It's the speed of light through space-time. Once you embrace that, that makes a whole ton of sense. It all of a sudden makes everything fall much more into place.

光速是"空间本身"的属性

I think that there is an ultimate speed. Isn't that shocking? It just simply says that it's a property of space in the same way that there is, you know, space can can transmit a certain strength electric field. It can trans- It can support a certain things.

Whatever space is, and we don't know what space is, but whatever it is, it has the capability of of transmitting these things at that one speed through space or time. And everything else comes from our insisting that we keep space and time different.

That's That's how I view it. And at least for me, that once I accepted that, it all became very comfortable.

观看原视频 →
