I signed halfway through the season. So they were rock bottom of the league when I joined and it was funny because I was actually pretty surprised that I'd passed the medical, which no one knows. I had a herniated disc that was pretty bad and but I didn't want to sign and be injured. So I did everything I could. I had steroids, I was doing the most to be fit because I didn't wanna let him down as well for taking that risk in signing me and giving me that opportunity.

After the first game, I made my debut in the second half. I can't remember who it was against now. But then after that, we didn't lose for like 11 games since I started the first game. So we went from rock bottom to like fifth in the league. And it was incredible. I mean, even I hadn't hit that form in a long time, you know, at West Brom. And so I think that, I mean, after that, I ended up having to have back surgery. So I had surgery right at the end of that season.

But it was a case of, you know, the club was not in a great position at the time in terms of its position in the league. I think it was its third year.

Was the quality of football very different? Like slower, less physical, less intense?

It was more physical, it was more physical, yeah, yeah. I kind of compare it to like checkers in America. It's just end to end and very fast and no real kind of structure like chess. You know, chess is slow, but then it's very, very quick. Whereas checkers is just, you know, it's just chaos, isn't it really? And that's kind of how I describe it. Less structure, but more physical. They don't keep the ball as much. They don't have that structure. The league is dominated a lot by Latin players. And so, the high talent. But in terms of footballing kind of structure, it's growing, but it's low.

On structure, we often speak to owners or CEOs, and they often talk about training grounds and facilities around it. Comparably, training grounds, facilities, amazing around it when you're arriving into Miami and the Americans crush it, or not?

Facilities are good. Facilities are good. The training ground is amazing. I mean, it's very difficult to compare it to a club like Arsenal. But yeah, they have good infrastructure there.

Do you feel quite pampered when you were in Arsenal? When everything is, I'm sure, so beautifully done and so perfect?

It's definitely a unique environment in terms of, I think, the way everything is set up there at the training ground. I think that the more higher pressure club that you're at, the nicer the environment. It kind of reflects how big of a club you're at. And obviously the food, the facilities, the level of care is another level.

梅西效应与 MLS 的天花板

What's happened with the club now? You've got the Messi effect. Everyone knows what that's done. How have you seen it? You stayed in Miami, right? So you might be… you stayed out there, you've seen this. You've been at a club in an early stage of its life. You've seen it grow into now or growing into something which is a global brand.

Well, I mean, when I arrived there, I think they were still figuring out the culture side of it more. And it takes years and years to build culture, right? You're talking about a club that was three years old when I got there. And so most people, they probably don't know what they're following as such. It takes time to build that. And I didn't realize that until I got there because I just grew up in the ranks at Arsenal and I just thought that that was the standard. So it was apparent to me when I moved there that for me it's culture first. It sets the rest of the tone for a club, for a community, for a business. It is the single most important thing. And I didn't realize that when I was a player, but I realized that when I went to Inter Miami.

And obviously, you know, now the club will be in a completely different place to when I was last there, which was three years ago. Now they have the world's greatest player. That will start to induce a new culture with probably how Messi wants or expects things. I think that that will probably be filtering through the club at the moment.

Is it night and day when you see the Messi effect? Like just the teams and teams of fans that are now new into Miami fans, to press, the spotlight, the attention that comes.

Yeah, I mean, I went to the first game that he played and the atmosphere was just completely different.

Was it when I was playing there? Yeah, not just there, but everywhere he goes in any stadium that he goes to play in in America. It's just the buzz around the place and the rate of growth of interest in the sport, not just from Messi being there, I think in general, it's increasing rapidly.

Well, this is a discussion we have a lot, right? And Harry's quite critical, not critical, skeptical, probably the right word, of, I know, weird, him skeptical. Why are you here? Yeah, you see these bounces in athletes when they go to places now because athletes are so, well, the top athletes are followed by, more so than a lot of clubs, right? But while that's amazing when they're there at the club, does that sustain when they leave? And if you're looking at Miami as a business, right? You're loving it right now. But how concerned are you that when Messi leaves, a lot of that appeal, a lot of that value goes with him?

I mean, it depends if, you know, it goes with him because is he going to, will he continue playing after this? So I think that the club will always attract amazing players, maybe not to the level of a Messi, but I think the way that fans and players are building their brands, they will actually be able to build brands that can be— I'm talking about brands, maybe not level of player, but brands that will attract fans because of technology, that will be huge audiences. So clubs will be able—Inter Miami will bring another player eventually, maybe not as close to Messi, but close enough.

But my argument is that essentially they will not sustain the Messi effect and that you have an increasing number of sports and events which take consumer time and attention and football will not break into tier one entertainment in the US as a result of the competitive budget for consumer attention.

I disagree. I disagree because I think if you look at how new the sport actually is there, you know, the MLS was founded in 96, right? What has happened since— now you're about to have a World Cup there. So it's been 30 years. If you look at what's happened in those 30 years, it's actually been quite a rapid growth. And I think if you look at what's gonna happen probably off the back of the next World Cup, I think the next 30 years, I think you will rapidly— I actually think the complete opposite.

Do you not think that is predicated on getting tier one players to leave tier one leagues in a time where they are playing their best football, not great players at the end of their career wanting to monetize building a brand?

Yes, some things definitely have to change. Some things definitely have to change. And some tough decisions have to be made by the governing bodies that are over there. That is also without a doubt.

What is the toughest decision that has to be made by the governing bodies?

I think they need to pay players more. I think they need to pay players more. I think the fact that you can have a player that earns $6 million a year and another player that earns $60,000 a year is just unacceptable, to be honest.

Can you explain the structure there? Because how pay works in the US is different to how it works in other leagues.

I don't know how and why it started, but yeah, you basically have three designated players on each team that are exempt from normal salary cap of every of each club. And I guess it comes down to the fact that the teams are basically owned by the league. And so what you have obviously you have that you have the single entity structure that's owned by the league. And so they can't just then go and pay players exactly what they want. They have to keep it equal. And I don't know how they're going to be able to change it. But to attract more quality players, as you're saying, you have to pay them more. It's really self-explanatory.

So the other thing that you're saying on that though is like every American has an English football team that they support. That's the funniest thing. I have so most of my friends are American. They all like Arsenal, they all like Chelsea, they all like Man U.

What about the MS?

I don't watch that. Yeah.

But I'm with the concentration of quality and talent towards the English Football League, which is great. You have more people want it. But how many Americans were watching even the Premier League, you know, up until how many years ago? Much fewer. Much fewer. Yeah. So I think that there's a lot of catching up to do, but I think that there are going to be significant milestones that happen after the back of this World Cup that's coming.

"把一半工资换成比特币"：背后真实的动机

Yeah. You mentioned pay. Tell everyone what you did with your salary when you first signed.

Yeah. So we announced a new sponsorship about two weeks after I signed the club, a new front of jersey sponsor called XPTO. They're a crypto finance company. And the club basically messaged me and said, oh, we're doing a launch of this new sponsor. Would you like to come? They're a crypto finance company and we can see your— they could see my Twitter and stuff. And they said that we can see that you like Bitcoin. And I said, yeah, sure. I went to the launch. I met the whole company, met the founders, and we just got on really well. And I did the launch with them.

And they said, would you— I actually asked them if they would be able to do it. And they said, yeah, we will explore it.

So I do what?

Pay half of my salary in Bitcoin.

Yeah. Half your salary in Bitcoin. Yeah. How much was that?

It wasn't, obviously. It was 85% less than West Brom.

So we keep you in here to wear you down. After six hours, you'll be like, it was 13, 15, 20. And I'm like, ah! Okay. The half salary in Bitcoin. Okay.

Yeah. Yeah. I mean, it was more of a— I did actually do it. It was— they didn't actually send me Bitcoin, it was more of like 50% of my salary was, it was like a direct debit. Cause you technically you're not allowed to, by law you weren't allowed to do it. So, cause we couldn't pass it through the MLS. So I just had like a direct debit that basically sent them 50% and then they sent me the exact— Yeah, they converted it and sent them back.

The reason why I announced that was because there were two reasons really. I wanted to kind of increase my network in the area of Miami and it actually worked because when I put it out on Twitter I had so many people messaging me. I had Pomp, Anthony Pompliano—I don't think you're, I don't know if you know, Joey Pompliano's brother. Yes. Balaji, the ex-CTO of Coinbase. Very smart guy. Michael Saylor. And it just completely spiderwebbed my network in that industry, which was what I wanted to do. I wanted to kind of get a deeper understanding of the technology.

But I also wanted to let people know that this is a powerful technology. And I wanted to try to help people understand it more. And I felt that by announcing something like that, you know, like a salary announcement that's showing high conviction. And so I thought that maybe someone might do some research on it like I did, you know, because it helped me massively when I first got into the space.

Did you feel the volatility of the price swings? Because obviously your salary is then tied to the price of Bitcoin. And so it's some weeks going up massively and some weeks you're not looking good.

Yeah, yeah, yeah. But by then I was already four years into the technology in terms of understanding it.

I'm not being a dick and I'm not asking for money or numbers, but at that stage, it's an awful term, post-economic, where you're like, eh, I didn't need it. Like when you go to the US, you're like, okay, I've made X amount of money. I know that's safe. I'm good.

Yeah, this was not about the actual money. This was how can I get people researching this stuff? I think that players have platforms, and I've never really come out and spoken about politics or religion. And I felt that for me, I was attaching my name to something that I felt can help many people. And that was why I wanted to do it.

What did your advisors say to you? But no, I mean— no, no, no, no, but I'm— Yeah, no, it's a good question. I'm actually being serious because like, actually, I think it's very sound financial decision making. Respectfully, agents and football agents are not very good investors, are not very good portfolio managers, actually aren't equipped to manage your money very efficiently at all. Most wealth advisors are actually not very good either and they're incentivized by fees and you trading and transacting, which often isn't the best either. What were they advising you?

By this time, I was already 27. And so, you know, I think if this would have happened, you know, before, then I don't think my mum would have— she had less control, she had less control by this point, but she was also not happy at all.

But also it sounds like you've done, you know, it wasn't like this was a, I wake up one day, read an article and I'm gonna go and bung half my cash in there. You said you spent four years looking at this. You make a very specific decision at a, as you say, specific time of your career on a much smaller salary. You probably assessed your percentage risk where you're going, okay, like I can do this. And as you say, if it happened to go to zero, let's just say, it would be gutting, but I'm not broken. So I'd imagine for you it's part of a much longer process than just a…

Oh, absolutely. No, no, no. The first time I bought Bitcoin was in 2017. The amount of Bitcoin that I bought through my salary was probably less than 2% of what I'd owned total. So it was purely publicity. It was purely publicity at that point.

退役之后：资产配置与"美国身份"

Can I ask you, it's lovely when money's coming in. Then you stop playing. And the money flows stop. And you see this with a lot of players where it's like, oh shit, didn't factor that into my thinking. What does that look like? Is it all presenting, investing, dividends? What does the money flows look like today for you?

Yeah. So, you know, I sold a lot of assets over the last three years since moving to America that ended up turning out all right. But yeah, I think as a US resident, owning foreign assets is a complete pain. So I think it was time for me to like consolidate and I knew I was gonna be based in the US. I knew I wanted to be based in the US longer term and then now I just— yeah, I now I just have it based in the US that is with a very trusted person that I work very closely with and it's spread across stock market and a bunch of other different asset classes.

创业：与 Matuidi 共同打造"美国足球机构"

Tell everyone what you're doing now. You got really cool business that we've already spoken about. You said how was one and who you're currently working with now is another, is like the top athletes from the business perspective. But what you guys are doing is really cool. Tell everyone about it.

Yeah, so I got lucky enough, to be honest, to play at Inter Miami with Blaise Matuidi, who obviously I played against him for PSG and against France as well. An amazing player, but an even more incredible person. Played with him at Inter. He decided he wanted to stay in the US long term, and I decided the same. And we, together along with Sylvain Marvaux, just as remarkable as Hal and Blaze actually, in terms of his intellect and his ability to operate at such a high level. I couldn't— I just can't believe the guy that he is.

We set up the club together two years ago, and the idea is to create a team— I mean, at least for me, I think we all agree that we want a world-class football institution built on strong values, strong community, and we want to base it in the US.

What does that mean? That's like training grounds?

No, youth academy. So it's youth academy training the next generation.

Yeah, I don't even like to call it an academy, honestly. I like to call it an institution because I think for us, there's no— I don't think we want to limit ourselves. I believe that we will probably break into the ownership level in terms of at the MLS level. We compete already in the MLS Next, which is the highest youth academy league that you can compete in. And then obviously you have the MLS that sits at the top and in between you have what's called the MLS Next Pro, which is kind of like the feeder league into the MLS. Non-MLS teams can also have Next Pro franchises, and it works pretty much identically to the MLS model, but just on a much smaller scale.

What's it like, academy side, kids coming into the game, talent, what's that like in the US compared to what it was or what it is your experiences in the UK? Where are we at with football at grassroots over there?

We are still behind to be candid, but it's growing. But I think culturally is what's keeping it behind. And that's what we're trying to inject.

I think what you have in obviously in the US is kind of like a top-down structure. So the grassroots is kind of like the last people that get affected by the sport, right? The leagues were kind of built first and the infrastructure was built first and the culture followed. Whereas in England and in Europe, the culture was what was built first. Football was built from teams out of churches and factory workers and war, England played against Germany on Christmas day, you know, like the culture was built first here. And that is what I think has slowed America down in terms of producing top talent. So what we wanna do is try to inject some of that kind of bottom-up culture, and that comes from the experiences that we had as players.

Doesn't it come back to also though, grabbing those kids and making sure they're playing soccer, not necessarily going and playing all the other options that you have in the US? Because over here, academy football, as you said, football is so ingrained into the fibers of this country. Football academies thrive because it's the number one priority for a lot of kids when they are first discovering sport.

But the market honestly is huge. America. It's the country so big. You have 350 million people there. And soccer is actually— it's really big there. The interest in the sport is not the issue there.

Do you not think it's exclusionary to young people actually? The cost of soccer for young people is very prohibitive in the US.

Yes. And this is a big part of our business. Obviously we operate as— we have to operate as a business because we don't own our own facility. We don't have 200 million to go and buy a facility and put kids through our system that way. We lease our fields, we have to pay our coaches. You know, we're not part of some big institution, unfortunately. But I think that there is a massive opportunity for big companies to come in and support and kind of, you know, join a movement because it has to be a longer-term vision if you want to create something really big there. And we can't do it on our own, obviously. We're not big enough.

But also, you generally have to— to make a sport successful in its domestic market is you have to ensure that you have a good pipeline of players that come from that market. Saudi's greatest challenge is great— you bring in all these international stars, but at some point, the academies have to be feeding Saudi stars in because that's going to be the lifeblood of that league for 20, 30 years. The US has the same. You want those players at the top, top level. I think we have four in the Premier League right now. You've got some that have really successful over the years but you want to be retaining the Chris Richards of the world that you know like in the US systems playing in those leagues inspiring the communities because that's how you build the culture. Yes we grew up here following the England players— yeah I love all the international players but still, yeah Frank, you know, when we were growing up with Chelsea fans, right? You have that kind of grounding and that to me feels so important for the next stage so you kind of coming at it from that reverse angle.

Yeah, yeah. And that is, you know, it's also selfishly given me kind of a new lease of life. I think the first year I retired, it took me— I was a little bit lost and now I've found that passion and it kind of made me think about all the people that helped me get a chance to have a career in this game and change the life of myself and my families. And now I feel completely responsible to do the same for other kids. And they need someone to look up to. We take that responsibility fully on our shoulders and we're on the field most days while we're there. And we're really on the ground trying to see how we can add value.

美国世界杯：英格兰、热浪与"日常感"

Finally, a couple of minutes on World Cup in the US. You play for England, right? You've had that experience. The pressures that come with it, I'd imagine are insane. The expectation of this country on its international players are huge, particularly coming up to a World Cup.

Oh, God. I mean, I wasn't lucky enough to go to a World Cup, but I can tell you that, yeah, putting on the England jersey, there is no feeling like it. There is no feeling like it because you realize that all of the grounds that you go to, that you've played at up until that point as a scholar, as an academy player, you realize that actually you are now representing all of those people. So the magnitude just completely is completely different to representing your club. And you have that at the same time as having an incredible feeling of pride. I can only imagine what it's like stepping out on the finals of a World Cup stage.

How do you think it's gonna be? I was speaking to someone from New York who lives in New York yesterday, and he was saying, you still wouldn't know the World Cup is on, which was interesting, right? So I hope it ramps up in the time. Do you—

Yeah. It's still got that feeling in Miami. Yes. Yeah. Yeah. I mean, I've been back a week and so, yeah, I just left and I— But do you think when it's on, it will be?

No one can stop World Cup fever. It's the biggest event in the world. And so I think what is—

Is it bigger than the Olympics?

Yeah, surely. Yeah. I mean, well, whatever veterans you're measuring on, they're the two biggest. But if you're really looking at fandom, right? Yeah, if you're watching, who's going to be watching? People love watching the Olympics, but every single pub pretty much in London, every single one is booked out. You literally cannot find space. Brazil, they close the Brazil Stock Exchange. That's how big it is. It's absolutely enormous.

But yeah, I think that the fact that it's spread out between the other countries is probably the only thing that will make it kind of less intense as it could be.

Weather? Everyone's been mentioning this. You played obviously out there in the conditions.

Yeah. I don't know how they're going to do it.

Really? Yeah. I don't know. I don't know how they're going to do it to that level.

What did you do to get through it? I know it sounds stupid, but is it like hydration? Is it electrolytes? Is it you change your prep plan?

I didn't play 190 minutes in, for example, in that 11 games, were my first 11 games. I played, I don't think I played more than 80 minutes. It was just impossible. Especially as, you know, I think for me, I arrived and played straight away. They've got a couple of warm-up games and I think that that will help them— that will help them a little bit, but it's just very difficult. Very difficult. Especially in Miami. So well, you know, I think it will play a part. It's gonna play a big part. Big part.

I saw— and I think again, I haven't checked it but I saw someone say that it'll be as hot out there as it would have been if you played the Qatar World Cup in the summer, which obviously they moved to the winter, in some of the cities where it's playing. Interesting.

Interesting. Yeah. Well, I think the hottest place on earth is in Death Valley, isn't it, in the US? Like, isn't that where the highest temperatures on earth have been recorded? So it's going to be insane.

快问快答（Quickfire Round）

Yeah. Are you ready for a quick fire round?

Okay. Are we ready for this?

What was your favorite stadium to play at that was not home ground?

The Signal Iduna Park. Oh, good one. Yeah. The what? Dortmund, Borussia Dortmund.

Who is the most impressive physical specimen you have played with or against?

Danny Welbeck, just an absolute specimen. He would walk into the gym after not being in the gym for months and doing upper body, and he would just put 100kg on the bench and just start doing reps. Really? He was just as complete. Or Mika Richards. But he was just huge, right? I mean, that was a strut. But he was also just strong as well, like naturally strong.

Him? Oh, those two? Who?

Danny Welbeck, by far. Danny— I mean, Danny Welbeck's obviously had a little like— well, he hasn't resurrected, he's been actually quite solid for a long time. 14, 13, 14 Premier League goals, highest scoring English player, Ollie Watkins just got him on the final day, but yeah, but he was unlucky. You know, there's a lot of people wanting him to go to the World Cup this summer.

I'll be honest, I'm very surprised he's not there. Quite a lot of people have said, how is he still playing? Yeah, yeah, quite a lot of— what's football, it's quite hard. How many home runs does he go?

A few people have said and I've heard it in their other interviews, he's like, he's quite up on the list of some of the most impressive people that people have played with as well. So I think he's massively, by the sounds of it, appreciated.

Yeah, he is.

Okay, who's the least impressive physical?

Santi Cazorla. Oh, well played. Why? Well played. Just like a pudding. Just just just just epitomized everything that I thought a footballer would not be. But yet, was the best player I ever played with.

What, just like bad diet, bad?

No, I just think yeah, genetically, like he just wasn't, you know, he wasn't built like your average footballer.

You finished off with the best player I've ever played with. So that's why I was going.

Because you've got a guy in there who you can save physically, but the talent was just…

Extraordinary, right? I mean, the best player. Most players that played with him will tell you, will say Santi Cazorla.

Is it wrong to say, like, how did he compare to a Mesut Ozil? Just in how they played?

I just think they were different. I just think they were different. I mean, Mesut closely follows, but I've just not seen— I've not seen the way Santi plays. I mean, the same with Mesut, really. I hadn't seen anyone play the game like him, very unique. But I think for Santi, because he wasn't blessed physically, do you know how good you have to be to be able to play at that level when you don't have the physical kind of attributes that you're almost— I wouldn't say you're at a disadvantage because it was also his blessing that he was able to be so agile and, you know, kind of could spin on a sixpence.

Van Persie the best striker?

Best striker. Hammer of a left foot. Yeah, Van Persie best striker.

Who did you hate marking most?

Eden Hazard. Why? Because you just couldn't get near him. I just felt that he— yeah, it was just one of those. I mean, there was— yeah, it was probably him. I didn't like playing against Antonio Valencia. He was tough. Even when he was at Wigan. I was playing against him when he was at Wigan. And he was, yeah, just another— he was a specimen. But yeah, Eden Hazard on his day was just unplayable.

He was one of the players— I think we used to be quite an ego-driven team in terms of we felt that we should outplay everyone that we played against just in terms of the style that we played and we never used to speak about opposing players at all but whenever we would play against Eden Hazard the team would say well we've got to play against Eden Hazard tomorrow. And that was remarkable considering some of the players that we had. And that was on you, right, as well? Not only did you talk about it, he was probably on your side.

I mean, he's just everywhere. He was just everywhere. He would pop up on the left in the middle, in between you and me and the centre back. And it was just—

Hell.

Yeah. He was just so good. He single-handedly won the league for them in 2015, I think.

You've played under several different managers. Who was the worst?

I would have to say my least favorite who I felt I least connected with was Alan Pardew.

How long did he last? Five, four, five months. Did you push him?

What's the most lavish purchase you've made?

So is there like a house included in that or not? That's not lavish. Let's say luxury. Like a luxury. Luxury. Highly liquid asset that you're like.

Yeah. Oh, that's a good one. That is a good one. I don't think I've ever been asked that. Probably my car that I bought when I got to Miami.

What is it?

It's a Defender.

How much was it?

It was jazzed up. Yeah, you got it. Yeah, and I couldn't get… I couldn't get a lease because I don't have any credit.

How much was that?

It was $140,000.

Dollars? It's not that bad.

Yeah, I wasn't into…

What about experience? Because you said you love experiences. Like most lavish experience. And that's probably like giving you some amazing—

Yeah, I used to go away on holidays and take my brother and friends and stuff. And we would just kind of— I would say, yeah, I would say holidays.

Is that one of the nicest things about being a professional, when you can do that with your family? You give an experience that you're just not going to have anywhere else.

Yes. That was the biggest— I felt that for me that was the biggest thing that I could best thing that I could spend my money on.

Nice. Yeah. Yeah. Kieran, this has been such a joy to do. Leaving on the best thing to spend my money on, that's a great way to end it. Thank you so much for being so open and fantastic with us.

Yeah. It's been a pleasure. Absolute pleasure. Thanks for having me on.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反常的洞察：他真正的"投资组合"不是比特币，而是"被嘲笑的勇气"
大家都以为这是一个关于"足球运动员押注比特币"的故事——一个金融决策的故事。但仔细听 Kieran 的叙述，你会发现他真正反复获利的资产，从来不是 BTC，而是**"主动靠近被群嘲的人"**的能力。
- Hal Robson-Khanu 在更衣室里被当成笑话——别人嘲笑他每天捧着笔记本电脑——而 Kieran 走过去问他在干什么。 - 他承认转会 West Brom 的一个真实动机，是为了离 Hal 更近。这是一个英超球员把"接触一个被嘲笑的队友"列为转会理由——这本身就是反常识的。 - 后来他在 Inter Miami 的圈层（Pomp、Balaji、Saylor）也是因为做了一件"在传统足球世界看起来很蠢"的事——把一半工资换成 BTC——而被打开的。
换句话说，他的真实 alpha 不是技术分析，而是**对"社会性尴尬"具有异常高的耐受度**。在更衣室文化里，被嘲笑是最大的隐性税；他能不交这笔税，所以复利在他这里发生了。
## 二、跨领域连接：足球赛程压缩 = 信息时代的"快餐化注意力"
他说

item.title: [a16z Deep Dives] Why AI Agents Need Context | Deep Dives with a16z
item.dc:creator: a16z Deep Dives
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、数据引力是虚构的，但虚构机制比真相更耐人寻味

George 抛出一个反共识论断："data gravity is completely fake"。他给出的证据是 Fivetran 在 AWS/GCP 上的流量面板：7000 多个客户，真实流动量小得惊人——因为做的是变更数据捕获 (CDC)，而非每日整库复制。

但更值得停下来想的是：为什么"数据引力"这个隐喻能流行十年？ 因为它把工程懒惰合法化了。早期数据管道每天午夜把整库复制一遍，造成读放大幻觉，于是工程师把自己代码的笨拙感受错认成数据的物理属性。这是一种"基础设施隐喻反映工程实践"的少见案例：物理学的引力是质量内禀属性，数据"引力"则是劣质模式制造出来的幻象。一个不易察觉的推论——当我们说某物有"引力"时，往往是在掩饰我们没看懂它真实的流动结构。

二、SAP 封锁 API，本质是对自身价值的招供

表面上看，SAP / Salesforce / Slack 关闭 Agent 访问是在保护资产。但博弈论上换个角度：封锁的那一刻，他们承认了一件事——自己的 UI、业务逻辑、工作流不足以独立产生溢价，必须靠"守门"才有价值。

这跟一家餐厅强迫客人只能通过自家 App 点餐如出一辙——这种行为不是自信，而是恐惧外卖平台暴露自己食物的同质化。George 推出的 opendatainfrastructure.com 评级把这种"封锁=弱势信号"的博弈论变成可对照、可比较的公开排行榜，相当于把企业软件的"诚实测试"做成了公共物品。一个意外的预言：未来五年企业软件的护城河叙事，会从"功能广度"悄悄转向"是否敢于敞开数据"——而后者恰恰是前者的悖论性证伪。

三、三十年的反爬虫战争，意外成了 Agent 最好的训练场

Martin 解释他为什么不用 headless 浏览器而是给 Agent 一台 Mac Mini：headless 触发反爬，iMessage 没 API，完整 UI 反而更稳。

这里藏着一个令人毛骨悚然的逆转：人类花了三十年加固"防止脚本行为"的 UI，现在反过来要训练 Agent 去 LARP 成人类来使用这些 UI。这与生物学的"红皇后假设"（捕食者与猎物共同进化）几乎完美对应——防御机制不是消灭攻击者，而是塑造下一代攻击者的形态。所以最准确的说法不是"Agent 替代 API"，而是"Agent 是被迫穿上人皮的 API"。这也解释了为什么 Martin 与 George 在"5 年后 Agent 主要通过 API 还是 UI"的赌局上意见分裂——两人都没说错，但暗含一个更深问题：当 Agent 的人皮训练数据足够多，"人类 UI"这层防御还有没有继续存在的理由？

四、AI 真正颠覆的不是创意，而是"长尾烦琐的可压缩性"

George 反复说 Fivetran 的诀窍是 "the trick is there is no trick"——750 个连接器，每个都有自己的怪癖，全靠"修一次 bug，所有客户受益"的经济学撑起来。AI Coding Agent 在他口中是"无限的初级工程师"，恰好擅长这种没有创意但需要广度的工作。

这与最初的 AI 替代叙事完全相反——AI 不在替代 CEO、设计师、战略家，而在替代"漫长的运维耐心"。一个反直觉的推论：未来护城河最深的公司，是那些核心业务就是"烦琐宽度"的公司，因为只有他们攒下了把"漫长烦琐"翻译成 AI 训练目标所需的领域怪癖知识。一个跨域连接：这跟 19 世纪保险精算师的崛起异曲同工——精算师不是因为聪明而值钱，而是因为他们的工作是别人不愿意承担的"细节累积"，一旦数学工具成熟，这些公司就拿走了整个金融基础设施。

五、计价单位的迁移：从"席位"到"角色"

George 顺口提到 Agent 不需要个体身份，只需要"角色"——一个 PM Bot 可以做 1000 个人的事，却只占一个 role identity。

这听起来只是 SAS 厂商的威胁。但计价单位的变化才是真正的暗流。互联网计量从 "page view" → "session" → "engaged minute" 的每次迁移，都伴随价值的剧烈再分配（Google、Facebook、TikTok 各自踩中一次）。SaaS 现在站在同样的拐点：seat → role → outcome？谁先定义新的计量学，谁就重写定价权。下一个真正的 SaaS 颠覆者，可能不是带来新功能的公司，而是带来新"消耗单位"的公司。

六、CEO 私下写 nano-agent，是一个隐秘的领先指标

George 一边管着 7000 客户的上市公司，一边在搓微型 OLTP 数据库、网球队管理 Bot、机器视觉应用。Martin 也在用 Nano Claude、自己的 Mac Mini Agent。表面看是技术 CEO 的不务正业，但若把它当作宏观信号：当大型组织的 CEO 开始能"业余地"构建可用软件，意味着"业余构建"的能力曲线已经穿过了某个临界点。

对照 2009–2012 年 CEO 开始上社交媒体，一开始也被视为不务正业，五年后没有 CEO 敢不用。可以大胆预测：未来 3–5 年，企业内会浮现一种新的暗物质——"CEO 私人 Agent 团队"。他们绕过组织流程、绕过 IT 审批、绕过年度预算，直接放大某个高管的认知与行动半径。组织自身的治理框架还没意识到这层影响力倍增器已经在运行。George 自己说出的 "Claude psychosis"——被 Agent 吸进去出不来——既是个人风险，也是这股暗流真实存在的副作用证据。

七、Postgres 是好的，但被"想象冻结"诅咒了

George 最大的彩蛋是："Postgres is bad actually，本科生写的存储引擎在很多方面比 Postgres 的 heap 更好。"

这听上去像挑衅，但揭示了一个反复出现的系统模式：成功的基础设施会进入"想象冻结"状态——人们停止思考替代品的可能，技术债以"事实标准"的形式被合法化。生物演化里叫"演化锁死"：物种的成功反而限制了进一步演化。AI 时代的特殊机会就在于此——当编码成本骤降，那些"想象冻结"的领域（数据库、操作系统、文件格式、HTTP 协议本身？）将首次出现真正的替代品挑战。George 在 Fivetran 之外搓 SQLite-on-S3 的小玩具，可能正是无意中卡在这条断层上。最有价值的 AI 应用，未必在新的领域里，而在那些被"我们以为这就是终态"困住的旧领域里。

📝 转录文本

开场预告（Cold Open）

There is a new reason to have all your data in one place which is AI agents need context. If you don't do that then it's sort of like using chat GBT from before chat GBT was connected to the internet.

Postgress contrary to popular belief is very old technology. It is not a good database simply because it was written a long time ago. It has a lot of technical debt.

Satia has said that there's going to be the collapse of SAS. Do you think the SAS apocalypse is a thing and we're going to see a massive shift?

The bigger threat is that AI native companies will just zoom and catch up to the established incumbents and maybe be better.

Like we'll actually have an HR and that HR team will onboard AIS as they come. They'll be part of teams. They'll join the Slack and in that world these aren't software. That's actually more seats, more consumption of software. And so do you think that for enterprise agents we're moving more to these you treat them like humans or do you think that that's too far?

嘉宾介绍：Fivetran 与 George Fraser

Um, so our guest today is George Frasier who is the CEO of Fiverr. Fiverr announced the merger uh with DBT. So maybe to start just give a quick overview of what Fiverr does.

So Fiverr, we've been around for a while. Uh we've been around since uh 2013. Had customers since 2015. Uh

2013 years.

Yeah, exactly. I've been doing this long enough that uh the slide about the uh the past state is the same in in my in my own uh slides is the same slide as the future state from [laughter] when I started.

Uh but uh what Fiverr does uh is we uh help our uh customers get all of their data from all their systems like Salesforce, Netswuite, uh all their SAS tools, their own databases into one place.

Um getting all your data in one place, it's not a new thing. Businesses have had the need to do this since, you know, filing cabinets. Um the the primary reason historically that people used Fiverr to get all their data in one place was to do business intelligence — was to build reports about things like what's your revenue, um what's going on with your sales team, what are we forecasting for this quarter, all those great things.

Uh and now there is a new reason to have all your data in one place which is if you want to use AI agents in business, AI agents need context. And it turns out that the same data foundations that work well for business intelligence and reporting — with some additions and some modifications — actually can work really well for AI agents as well.

AI 时代的数据行业演化

I mean talk about a sector of the industry which is under a lot of change because of AI. So maybe can you give like a high-level overview of how it is evolving, what are some of the considerations about, you know, the shifts in data, and in particular like we're seeing a lot of changes — how vendors view their own data, how the big labs use data. So just talk a bit about what the industry is.

The thing about data in the context of business is — it is always born somewhere else. It's always born in systems of record like Salesforce, like Workday, like SAP. And even if it's your own applications data — if you're a software company and you run your own database, the data is born in that database.

And since, as I said, a time in memorial, uh, businesses have had the need for internal use to centralize a copy of all their data in another location. It doesn't work to just go and do all of your reporting and ask all your questions in each system individually. Some kinds of questions require you to look across the entire system. And so that is not new.

Um however uh these AI agents are new. And there has been a in the last year a reaction which really really started with the stock market. Um, as we saw the SAS apocalypse happen and as we saw the stock prices of all of these systems of record that I'm talking about plummet, um, people viewed them as under threat from AIS.

We have seen uh, some of these companies start to think that a great strategy for dealing with AI might be to lock it out [gasps] — and to uh, say "your data is our data now and you can't take it elsewhere. Uh, and if you want to use AI on it, you have to use the AI tools that we provide."

Uh, notably, just a couple weeks ago, SAP announced a new uh, API policy that literally said uh, all AI agent access was banned except specifically except in a way specifically approved by SAP. Now, if you're an SAP user, don't panic. This is just a policy. You have contracts with SAP, those uh are authoritative as to what you are and are allowed to do. So, don't overreact to these policy memos, but it just shows um how extreme the reaction of some of these companies has been.

拆解：SAS 厂商在怕什么？

I just want to tease this apart because I think there's a lot of confusion on what exactly is going on, right? So, this is locking down access to the data that an agent would use instead of an app, right? It's not

access to data because you're going to train your own model.

That's right. And do you so I I'll tell you I

very few people are in the business of training their own models. Most people when they want to access their own data in their own systems of record — even if those systems of record are managed by vendors — um they are using it for context. They're using it in order to ask and answer questions about what's going on in their business.

So the the concern is my SAS app has less value as an interface because now the agents can access the data directly and basically perform the same functions the SAS app was before. Is that the concern?

I think there are many concerns. I think that

can you actually just straw man the set of concerns because like this is one of the biggest reactions I've seen in the industry in a very long time and I'm kind of trying to come to grips with what the the actual worry is.

I think uh people are worried that their uh that their systems that they've spent many years building will simply be less valuable in a world where uh their users are no longer humans but their agents.

Um I think they're worried that um but why isn't this just another seat? Like I mean it seems like — I mean arguably this is positive because there's going to be more consumers of

seats. Agents don't need as many individual identities. When you have AI agents accessing systems, you really you just need roles. You don't need you don't need the same granularity of users.

Uh if you have uh many product managers, each will have their own identity in a system. But if you have a product manager bot, you really might just have one role that it uses. And it has a it might have a single identity and yet do the work of hundreds or thousands of people. So there's not an easy answer like that.

Um furthermore, these companies have a history of having open APIs. Open APIs are a good thing. If these companies did not have open APIs, they would have been consigned to the dust bin of legacy SAS decades ago. I mean this is a thing that happened in the '9s, right? The evolution of open APIs. Uh and um their customers have been using them and depending on them for decades and those same APIs are the primary target of AI agents. So it is very hard to differentiate uh whether the users are accessing the APIs in the same way that they always have been or whether they are accessing them in agentic ways that may substitute for human workflows.

这只是 90 年代讨论的重演？

I'm going to keep poking on this cuz I still think it's not a real concern. So let me make another bad argument. So let's say you had opened up all your APIs in the 90s, which is the case. Like why couldn't I just write a procedural um uh app which is my own version of the SAS and therefore also disintermediate your SAS? Like why why why are agents somehow different than me just writing my own software or my own dashboard?

Maybe they're not. Um this may all be kind of much a do about nothing. I I think it's foolish for them to close down their APIs. So you're putting me in a weird position.

No, I I do. I do, too.

of trying to [laughter] trying to defend a position that I think is is stupid.

Yeah. Yeah.

So I I think you know a lot of these threats are not new — like "well maybe they'll use programmatic access and thereby use less seats. Maybe they'll move some functionality to their own interfaces." I mean that is a real thing that has been happening.

I just want to let you know I I am old enough to remember these discussions in the '90s. Like the rhetoric was exactly the same. The reaction exact. "We could never open up APIs. We can never like have them do this because they're going to disintermediate us." And it just turns out that if you're buying into a business process — like the operational flow of something like — like that is set up by the company that you're buying it from. Like Salesforce knows how to run the Salesforce. And so whether it's an agent that's consuming it or SAS, I would argue that like there's still the value there.

I completely agree and I will point out another piece of evidence for that claim which is if you look at the budgets of real companies that are heavy consumers of software, they spend 5 to 10% of headcount on software.

Software costs are immaterial in the grand scheme of things. Software compared to everything else a typical business is so cheap. The idea that they're going to use AI to value engineer the number of seats they have on Slack or something is ridiculous. Uh they're going to use AI to go make their business work better in whatever it is that they

they're actually [laughter] they're not trying to take that, you know, 5% software spend and turn it into four and a half. That that is not the highest best of use of a

I mean I mean famously all the big AI labs including — I mean and you know Andre and Horowits — and we're all very heavy users of AI like still use these these SAS tools. And so like four years into this we don't have a lot of evidence that like

as do we — as do OpenAI and Anthropic, exactly — who are both Fiverr customers, and we replicate lots of data from these very SAS tools on their behalf into their data links. So uh if they're still using them [laughter] Yeah. Yeah. Right.

Uh is it do we really think the company of the future is not going to be

Right. Right.

这对客户是坏事——以及给 CIO 的建议

But one thing we can both agree on is — this is bad for customers, right? Like this kind of locking down the the APIs is bad for customers. So maybe talk through like (a) and why it's bad, which may just be obvious — and then (b) kind of your recommendation for how to manage that, like assuming that this is happening industrywide.

So the reason it's bad for customers — anytime vendors put up walls and try to um regulate data access — is that uh you need to have all your data in one place in order to do meaningful reporting, in order to understand just what the heck is going on in your business, and in order for AI agents to work in the context of business.

If you don't do that then it's sort of like using chat GBT from before chat GBT was connected to the internet. Uh if you used it back then you remember it used to have this knowledge cut off and it would tell you "I can only answer questions that happened by, you know, in my training window six months ago." Yeah. "Six months ago was when I was trained. I don't know anything after that because I'm not connected to the internet."

That is what using today's AIS is like if you're using them in a business context and they don't have access to your business data. And uh so it's very important for every company who wants to do things with data to create their own data platform where they have a copy of all of their own data and it's being kept continuously up to date. And anytime vendors start putting up barriers, it just makes it harder to uh to get that done. And the customers will still do it. They'll just work around these barriers at great cost and complexity.

You know, one of my favorite things that you've done as a company to educate the customers on this is this benchmark. Um, can you maybe talk through what that is?

Well, we have a website. Is that what you're talking about? Open data infrastructure.

Open data infrastructure.com.

Open data infrastructure.com.

It's a benchmark — like it does scoring, right?

Yeah. So, we score — the list is growing. We're trying to make it as long as possible. We score uh as many vendors as we've been able to to catalog uh so far on their data access policies. So we basically score them on whether uh they try to charge egress charges, whether they try to make you pay for getting your own data out, whether they um uh whether they make it impossible to get a complete copy of your data cuz some vendors will do that or they'll make it just very difficult. Um and whether they whether they have uh terms of use restrictions on accessing your own data. So, we have — there's a big grid on there and it rates.

Are you are you comfortable saying who the worst offenders are?

Well,

or should you just point people

just just go to the website and read it. It's all very fact-based and very uh well well evidenced. I mean, the worst offenders historically have been uh SAP has always been really bad.

I mean, even when I was running a large business, I mean, that was always

It's interesting because they were getting better. And I've sensed in the last few years that there's sort of two camps within SAP. One of whom who regards it as "it's the customer's data. The customers got to be able to do what they need to do with their own data." And then there's sort of the old guard who views it as "SAP's data and you'll do with it what what we tell you to."

Um and uh and and then you know historically Salesforce has been really good with the exception of Slack where they are terrible.

Yeah, I know. [laughter]

Uh but uh they've started to get squirly about this. So it's it's a moving target. I am hopeful that this is merely a brief flirtation with closed data by most of these vendors and they will realize this is not a good idea — that they are at war with their own customers and it's not even going to work anyway — and we will go back to the trend towards ever more openness.

You just answered my next question but I'm asking anyways which is — like do you feel like this is just like a repeat of the open data API? It'll resolve quickly like, you know, we always kind of go through this soul-searching and then we will resolve back to where we were which is like open data is the right thing. I do — do you think that's the path or do you think it's different this time?

Yes. I think it will be the same. That's my prediction. It will be the same path. As vendors will discover that they cannot provide inside their own platform a solution to every data problem that their customers have, because they are simply so diverse. And instead they simply create a mechanism for the customers to to replicate it to their to their data platform of choice and and do with it what they will.

And even if they charge fees for that, it's not the end of the world. On open data infrastructure.com, you really get yellow for charging fees. 数据引力是真的吗？

You know, this is probably related to this notion of of of, you know, or this belief in data gravity. And I mean, one thing that I've loved working with you over the years is like you're exceedingly smart and you're exceedingly contrarian. Uh I I think data gravity is completely fake. Uh I [laughter] am the only person who thinks this.

I think you're the only person.

If you want if you want to see —

maybe describe what data gravity means. People use the term a lot but like I don't think they understand the implications.

Data gravity is the idea that uh business data is so large that it's very expensive to move around because of egress charges uh of cloud vendors. Um and uh if you want to see — and that therefore it's very important that you choose a physical region in the world where all your data is going to live in a specific uh region of a specific cloud and then you build all of your data consuming services in that same location, or you can partition

because essays —

well this is — this is a — the term data gravity does get used to mean multiple things. This is this is one particular

incarnation of the idea of data gravity and this is the one that I am saying is — pick that egress charges are so important.

Um and if you want to see evidence against this, come look at the networking dashboard of Fiverr — various AWS and GCP accounts. You will be astonished. Despite replicating uh huge data sets for uh thousands of companies — we have uh 7,000 customers of size and thousands more little ones — um the the the amount of data being moved at any given time is tiny.

And the reason is that we're doing change data capture. You can have a huge data set, but if you just replicate the changes, the changes are always much smaller than people think.

And I and I think that a lot of this idea of data gravity came from dumb data pipelines that people wrote where they would copy their entire company's data sets out of their database every day, once a day at midnight. And so they just had this crazy read amplification. You know, they were just repeatedly copying the same data over and over and it gave them the impression that they had so much data, but they really don't.

Maybe a good argument not to roll your own at these things anyways.

Yes. Yes. This is what happens when people roll their own data pipelines is uh they fall back on these patterns that are easy to get right — like that that pattern I just described. It's extremely easy to get a correct replica just by copying it all over and over. Um, but very expensive to operate in the long run.

Yeah, I think uh number one, you should insist on having a copy of all of your own company data in a data lake that you control. Uh don't let go of that for any vendor.

Um you have a lot of leverage. Uh these vendors actually have a lot of obligations to let you do that. Uh the reason why they get away with um blocking people is simply because people don't fight. Uh so pick fights and uh write it into your um into your — if you have big contracts with vendors and you're redlining your MSAs — uh write language uh guaranteeing your own data access into those MSAs.

Are you are you seeing those show up now? Actually I hadn't even considered that.

We have model language on open data infrastructure.com that we recommend you incorporate into your MSAs. Uh, and even if you don't get it, just by asking for it, you are sending uh a signal. So, you know, if you if it's a $10,000 contract, don't don't do it. Uh, [laughter] but if you're if you're uh signing, you know, $500 million contracts, insist on data access in your MSA. Uh so their their data their data platforms look very typical. And I think um this is a very important message if you are thinking about data foundations for AI. Do not make the mistake of thinking you need to build some exotic new system as a data foundation for AI. The right data foundation for AI is probably the one you already have. If you have a reasonably modern uh data platform — something like Snowflake or Databricks or BigQuery or maybe even you have transition to an Iceberg data lake with those compute systems running on top — um that is a great foundation for your context for AI as well.

No, I think about that a lot and I make, you know, you got to you got to like keep it at bay. Uh because it it it is like uh you know, there there is a danger of claude psychosis, which is a term that I I love, but you can just get sucked in.

Uh but you know, I have a lot of time. I don't have any kids so I have a lot of [laughter]

I mean people with children will will say it's like uh it's like having a whole another uh self uh [laughter]

Yeah. Yes.

You don't trust me and I do. Yeah. Yeah.

反思练习：如果我是新来的 CEO，会立刻改什么？

I mean, one thing I do I do really appreciate about um you as an executive assist, you as a founder is you're actually quite reflective. And one of the mental exercises that you've been doing as long as I've known you was like pretending if you were a new CEO brought in by the board to fix Fiverr, like what would you immediately unwind? Which, by the way, always happens when you bring in a CEO. And I know you kind of do this mental exercise. So, is there anything recently that you've thought like, "boy, if I was brought in to like run things, I would change this"?

Many things. I — that's an exercise I I do regularly. I'm trying to think of a recent example. I mean, a not so recent example is I did do some things to try to simplify pricing. I I think those have been successful. They were painful for the company because they mostly cut prices for small uh customers.

Yeah.

Um, another exercise I do is I ask myself, what should other CEOs do? It's hard. It's a it's a trick to get yourself to to do things that are big and scary.

Yeah.

Uh so in the DBT merger, you know, when when I was reflecting on that uh on whether that was a thing we should seriously consider, one of the tricks I use is I asked myself, "what should Sridhar do?" Sridhar is the CEO of Snowflake who we've worked with for a long time. It's a good mental exercise. And then I just go do that [laughter] and that was like a clear answer in my mind — was like merge with DBT. Absolutely.

By that — by that framework, even though it seems very big and scary, when you imagine "is this a good idea for someone else?" uh then you can kind of get there.

担忧与机会

Yeah. Maybe just kind of a softball cliche final question. Um but you know you do have like the unique perspective of managing a CEO during this transition. So, first, what are you most kind of existentially worried about? And second, what are you most excited about?

Well, I think the thing I most worry about is just, you know, that the uh at some point the coding agents will get so good at writing connectors that people will just shift to DIY. Um, I think that's a real threat uh to Fiverr.

I think some different businesses are more and less threatened by like "maybe the customers will just vibe code it themselves." That is a thing I worry about with Fiverr. And um we uh we will we will find a way to thrive in that world if we get there and we we um we will provide the tools that you use to do that if that indeed becomes possible — even if it comes at a short-term cost to ourselves. Um but I do worry a lot about that.

Uh and then the the biggest opportunity um I think is that AI is just a whole new set of things to do with data. The need for getting all your data in one place, organizing it, um is so much greater now than ever before. Um I think that there's a whole set of tools that people are going to need on the other side of that data platform. And I think we — and especially we and DBT — are perfectly positioned to provide them.

Amazing. Well, it's always a pleasure to have you, George. Thanks for coming.

Good to see you, Martine.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、数据引力是虚构的，但虚构机制比真相更耐人寻味
George 抛出一个反共识论断："data gravity is completely fake"。他给出的证据是 Fivetran 在 AWS/GCP 上的流量面板：7000 多个客户，真实流动量小得惊人——因为做的是变更数据捕获 (CDC)，而非每日整库复制。
但更值得停下来想的是：**为什么"数据引力"这个隐喻能流行十年？** 因为它把工程懒惰合法化了。早期数据管道每天午夜把整库复制一遍，造成读放大幻觉，于是工程师把自己代码的笨拙感受错认成数据的物理属性。这是一种"基础设施隐喻反映工程实践"的少见案例：物理学的引力是质量内禀属性，数据"引力"则是劣质模式制造出来的幻象。一个不易察觉的推论——**当我们说某物有"引力"时，往往是在掩饰我们没看懂它真实的流动结构**。
## 二、SAP 封锁 API，本质是对自身价值的招供
表面上看，SAP / Salesforce / Slack 关闭 Agent 访问是在保护资产。但博弈论上换个角度：**封锁的那一刻，他们承认了一件事——自己的 UI、业务逻辑、工作流不足以独立产生

item.title: [Lex Clips] Dark energy explained by physicist | Don Lincoln and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：「常数」其实在偷偷膨胀

视频里有一个被绝大多数科普忽略的细节——"暗能量是常数"这句话本身是个语言陷阱。Don Lincoln 指出：所谓"常数"指的是密度恒定，而不是总量恒定。这意味着随着宇宙膨胀，暗能量的总量在悄悄增加——空间越大，凭空"长"出来的能量越多。

这违反了我们日常对"守恒"的所有直觉。普通物质遵循"摊薄"逻辑（体积↑→密度↓），暗能量却遵循"复制"逻辑（体积↑→总量↑，密度不变）。换句话说，宇宙不是一个封闭系统，它在自我增殖。这是当代物理学最大的"会计丑闻"——能量守恒在宇宙学尺度上被默默搁置，而几乎没人在公众讨论中提起。

二、跨领域连接：暗能量、区块链与「涌现式记账」

这里有一个让我着迷的隐喻：Lincoln 描述的"空间量子像沙丘里新出现的沙粒"——每一颗都自带一份固定的能量。这与区块链的工作机制惊人地同构：

  • 每一个新区块（=空间量子）被铸造出来时，都自带固定数量的代币（=暗能量份额）。
  • 系统的总价值随时间增长，但单位铸造率（密度）保持恒定。
  • 没有"中心账本"，能量/价值是在每次"扩展事件"中涌现式注入的。

这不是一个浪漫的类比，而是一个结构性的同构——两者都是"在拓扑扩张中持续注入恒定密度信息"的系统。如果你接受这个视角，那么暗能量问题就从"宇宙里多了什么"变成了"宇宙是怎么记账的"。这把物理学的本体论问题转译成了信息论的协议问题。

三、隐藏的方法论真相：物理学家正在"借量子引力之刀，杀空间连续性之牛"

视频结尾提到的"量子纠缠引力实验"听起来像个独立的话题，但它其实是整段对话的隐秘伏笔。注意 Lincoln 的逻辑链： - 如果引力被证明是量子化的 → 理论家会"关掉一整个房间的灯"（连续引力理论集体出局）→ 注意力转向量子空间 → 暗能量"空间气泡"模型获得合法性。

这是一种学术界罕见公开承认的"曲线救国"策略：你不需要直接证明你的疯狂理论，你只需要摧毁它的所有竞争者所栖息的概念基础。

更出人意料的是：这个策略与进化生物学中的"生态位清空"机制完全一致——恐龙灭绝后哺乳动物才得以辐射。物理学的范式革命不是通过证明新理论赢得的，而是通过让旧理论赖以生存的"概念生态位"消失而完成的。库恩的"科学革命"在这里以一种比他描述更冷酷的方式上演：不是说服，而是清场。

一个对你的提问：如果暗能量真的是"空间量子在出生时自带的能量配额"，那么宇宙的"创世"就不是一次性事件，而是每个普朗克瞬间都在重新发生的微型大爆炸。这意味着——你愿意接受"创世"是一个进行时动词，而不是一个过去时名词吗？

📝 转录文本

暗能量与宇宙的深远未来

Lex Fridman: One of the reasons dark energy is super interesting is it kind of gives us a mechanism by which we can talk about the deep future of the universe. I mean, we have observations about the expansion of the universe, but it's also giving us the mechanism of that, right? So we can talk about any weirdness — any good model we have that captures some of the weirdness of dark energy might give us insights about how this thing ends, how the universe… about the deep future of the universe, right?

Don Lincoln: Absolutely. As it stands right now, if dark energy is real — and who knows, you know, if it's real exactly as we've measured it — then as the universe gets bigger and bigger, dark energy becomes a bigger and bigger component of the energy balance of the universe, and it takes over and it drives the continued accelerated expansion of the universe.

And if dark energy gets lower, for some reason that we don't understand — maybe it changes over time, gets smaller — that could change things. If it gets bigger, it could change things. That is one of the big open questions: whether it's constant over time or not.

一项新测量的暗示

There has been a recent measurement that suggests that dark energy is getting smaller. However, that is a new measurement, not confirmed, blah blah blah blah blah — nobody should believe it. But it's a hint that maybe it's changing, which is kind of cool in itself, because the current bias — and until recently — is that dark energy is constant.

「常数」的真正含义：密度，而不是总量

Now, I want to be super careful, because it's misleading. People say dark energy is constant. Dark energy is a density. Now think about that. You have a certain density — let's start with that. Then the universe expands. So energy is volume times density. If the universe gets bigger and the density is constant, that means dark energy is increasing. It's not just increasing as a fraction and overwhelming ordinary matter — but ordinary matter, as the universe expands, its density decreases, because it's constant and the volume gets bigger, so the density drops.

Dark energy, until recently, is thought to be constant density. So that's what's implied when you say "constant" — you say constant density, which means it's actually increasing, because space is increasing. The size of space is increasing. And so that's a weirdness.

这把我们带向空间的本质

And that then ties in to the nature of space. Why does that tie into the nature of space? Well, because if dark energy is a field in space, if you increase the volume, you would think the energy density would drop.

But if space is increasing, and space is quantized — and I don't know if it is — then maybe what's happening is space isn't stretching, but like little space particles are appearing as the space… you know, there's like bubbles of space appearing, and each bubble contains a certain amount of dark energy. And so therefore that would give you a sense that dark energy is a property of space rather than a field in space.

But that's all very hand-wavy, guess-worky stuff.

这是「真实存在的东西」吗？

Lex Fridman: So if you had to bet all your money — is dark energy like a real — [snorts] — physical… What does that even mean? A thing that exists, versus is this just a renaming of the cosmological constant?

Don Lincoln: Unfortunately, I think it's both. [laughter] Well, I mean, it is. It's describing a reality, but it's also maybe telling us something about space — literally a property of space.

Yeah, it's — I mean, that's kind of what it looks like. Given that it seems to be constant density, that seems to me… Now, this is not something anybody should believe. Please, nobody believe this. But it seems to me that this is leading towards the idea that:

  • A, it's a property of space.
  • B, space is quantized.
  • C, as space is expanding, little quanta of space are appearing.
  • D, each one of those quanta has a certain amount of energy associated with it.

And that would kind of explain the constant density. Now please — that's not… nobody accepts that. This is just nonsense.

沙丘里的新沙粒

Lex Fridman: But a lot of the stuff that you just said is experimentally — probably experimentally testable. You can probably construct it. Oh, it's the bubbles of —

Don Lincoln: Well, finding out the bubbles of space — but those quanta, conceivably, are Planck-size bubbles.

Yeah, the quanta. Well, they'd be quanta of space. I mean, the idea is, you know, you look at a sand dune and it looks smooth and continuous, but you can see individual grains of sand, right? And so what this is saying is, as this dune expands, new grains of sand are appearing, and each one of them is a quantum of space.

未来的实验：量子纠缠中的引力

Lex Fridman: So what kind of experiments can we do in the coming decades or centuries to understand dark energy better?

Don Lincoln: I mean, people have been talking about quantum entanglement of gravity. In standard quantum mechanics, a particle can be in two places at the same time. All right. So now you have two particles. So this particle can be in two places at the same time. This particle can be in two places at the same time. And you put them near one another — well, if they're close to each other, there's a certain gravitational force. If they're far apart, there's a certain… and if one is close and one is far, you have another one.

You can calculate the effects of gravity having to do with quantum entangled particles being in two places. And people are talking about doing this and trying to see if, in doing such a measurement, they might be able to definitively determine whether gravity is a quantum phenomenon or a continuous phenomenon.

一项即将到来的判决性测量

And that is potentially a measurement that could be done soon-ish, because the technologies inherent in all of this recent work on quantum mechanics is allowing people to be able to make instrumentation that might be precise enough to do this measurement.

Now, this will not tell us what quantum gravity is. It will not tell us anything — but it will tell us that gravity is quantized. And just knowing that — well, for one thing, it shuts out a whole realm of continuous gravity, and the theoretical community turns its attention: "forget this stuff and think over here."

Now, that doesn't tell you that space is quantized, but it tells you that gravity is quantized — if it bears out. So, and if gravity is quantized, then people will start thinking more about space being quantized.

观看原视频 →

这违反了我们日常对"守恒"的所有直觉。普通物质遵循"摊薄"逻辑（体积↑→密度↓），暗能量却遵循"复制"逻辑（体积↑→总量↑，密度不变）。换句话说，**宇宙不是一个封闭系统，它在自我增殖**。这是当代物理学最大的"会计丑闻"——能量守恒在宇宙学尺度上被默默搁置，而几乎没人在公众讨论中提起。

item.title: [Sequoia Capital] Knowing What Your Customers Want, All the Time: Listen Labs' Alfred Wahlforss
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Capital
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：Listen Labs 不是"更便宜的市场调研"，而是"更贵的市场调研"

表面叙事很诱人——AI 替代人工、规模化降本、Bain/McKinsey 颤抖。但 Alfred 自己泄露了真相："我们能向客户收取数十万美元，去跟八个国家的 20 位医生交谈。"这不是消费级 SaaS 的下沉故事，而是让"过去做不到的事"变得可做——而非让"做得到的事"变得便宜。

这里有个让人意外的对照：Listen Labs 的真正竞争对手不是 Qualtrics，而是"不存在"。是那些公司本来就不会做的研究、那些根本不会被问出来的问题、那些被默认为"不值得调查"的 99% 决策。当你把单位访谈成本降到接近零，需求曲线下方那片巨大的灰色三角形（消费者剩余）就被照亮了——而这片灰色，过去甚至没有名字。

二、隐藏的护城河不在模型，而在 Eval：一个跨领域的暗线

Alfred 说漏了一句最关键的话："我们的 eval 从 20% 爬到了 85%。然后我们设计了一个更难的 eval，现在又回到 20%。"

垂直 AI 公司的真正护城河不是数据、不是模型，而是"定义难题的能力"。这与一个完全不相关的领域惊人地同构——奥数命题人。一道题的价值不在解出它，而在出题人定义了什么叫"会做"。Listen Labs 实际上在做的是：每当模型变强一点，他们就重新定义"什么叫好的访谈"，把目标线再往前推 65 个百分点。基础模型的进步对他们是燃料而非威胁，因为评价权握在自己手上。

这其实是 SAT、米其林、ISO、信用评分等"标准制定型"业务的隐藏血脉——谁定义衡量标准，谁就锁定行业。Listen Labs 看起来在做问卷，本质上在做"什么叫一份好访谈"的认证机构。

三、最让人脊背发凉的悖论：AI 比人类更容易获得人类的真心话

"人们对 AI 更诚实"——Alfred 轻描淡写地说出这句话，但它的含义是核弹级的。

传统智慧（包括所有心理治疗师的训练）都建立在一个假设上：信任来自人与人之间的非语言共鸣。Listen Labs 的数据正在拆解这个假设：当一方变成"非判断性实体"时，被访者更愿意暴露脆弱。这与一个看似无关的现象同构——忏悔室里隔着木板的牧师为什么有效？因为匿名+非互惠的关系，反而让坦白变得安全。Listen Labs 重新发明了天主教忏悔，只是把神父换成了 GPT。

把这个洞察推到极致：人类的"真诚"原来是一种摩擦力。社会判断、共情压力、互惠义务……这些我们以为是真诚的前提，反而是真诚的阻碍。这意味着未来心理治疗、医患沟通、亲密关系，可能都将面临一个不安的问题：在 AI 面前展示的那个我，是不是比在人类面前的那个我更真实？

四、跨领域连接：Listen Labs 在悄悄重建"个人偏好的征信局"

注意 Alfred 说的这一段——某人在一次和球鞋无关的访谈里随口提到自己是 sneakerhead，这条信息被存进数据库，下次 Nike 发布新品时，他就成了被优先邀请的访谈对象。

这是 Equifax 的结构，但操作对象不是"还款记录"而是"偏好痕迹"。征信局的天才之处在于：你以为自己只是在和某家银行打交道，但你的每一次违约都成了"全行业可查询的资产"。Listen Labs 在做的事一模一样——你以为你只是在为 Sweet Green 做了一次访谈，但你的整个"消费人格"被沉淀成了跨品牌可调用的资源。

这暗示了一个 Alfred 没明说的终局：Listen Labs 的真正产品不是访谈，而是"人类偏好的可寻址化"。当他们说"目标是 10 亿人的 audience"，这不是用户规模数字，而是 Equifax-for-preference 的临界质量。一旦达到，所有想要触达任何细分人群的公司都必须经过他们——就像今天的银行无法绕开征信局。

五、一个最微妙的哲学反讽

Alfred 说：即便到了 AGI，人类仍然有价值，因为人类是不理性的。

仔细品这句话——它意味着，在一个理性能力被无限复制的世界里，非理性反而成了稀缺资源。人类从"决策者"被降级为"chaos generator"——我们存在的意义不再是想清楚，而是想不清楚；不再是做决定，而是产生那种 AI 无法预测的、突然对某个 TikTok 趋势着迷的混乱模式。

这与艺术史上一个经典反讽同构：摄影术发明后，绘画不再需要"画得像"，反而被解放去探索抽象。AGI 时代的人类，可能正在经历同样的角色重定义——我们最有价值的部分，是机器最算不出来的部分。Listen Labs 实际上在为这个新世界建立基础设施：一个专门捕获"人类不可预测性"的工业管道。

📝 转录文本

开场：愿景预告

Our goal is to get to a billion people in our audience and then to be able to stratify and know what exactly is this person an expert on. And it might be, you know, even something like sneakers, you have some people who are influencers and kind of early adopters. And if you're able to find that audience and interview them first, the insights are much more valuable. And we can learn across all of the interviews that we do. We build profiles of people as we do more interviews in the platform and then we can search and find the right person.

嘉宾介绍

Okay, today we're sitting down with Alfred Walforce, founder and CEO of Listen Labs. Listen is an AI first customer research platform that can run thousands of voice interviews simultaneously. You launched about a year ago and you now serve 20% of the Fortune 500 including iconic brands like Microsoft, Anthropic, Sweet Green, NBC and others. Um, and Constantine are very very excited to sit down with you today and talk about market research and how it's getting uh transformed with AI.

Yeah, thank you for having me.

Listen Labs 是什么：AI 访谈代理 + 受众网络 + 模拟预测

Maybe just to get started, so you are building an AI enabled platform that scales market research. What does that mean?

Yeah. So, we have this AI agent that can understand your customers better than you can. Uh, and the way we do that is by talking to them. So, to give you an example, you can ask a question like how can you improve curses on boarding and then listen will create an interview guide um which is an instructions for the agent to make the interviews and then we have an audience. We have 30 million participants. We can find pretty much anyone from an encologist to a software engineer and we'll go and actually talk to them and have hundreds of those interviews and then analyze the data, give you recommendations.

And now the final step that we're just launching in a couple months is simulation. So after you've done tens of thousands of interviews in the platform, can you predict how your customers will answer questions in the future? Put it another way, as we get closer to AGI, it will be easier to build things, but the hard part will know what to build and that's what we're building at Listen.

客户故事：Chubbies、Manscaped、Skims

Awesome. Do you have any favorite customer stories?

Um, yeah. So, Chubbies is one of our customers. They've been Yeah, they've been like one of our early customers.

What they use you for?

They use us for everything. So, a lot of marketing testing for testing shirts to understand um what products perform well and what doesn't. And one of my favorite examples is they discovered that chest hair interface really poorly with one of the materials they have. So, it's like really uncomfortable to wear one of their shirts and they changed the shirt and it became like radically more comfort comfortable. Um so, we saw you know the small things to the big things. Manscaped um changed their Super Bowl ad with insights from from Leen. So

never heard of that, but I'm not going to ask. Uh

Um yeah on on the modes I mean we have the clear modes which are the network effects on the panel where you have supply and demand dynamic. We also have um the network affects the data mode that as we do more interviews you gets better simulation and then the product is very sticky because you have all these interviews in your platform and you don't want to lose that you want to track things over time

but even like the simplest things I think in terms of product advantages like one of the first things that Brian Sher said one of our our partner

was that founders want to build something that's complex X, but customers want something that's stupid simple and it just works. They don't want to configure their own workflow. They don't want to sit and build a custom software. And just one example of this is creating the interview guide is really difficult. It's actually an academic subject

and it's one of the reasons why you have services firms because they know

what methodology to use if you want to understand pricing or brand perception these kind of things.

You don't want to lead the witness.

You don't want to lead the witness

and it's really hard to get that right. In the beginning, we just used the vanilla LM models and the customers would create the interviews, they would get the data back and then they come back to us really frustrated saying like yeah

what is this? Like I can't use this data for anything. And we took the blame for that.

Now, we've trained it to follow the best practices so that you always get good data out of the interviews. And I think that's the advantage you have as a vertical AI company that you can essentially train this agent to follow um best practices in in the work that you do.

终局想象：从产品评判到产品共创

So I want to go back to the concept of tide ponds that you had mentioned earlier. Um I think it's really interesting and so much of market research as I understand it today is almost more um inviting people to pass judgment on ideas that you feed them.

But it seems to me that one of the you know hallucinations can be a bug. They can also be a feature with generative AI.

And you know, do you think we're gonna see user research actually evolve into live product ideation? I could almost imagine, you know, AI inventing solutions as customers are going about their interview process, even helping visualize those solutions. Are your customers doing that already or do you think we're going to have the moment where AI can can create a tide pods idea in a market interview anytime soon?

Yeah, I think that's really exciting. Today they do that manually, you know, use AI to generate images of different concepts and feed that into the interviews. But I think specifically also with simulation it becomes really powerful. So we now have an MCP as well so that you can feed that into uh Claude and then you can tell Claude like hey run listen in a loop and then come up with um a bunch of ideas for how to market something or different concepts and then you can have it run like that.

I'm even thinking in the course of an interview as somebody's complaining about, you know, the tide can't I can't sound very portable for the AI to be, you know, live brainstorming with you solutions, not just

this is what it could look like. An image generator, too.

Yeah,

that'd be cool, Sonia.

Yeah, I think it's a good idea. You should be on our product team.

收尾

Awesome. Well, Alfred, uh, we really love what you're building. Um, thank you for taking the time to share insights both on the broader market which I think is just so fascinating and also what it takes to be building in the application layer right now. Um, we really admire the business that uh you've built and thank you for your continued partnership.

Thank you so much.

item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：Listen Labs 不是"更便宜的市场调研"，而是"更贵的市场调研"
表面叙事很诱人——AI 替代人工、规模化降本、Bain/McKinsey 颤抖。但 Alfred 自己泄露了真相："我们能向客户收取数十万美元，去跟八个国家的 20 位医生交谈。"这不是消费级 SaaS 的下沉故事，而是**让"过去做不到的事"变得可做**——而非让"做得到的事"变得便宜。
这里有个让人意外的对照：**Listen Labs 的真正竞争对手不是 Qualtrics，而是"不存在"**。是那些公司本来就不会做的研究、那些根本不会被问出来的问题、那些被默认为"不值得调查"的 99% 决策。当你把单位访谈成本降到接近零，需求曲线下方那片巨大的灰色三角形（消费者剩余）就被照亮了——而这片灰色，过去甚至没有名字。
## 二、隐藏的护城河不在模型，而在 Eval：一个跨领域的暗线
Alfred 说漏了一句最关键的话："我们的 eval 从 20% 爬到了 85%。然后我们设计了一个**更难的 eval**，现在又回到 20%。"
**垂直 AI 公司的真正护城河不是数据、不

item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反常识的观察：黄仁勋悄悄宣布了"CPU 的物种灭绝"

表面上，整场演讲被解读为"Vera Rubin 上市 + 七大战略"。但真正的核弹被埋在一句话里："过去所有的 CPU 都是为人类设计的，Vera 是第一颗为 agent 设计的 CPU。"

这意味着什么？过去 50 年，整个云计算的商业模式建立在一个假设之上——人类用秒计时，按 vCPU core/小时租用。AWS、Azure、阿里云的整个定价骨架都依赖"人感知不到的延迟 = 可压缩成本"。

但 agent 活在纳秒世界里。当一个 agent 等待 100ms，它就等于损失了一次工具调用、一次推理跳。Jensen 真正说的是：云计算的计费单位即将从"core/hour"被迫切换到"token/watt"——这是自 2006 年 EC2 诞生以来云行业最大的一次单位制崩塌。

讽刺的是，Intel 和 AMD 在这场演讲中一次都没被提到，但他们才是真正被掀桌的人。

二、跨领域连接：他正在把"计算机产业"重写成"工业革命的第三幕"

把演讲中三个看似无关的细节连起来： - "1 gigawatt means 1 gigawatt"（电力是硬约束） - "compute is revenue, throughput per watt is revenue"（瓦特是营收单位） - 生态从"软件开发者"扩张到"发电机、冷却系统、电网供应商"

这是一个经济学坐标系的迁移。请注意一个反直觉的等价：

Nvidia 现在卖的不是芯片，是"每瓦特的边际生产力"——这正是 1769 年瓦特改良蒸汽机时发明的概念单位。

更深的隐喻：Jensen 把数据中心讲成"AI 工厂"不是修辞，而是会计准则的迁移。AI 工厂的折旧表、ROIC、产能利用率，正在向 19 世纪的纺织厂、20 世纪的炼油厂靠拢。这意味着分析 Nvidia 的正确财务框架，正在从"半导体周期"切换到"重资产工业周期"——而华尔街目前还在用错的尺子量它。

跨界类比：台积电之于晶圆，正在变成 Nvidia 之于"瓦特→token 的转化率"。前者卖制程，后者卖能量转化效率。

三、被所有人忽略的暗线：Cosmos 3 是对"互联网数据"的一次哲学性宣战

注意他讲 Cosmos 时说的那句话："互联网上所有的语言数据都是从'我们写、我们读'的视角，而机器人需要的是机器自己的视角。"

这是一句被快速带过、却在重写 AI 产业地图的话。它的含义是：

  1. OpenAI、Anthropic、Google 拿到的全部互联网语料，对 physical AI 几乎是无用的——因为视角错了。
  2. 第一人称视频数据（机器人、AR 眼镜、车载摄像头）才是下一个"原油"，而没有人拥有它。
  3. 这解释了为什么 Nvidia 把 Omniverse + Cosmos 当成生死线：如果真实世界数据稀缺，那就用模拟器合成第一人称数据。

跨领域的"啊哈时刻"：这与儿童认知心理学惊人一致——皮亚杰发现，孩子要到 7 岁左右才能脱离"自我中心视角"理解他人立场。当前的 LLM 类似于一个永远活在"第三人称叙述"里的成年人，它从未真正"具身"过。Cosmos 3 在做的，本质上是给 AI 补上人类婴儿在出生第一年完成的事——通过身体动作建立第一人称世界模型。

由此可推一个大胆预测：未来 24 个月，"第一人称数据"会成为继 GPU 之后第二个被疯抢的稀缺资源。拥有大量第一人称视频的公司——Meta（Ray-Ban）、特斯拉（FSD 车队）、字节（短视频原始素材）——可能在物理 AI 时代占据语言模型时代 OpenAI 的位置。Nvidia 不想等他们，所以自己用 Cosmos 造合成数据。

四、一个隐藏的系统性真相

把 Jensen 整场演讲压缩成一句话：他在告诉所有 CEO，公司的组织架构正在从"人类组织 + 工具"变成"agent 组织 + 人类作为最后一道审批"。

NVIDIA Open Shell 听起来像一个开源项目，但它的真正定位是：agent 时代的"企业操作系统 + 合规审计层"。Red Hat、Canonical、Microsoft 都站队——这是 1990 年代 Linux 出现以来，企业 IT 栈最大的一次重构。

最反直觉的推论：未来 5 年内，企业里"被裁员"的可能不是基层员工，而是中层经理——因为中层的核心职能（任务编排、信息路由、状态汇报）正是 agent harness 在做的事。Open Shell 的隐藏角色，是"中层管理者的 SaaS 替代品"。

Jensen 没说出口的那句话是：你买的不是 GPU，是一个比你公司还便宜的影子组织。

📝 转录文本

NVIDIA GTC Taipei — 黄仁勋演讲全文

开场：回到台湾，感谢生态

Nvidia GTC Taipei Foreign Enjoy. So great to see all of you. Very good to be home. It is incredible how large our ecosystem in Taiwan has become.

Most of the time when people think about ecosystem, they think about our software stack. They think about the developer ecosystem above the computing systems that Nvidia builds. But Nvidia's ecosystem spans all the way upstream to all of our supply chain here in Taiwan where it all begins and downstream all the way to data centers and eventually to end users. Today we're going to talk about almost all of the ecosystem.

There's so many people to thank. I love my ecosystem here. I mean, there are so many companies here and some of my favorite ecosystem partners. So many Taiwan's rich ecosystem, the richest ecosystem, the world's best supply chain ecosystem. Unbelievable.

一、Agentic AI 已经到来：Token 成为利润单位

Two years ago when I was here, I started to talk to you about how AI has moved from generative AI and the other waves of AIs that are coming. The next wave of AI was agentic AI. And today we can say that agentic AI has arrived. That useful AI has arrived.

Now what does that mean from the industry's perspective? From the industry's perspective, that means that tokens are now in extraordinary demand. Because if you could do this, you're going to want to produce more of it. And because tokens are now profitable units, tokens are now profitable units of revenues because it is now profitable.

The AI companies want to build a lot more tokens, generate a lot more tokens, build more AI factories, which is the reason why compute demand here in Taiwan has skyrocketed. It is precisely the reason why all of you are so busy and your businesses are doing so well. In fact, that looks like some of your stock price, the compute pattern has changed, everything has changed.

So the first idea is that useful AI has arrived. AI is now a profit generator. AI is now a GDP generator and behind it is a whole new kind of computing pattern. Not just a large language model, but an agent. Today, almost everything we're going to talk about is going to be based on this.

二、什么是 Agent？新的计算模型

So, let me take a quick moment and show you what I'm talking about inside in this is a this is an agent. It's an agent application.

In the old days, this would be application. This would be code and this would be operating system application code running inside an application inside an operating system.

Today it is agent which consists of a large language model or many sitting inside a harness and that harness helps it orchestrates it to do productive work. This is the input. When that input comes, it has to understand, observe, reason, act, use tools. Use tools. That tool could be a spreadsheet, web browser, a data processing engine, database engine.

For example, this is orchestrated this harness orchestrate this routing of information every single time it touches either processing the context, understanding what is happening, reasoning about what to do, coming up with a plan that you can act that it acts on. That orchestration path is orchestrated by some software.
