Now, they do not have a good enough measurement at this time to say that the gravity that antimatter experiences is 100% that of matter. What they have measured is that antimatter fell down with 75% the strength of regular matter. But there were big uncertainties — plus or minus .13 due to the experiment, which was good but imperfect, and plus or minus .16 due to their theoretical model. So it's like 75 plus or minus something like 29 — and that means there's a good chance it's between .5 and 1, which means it's consistent with one. So they are improving their measurements.

四、制造反物质有多难？费米实验室的数字

Well, if I can, I would love to take a bit of a tangent on that topic, because I went down a rabbit hole watching some of your videos on antimatter. Fermilab was the hub for the production of antimatter for quite a while. — It was. — I saw that NASA said that the global estimate for the current rate of production of antimatter is 1 nanogram per year. Can you speak to how hard was it to make antimatter? And also you did mention in a video that if matter and antimatter meet, they produce a lot of energy. I think 20 grams of antimatter is equivalent to a 1-megaton nuclear warhead in terms of explosive energy. So all those questions together — how hard is it to produce antimatter? — It's freaking hard.

Okay. All right. So here's the deal. At the time — until 2011 — Fermilab was the most powerful anti-proton production facility on the planet. Every 2.3 seconds, we would smash 10¹³ protons into a target and we would get out 10⁸ anti-protons. So basically, in order to get a single anti-proton, we needed to smash 100,000 protons into material.

Every 2.3 seconds we would get on the order of 10⁸ antiprotons. What we would do is collect them over the course of 12 hours or so. We would have to collect them and cool them down and so forth — on the order of 10¹² anti-protons every 12 to 24 hours. 10¹² sounds like a lot. It really does. That is a trillion.

But you need to remember that a gram of antimatter is 10²³ antiprotons. So that means over the course of a day we were able to create something like 100-billionth of a gram. If we did that for a year, that would be about a nanogram. So about a nanogram a year, give or take. That's a reasonable estimate. A nanogram — one-billionth of a gram.

So at that rate, with that facility, it would take a billion years running with very little downtime to make a single gram of antimatter. If you combine 1 g of antimatter and 1 g of matter together, the energy release is equivalent to the combined Hiroshima and Nagasaki explosions. So that tells you — if you wanted a megaton, you need about 25 times more. So you would have to run for 25 billion years to get a megaton of explosive power.

五、$1.5 千万亿美元的炸弹：反物质的经济学

Let me lay it all out, because I think it's pretty interesting actually. This is a NASA estimate of how much it costs to produce antimatter. Looking at the cost of the accelerator, everything combined to do enough for a 1-megaton antimatter bomb — if such a thing would even be possible — on the order of 25 grams, would cost, based on the NASA estimate, about $1.5 quadrillion. By the way, NASA wasn't talking about a bomb — that's just me adding. NASA was talking about the estimate of $62 to $63 trillion per gram of antihydrogen, actually, is what they're referring to.

So compared to current best estimates of how much it takes to produce a 1-megaton nuclear warhead — everything combined is about $10 to $50 million in the United States. So you're talking about a difference, in terms of a weapon with equal power: $50 million versus $1.5 quadrillion.

六、反物质推进与星际旅行的诱惑

To me, what's interesting — weapons is just one indication of this. One other possibility, and NASA also writes about this, is the use of antimatter in propulsion systems — just like you can use nuclear fusion, and maybe even nuclear fusion down the line, in propulsion systems. I saw that one gram can help get us to the Alpha Centauri star system — if we can get to 0.2 times the speed of light — in 20 years, meaning it would take us 20 years to get to Alpha Centauri.

Is any of this a possible future? The use of antimatter for generation of energy — because we should mention that it's extremely compact. It has the obvious downsides that it's extremely costly to produce; we don't know how to do that kind of scale. The upside is it's compact, very powerful.

So the short answer is: it is not a physics problem. It's an engineering problem. So I have people for that. Okay. Um, okay. But — the truth is that antimatter, if you are able to assemble it and store it, sure, it would be able to take that antimatter, heat up matter and shoot it out the back of a rocket, and it would do what rockets do, and it would make us go quick, and that would be fine.

And we should mention — the thing that you just mentioned is correct. One of the hugest challenges is the containment.

— Because antimatter, when it comes in contact with matter, is a problem. — Right. So if you were unable to contain your trip to Alpha Centauri for even a millionth of a second — boom. And that would not be good. — Yeah.

You know, it reminds me of Star Trek where Scotty's saying, "Captain, the antimatter pods — we're losing containment, going to blow." That's exactly what would happen.

So the short answer is yes, antimatter as in principle we could make and use as a source of energy, but there are probably far less expensive sources of energy. It depends on what you need to do. The Voyager probes are still chugging along with plutonium. They're running out of energy at this point, but we could presumably do a somewhat better job if we needed to.

So I like the idea of antimatter, but the reality is the danger — not the obvious danger of weapons, but the danger of: if you wanted to be in a ship run by antimatter, if it ever got loose, well, you would never know it. That would be that.

七、突破口在哪里？答案：能量密度

The reason I find this kind of inspiring is — antimatter is in the space of physics that has a lot of mysteries. There's a lot of exploration to be done. And this kind of connection to energy means that if we have a bunch of breakthroughs on the antimatter side, that might lead to a better propulsion system, better energy generation systems. — In principle. — There's some combination of engineering here, but there's some combination of understanding the fundamental physics. — I mean, we know how to do this. You take energy, you make antimatter. You have to contain it, you have to store it, you have to do all the hard things. But I would be shocked if there was some like new addition to the theory that made antimatter production easier. — Interesting. So we know how to produce antimatter with accelerators. You're saying there's no breakthrough in physics that could lead to different mechanisms for the generation of antimatter? — You have to concentrate energy. That's it. If there's another way to concentrate energy, that would work too. — And our best knowledge of how to concentrate energy is the accelerator.

And remember — we're talking concentrating it into volumes the size of a proton. If you concentrate it to the size of your thumb, well then, it's really the density that matters, the local density. When you smash two protons together, all of that's occurring in a tiny tiny volume. So it's the local density of energy that matters. If you had a lot of energy in a thimble or something, it's probably not dense enough. It really has to be in close proximity for that to happen.

So if there's another way — we know how to do it, to make that density thing with accelerators. If someone has a bright idea on how to make highly dense energy, then yeah, making antimatter is a piece of cake. But that's the crux: concentrated energy.

— Yeah, and how to do so in a cost-efficient manner. Not trillions of dollars.

八、最大的谜：反物质都去哪儿了？

Well yeah — so one of the big mysteries with antimatter is the bigger why. Where is the antimatter that should kind of be there? If the whole idea is that anytime you generate matter, you generate the same amount of antimatter — and yet, when we look out into the observable universe, it seems like there's not antimatter for the most part there. Correct? So what do we understand about this mystery? What are the possible explanations as to why?

There's this thing called baryogenesis. And, reiterating a little bit what you just said — these are both Einstein things. Einstein says that when you take energy you make matter and antimatter in equal quantities. And Einstein says: after the Big Bang there was a lot of energy in the universe, which should have made matter and antimatter. We only see matter. Where'd the antimatter go? And the answer is: we don't know.

However, there are some ideas. And there's a lot of thinking on it. In fact, Fermilab is doing an experiment right now with neutrinos, trying to better understand what it was that made the matter and antimatter not be the same.

Now, we do have a measurement of how much different it should be. And it's kind of neat. We can do this by counting the number of protons in the universe — just looking at galaxies and so forth. And then we can look at the cosmic microwave background, which is sort of the aftermath of the Big Bang. And we can count the number of photons from the cosmic microwave background. With a little bit of math, we can then say that somehow in the early universe, something made a very very tiny asymmetry:

For every billion-billion — with a B — antimatter particles that existed in the universe, there were a billion and one matter particles. The billions cancelled, annihilated, destroyed each other — and that extra one that's left over is us.

So what physics mechanism made that ever-so-slight asymmetry is not understood. There are some thoughts.

One thought is that — well, it's just how it was when the universe was formed. There was an asymmetry. It was not made by matter or antimatter.

Another possibility is — there are various numbers of theories all under the word baryogenesis. Baryo coming from the word baryon, which basically means protons; and genesis meaning the creation of. We'd say that simply because the protons are the heaviest particles, so baryogenesis is just the creation of matter. There are a number of theories in quantum mechanics that say that matter and antimatter can oscillate back and forth into one another. And there is a slight asymmetry in how that happens. We know that this is true to a degree — we've measured it in the 1960s with a different form of matter. Not protons, but a type of ephemeral matter that only exists in particle accelerators. So we know that there is a slight difference between matter and antimatter, but it's not enough. It doesn't explain that. We're not sure.

九、轻子起源：费米实验室押注的颠覆性假说

So at Fermilab, we have this idea which kind of turns things on its head. It's not baryogenesis, it's leptogenesis. Leptons are the electrons — and because Fermilab is currently the world's most powerful neutrino accelerator, and neutrinos are leptons, there is this idea.

Now, leptogenesis is incredibly complicated, but the idea is: it is possible — we know that neutrinos actually change their identity. There are three different types of neutrinos, like cats and jaguars and tigers. If you have a beam of just cats, if you go along a little while, you find there's cats and jaguars; and then tigers; and then they'll be back to all cats again. This oscillation thing is called neutrino oscillation. We've known it's been true since 1998.

What we are studying is: we're going to make a beam of neutrinos and another beam of antimatter neutrinos. We're going to study the oscillation behavior of the two of them. And it is possible — it is unlikely, but it is possible — that the two of them will oscillate at slightly different rates. And if the neutrinos oscillate at slightly different rates, then that, along with several other highly improbable things, can tie together and might explain why there is more matter in the universe.

If I was going to bet the farm, I'll bet that they oscillate at the same rate. But I don't know, and you don't know — till you do the measurements. So that's what we're doing. There are some other experiments trying to measure it right now. So there's a big race between the Fermilab group and another group in Japan to see who gets there first and makes this measurement, and we will find out.

If it turns out though that there is a difference in this oscillation rate between matter and antimatter, it will be a huge clue in this very very difficult puzzle. I wish I could tell you I knew what the answer is — but literally nobody knows. And that's the thing of being a research scientist like me: if you're not confused, you're not doing your job.

十、收束：我们都是一个"幸运的会计误差"

— So, there is this desperate — or not desperate, exciting — search for this tiny asymmetry. — Yes. — It's so crazy to think that everything we see around us is a result of this tiny asymmetry. That there was this gigantic annihilation of matter and antimatter in the early universe — and this is just some little accident. — Yeah. Yeah. That's crazy. — It's a happy accident. That is just — I mean, it's totally crazy.

## 一、最反直觉的发现：我们整个宇宙是一个"会计四舍五入误差"
十亿分之一的不对称——每十亿个反物质粒子对应十亿零一个物质粒子——这个数字本身就是宇宙学里最荒诞的诗。**你、星系、爱情、痛苦，全是大爆炸后那场惊天对消中"没消干净"的零头**。这意味着宇宙的"主旋律"其实是湮灭，而存在只是噪声。换句话说，**"存在"在统计意义上是异常值**，不存在才是宇宙的默认态。这彻底颠覆了哲学中"为什么有物而非无"的传统提问方式——正确的问题或许是："为什么湮灭没有彻底完成它的工作？"
## 二、出人意料的跨领域连接：反物质生产 ≈ 风险投资的失败率
费米实验室每砸 10 万个质子才换回 1 个反质子——**0.001% 的"成品率"**。这个数字几乎精确对应硅谷顶级 VC 投出独角兽的命中率，也接近癌症免疫疗法中 CAR-T 细胞最终成功识别肿瘤的比例。**任何一个需要"在巨大能量/资本/信息密度中筛出极稀有目标"的系统，都会收敛到类似的瓶颈结构**：你无法靠优化通道效率突破这个比例，因为限制因素是"局部密度"而非"总量"——Don Lincoln 反复强调"是局部能量密度决定能否生成反物

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The Hidden Problem With MCTS – Eric Jang
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、MCTS 的"伪保证"——一个被普遍误解的训练神话

表面上，MCTS 被描述为"严格优于策略网络的改进算子"，但 Eric 在这段对话里悄悄揭开了一个被工程师们集体回避的事实：MCTS 的优越性不是数学定理，而是一种工程信仰。它的"保证"建立在两个前提上——价值网络足够准确、模拟次数趋于无穷。一旦任何一个前提失守，MCTS 不仅不能改进策略，反而会主动污染它。

这里有一个令人意外的观察：MCTS 的失败模式不是噪声，而是系统性偏见的放大器。当训练数据缺失某类局面（例如对局都在中盘投降），叶子节点的价值评估会系统性失真，而 MCTS 的反向传播机制会把这种失真沿着搜索树向上累加并放大。换句话说，搜索越深，错误越被"权威化"。这就是为什么一个看起来"更聪明"的算法会让模型集体退化——它把局部的盲区，编织成了全局的幻觉。

二、跨领域连接：MCTS、Dagger、自动驾驶与"幸存者偏差"的共同骨架

Eric 自己提到了 Dagger 和自动驾驶的偏离纠正，但更深的联系在于：这三者本质上都在对抗同一种结构性病——分布外状态的评估真空。 - MCTS 因为对局总在中段结束，失去了对终局的"肌肉记忆"； - 模仿学习 因为专家从不进入危险状态，学不会从危险中恢复； - 自动驾驶 因为路测数据偏向正常道路，对极端工况盲目自信。

把它们并排看，会浮现一个意外的洞察：这其实是"幸存者偏差"在强化学习中的算法形态。亚伯拉罕·瓦尔德当年在二战飞机上发现：你看到的弹孔分布，恰恰是"没被打下来的飞机"留下的，真正致命的位置反而没有数据。MCTS 的训练 buffer 正是如此——它积累的全是"被认为重要的对局轨迹"，而那些被资源效率优化掉的"非重要片段"，恰恰是模型未来崩溃的位置。Eric 提出的"10% 的对局打到终局"其实就是机器学习版的瓦尔德修正：故意收集那些"理应不重要"的数据，因为重要性本身就是一种偏见。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：所有"自我改进"算法都共享同一个脆弱性

如果把视角再拉高一层，会发现一个更深的模式：任何依赖"自己评估自己"的系统，都内嵌着同一类崩溃几何——AlphaZero 的自我对弈、LLM 的 RLHF、人类的自我反思，乃至组织的 KPI 体系，全都遵循同一条曲线：评估器的盲区会通过反馈循环被放大成系统的核心信念。

这里有一个反直觉的推论："提早投降"这种看似理性的效率优化，恰恰是 AlphaGo 体系最危险的设计选择。它在短期节省算力，却在长期掏空模型的"终局直觉"。这种现象在人类组织里有一个对应物——那些过早结束的项目、过早放弃的对话、过早裁定的失败，构成了组织永远学不会的盲区。所谓"知道何时止损"的智慧，从信息论角度看，其实是一种主动制造分布外灾难的机制。

所以 Eric 那个"让 10% 的游戏打到底"的工程 trick，其实暗含一条普适法则：任何自我改进系统，都必须强制保留一部分"看似无意义的完整经历"，否则它终将在自己最自信的地方崩盘。这条法则同时适用于围棋引擎、语言模型、科研团队，以及——你自己的学习方式。

📝 转录文本

MCTS 的核心机制与 Dagger 的类比

What MCTS is doing is basically saying like you play this game where you eventually lost, but on every single action, I'm going to give you a strictly better action that you should take instead. It does not guarantee that you are going to win, but it does guarantee that, you know, if you take these tuples as training data, so that you retrain your uh your your policy network to predict these ones instead of these ones, you're going to do better.

And uh this is very related to dagger in robotics and in imitation learning where you want to collect a intervention here. And even if you're in a in a not great state, for example, like a self-driving car that, you know, veers off the side of the road, there is still a valid action that kind of corrects you and brings you back.

提问：MCTS 一定优于策略网络吗？

Yeah. Okay, so um pedantic question. But is there a guarantee that MCTS must be better than the policy? For example, you could imagine early on in training, because MCTS is informed by the value network,

Yeah.

early on in training, uh when the value network hasn't been well trained on finished uh games, um that like MCTS is worse than sort of randomly initialized policy. So, is it just like a heuristic that MCTS is better than the policy, or is that like is there some guarantee?

回答：这只是启发式，不是保证

Right. In in practice, it is a heuristic, um and it does work in also in practice, but let me illustrate an example where MCTS can give you a worse distribution than your policy network.

So, um and this can often happen if your self-play algorithm um has trained to a good point, but then somehow it's uh it collapses because it's um it's not trained on diverse data or something, right?

失败案例：终局价值评估的崩塌

So, um let's say we have a board state where um the policy recommendations here are very good. So, so like, you know, pi of AS is like great. But, um somehow, because maybe we're playing on a lot of games where the the bots just resign instead of playing all the way to the Trump Taylor resolution, they kind of forget um how to evaluate those kind of late stage plans, right?

Like in the in the case that we showed with the corner play, maybe like 100% of our training data in our replay buffer has lost examples of how to evaluate the value function at those states. So, you might end up in a scenario where your terminal value is like very bad. And if the terminal values of the leaves are not good, then this will actually propagate all the way up and cause your your your puck selection criteria and your backups to be off. And then you're end up visiting a very very different distribution than what your policy initially recommended.

模拟次数与方差问题

Um also, if your number of sims is low, then you might also have a variance issue where you just don't explore enough, right? Like like a it's only guaranteed to converge when you kind of take end to infinity. Um So so variance in, you know, your search process as well as inaccuracies in your evaluation can definitely screw with the quality of your policy network recommendation. And so that's why it's not a guarantee to improve.

AlphaGo Lee 的工程对策

But and and that is why I think I I suspect why AlphaGo Lee had the playouts to the end in their training algorithm so that they could ground this thing in real playouts.

Yeah.

Um in practice, what you could also do is just like for 10% of the games, you you prevent the bots from resigning and you just say like resolve it to the end. So you get some training data in your replay buffer to really resolve those kind of like late stage playouts that normal human players would would kind of not play to.

Yeah, yeah. If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、MCTS 的"伪保证"——一个被普遍误解的训练神话
表面上，MCTS 被描述为"严格优于策略网络的改进算子"，但 Eric 在这段对话里悄悄揭开了一个被工程师们集体回避的事实：**MCTS 的优越性不是数学定理，而是一种工程信仰**。它的"保证"建立在两个前提上——价值网络足够准确、模拟次数趋于无穷。一旦任何一个前提失守，MCTS 不仅不能改进策略，反而会**主动污染**它。
这里有一个令人意外的观察：**MCTS 的失败模式不是噪声，而是系统性偏见的放大器**。当训练数据缺失某类局面（例如对局都在中盘投降），叶子节点的价值评估会系统性失真，而 MCTS 的反向传播机制会把这种失真**沿着搜索树向上累加并放大**。换句话说，搜索越深，错误越被"权威化"。这就是为什么一个看起来"更聪明"的算法会让模型集体退化——它把局部的盲区，编织成了全局的幻觉。
## 二、跨领域连接：MCTS、Dagger、自动驾驶与"幸存者偏差"的共同骨架
Eric 自己提到了 Dagger 和自动驾驶的偏离纠正，但更深的联系在于：**这三者本质上都在对抗同一种结构性病——分布外状态的评估真空**

item.title: [Lex Clips] The value of hard work - Advice for young people | Don Lincoln and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的观察：他的"贫穷"恰恰是他成为科学家的隐藏燃料

大多数人会把 Don 的故事读作"逆境中崛起"——父母没受过高等教育、来自乡下、缺乏学术引路人。但仔细听他的叙述，会发现一个反转：正因为家里没有"学术地图"，他才不得不在科幻小说、Asimov、Sagan、Gamow 这些通俗科学读物里自己拼凑世界观。而通俗科普恰恰是最容易点燃哲学性好奇心的载体——它不教你解方程，而是让你迷上"宇宙为什么是这样"。

一个有教授父母的孩子，往往从小被推进结构化的训练里，问题被提前框定。而 Don 的"信息匮乏"反而保留了他提问的原始野性——他先是被宇宙起源、宇宙终结这些近乎神学的问题击中，然后才一步步走进物理。换句话说：他不是"克服了出身"成为科学家，而是那个出身塑造了一个特定品种的科学家——一个带着哲学胃口、却又坚持要"做实验拿答案"的人。

跨领域连接：Don 的"8 点到午夜"和顶级运动员、僧侣、艺术家共享同一套神经回路

注意他描述工作狂阶段时的措辞——"我无法想象做别的事情"、"宇宙不能赢我"、"东西坏了让我生气，所以我投入更多"。这不是常规意义上的"努力"，这是一种愤怒驱动的痴迷（rage-fueled obsession）。

这种状态在三个看似无关的领域里反复出现： - 顶级长跑运动员描述训练时常说"痛苦让我兴奋" - 禅宗公案修行者面对无解的话头会进入"愤恨疑情"——越想不通越要想 - 作家村上春树说写小说像挖深井，"不是因为想，而是因为不挖会死"

Don 无意中暴露了一个心理学秘密：真正的"心流大师"内核不是热爱，而是一种与对象的对抗性亲密。他把宇宙拟人化为对手（"我不让宇宙赢"），这种敌意化的爱比纯粹的"热爱"耐烧得多——热爱会疲惫，而"不服气"几乎不会。

隐藏的系统性真相：他给年轻人的建议里藏着一个"反建议"

表面上 Don 在说"努力工作，找到热爱"。但如果你重听一遍，他其实说了两次相互矛盾的话： 1. "如果它不给你带来快乐，那它可能不适合你"（近藤麻理惠式的温柔建议） 2. "你必须无法忍受不知道答案"（近乎病理性的强迫驱动）

这不是矛盾——这是一个筛选漏斗。他在用一种几乎温柔的语气，传递一个相当残酷的标准：科学不是一个"职业选择"，而是一种人格诊断。能听懂第一句的人会觉得被允许、被安慰；能听懂第二句的人会认出自己。

这里有个被忽视的洞见：他写科普书的真正动机不是"启蒙大众"，而是在茫茫人海里发出一种声纳信号——只有那些会被"宇宙如何诞生如何毁灭"这种问题真正咬住、晚上睡不着的孩子才会回应。他不是在传播科学，他是在用书做基因检测，识别同类。这种"通过自我表达来寻找同族"的模式，和音乐人写歌、诗人写诗的深层动力完全同构。

📝 转录文本

一、童年与启蒙：乡下穷孩子的科学之路

Lex: You have a really inspiring life story. Your folks didn't go to college. Can you just tell me about your childhood and where you found the love for physics and science, and maybe how you found your journey to become a physicist given the context of where you came from?

Don: Well, [sighs and gasps] you know, I grew up a poor kid in the boondocks. Great parents, but not ones that could guide me terribly academically — but very, very nurturing. You know, my mom would laugh that she could stop helping me with math after like sixth or seventh grade. Um, but they were supportive.

And there were a couple — three things, I think — that folded into it.

1. 科幻小说与想象力

One is, I was a voracious reader as a kid. I loved science fiction. I would read a book a day. It drove my mother nuts, because she would try to be nice — she'd buy me a book and I'd say thank you, and the next day it'd be done. You know, it just drove her completely nuts. But anyways, science fiction is good for fostering imagination. And so that's precisely what it did.

2. 1970 年代的科普巨人

In addition — and this is where the more serious science came along — there were lovely [sighs] science communicators that were popular in the 1970s: Isaac Asimov, Carl Sagan, a guy by the name of George Gamow. They wrote books about science aimed at a lay person. I was a kid. I surely couldn't read a textbook and understand it, but I could read and, you know, get a hint of what science was.

3. 不可抑制的好奇心与哲学倾向

And on top of that, you know, I was, as most scientists — people who became scientists — irrepressibly curious about everything. Um, and I had sort of a quasi-philosophical mind. I mean, I was interested in things — questions that have in the past been theological, and then philosophical, and now are more scientific. Questions about: How did the universe come into existence? Why is the universe the way it is? Why are the laws of the universe what we see them to be? How was the universe created? How will it be destroyed?

These are, you know, big questions that have bothered humanity for — well, thousands of years. And so, you know, you said I had philosophy and religion minors in college, and I did, 'cause I was curious about that. I was hoping that learning that history might help me understand these questions. And it was in college where I came to realize that the answers that I was searching for were not to be found in those directions — but I still learned about how those questions have been asked in the past.

二、为什么选择粒子物理而非宇宙学

And so I became a scientist. And the only question was: was I going to be a cosmologist/astrophysicist, or a particle physicist? When I had to make that decision, it was the mid-80s, and at the time there were a lot fewer cosmology measurements. There was an awful lot of thinking about the universe, and not enough measuring. Whereas with particle physics — by God, you could do experiments. And so what attracted me was the ability to actually get an answer, and not just mull over what an answer might be. And so I became a particle physicist.

三、为下一代乡下孩子写书

It was difficult without having, you know, family mentors or anything like that — but, you know, I managed. And that actually is why — well, I'm here, and why I have spent a fair bit of my time writing books and so forth. Because I figure that there has to be some other kid out there in Iowa, Kansas, Montana — somewhere out in some little town without a lot of access to the kinds of things that people who have highly educated parents do. And I'm hoping that some of them will have read some of the things I've written, and will find their own path forward, because I found it very rewarding over the years.

And, you know, I've been doing this long enough that I'm sure this is true — I've had kids come up to me at the lab and say, "Hey, I'm a summer intern because I saw your video, or read your book, or whatever." So I know that, at least, I've made a small impact. I mean, I'd always like to do more, and I appreciate the opportunity that your audience affords me — 'cause I think it's important to talk about these things. These are really cool, fascinating questions. They are unanswered, and they are just waiting for youngsters to come and spend some time thinking about them — because one of your viewers might be one of the people who answer these questions that have stymied very smart people for decades.

四、努力的价值：8 点到午夜的费米实验室岁月

Lex: And we should also say that you're a legit scientist. So — we'll mention Sean Carroll, who's a legit scientist, legit physicist, but is also a good science communicator. Anyway, I did want to mention — I don't know if this is true, but I kind of heard you talk about this — that when you first showed up to Fermilab, you were like working crazy hours, working extremely hard…

Don: 8:00 a.m. to midnight.

Lex: I did. Uh, first of all, I love that. [laughter] Can you speak to what drove you, and maybe the value of hard work in that context — in your early career, when you discover a thing you're passionate about?

Don: Well, yeah. I mean, obviously being smart — you know, if you're Einstein, then maybe you can slack, I guess. Although even he didn't do that. But I'm not Einstein. But the fact is, when I was young and I was unencumbered — no family, no kids or anything — I couldn't imagine anything I wanted to do more.

I mean, some people, they want to go out to the club, they want to — I don't know — play soccer or something. But I wanted to make measurements, and I wanted to understand and learn. And that was fantastic.

研究生时期的作息

And so, as a graduate student — and this isn't for everybody — but I worked outrageously. From Monday through Saturday, I would be at the lab voluntarily, because I wanted to be, from 8:00 a.m. to midnight. And on Sunday, I would work from 8 until about 5:00 — and that's because from 5 to midnight I had to wash clothes and buy groceries and things like that.

And I loved it, you know. And I still love it. I can't do that anymore — but that's simply because I have other obligations. But had I been rich, I would have done the same thing. It's something I truly, truly loved. There is absolutely nothing more fascinating to me than having a hard problem and figuring it out.

五、把聪明人和"真科学家"分开的，是 Grit

That work ethic — well, there's a couple of things that separate smart people from no-kidding scientists, 'cause all scientists are smart. But the thing that many scientists have is a drive and a real grit.

For me, and for so many scientists that I know, trying to measure something and having it not work just kind of ticks me off — and I am not going to let the universe, in my lab or whatever, beat me. Some people, if the thing breaks, it's like, "Oh man, that didn't work" — and a lot of people, "Well, I'm going to go home, I'm fed up." No, it would just kind of make me mad, and I'd put more effort into it.

And [laughter] — okay, I was crazy, I worked long hours — but I think the people who are really good at this will do maybe not that much. Some people have to have a better life than that, but a lot — because you can't imagine not knowing the answer.

六、给年轻人的建议：识别自己是不是"那类人"

And when you see that as an older guy — you don't, maybe not to that degree — but when you see that kind of drive, that intensity of trying to get the answers, you know that person's a winner.

And so, if some student out there — if it doesn't bring you joy, as, uh, what's her name, the Japanese gal says — if it doesn't bring you joy, then it might not be for you. And then you could be a person who reads about it and is involved, but if you want to be a real scientist, it has to be just part of what you are.

Lex: And, by the way, it is a hard life, but it is also a very fulfilling one. So working hard toward the thing you love is a really fulfilling way to be.

Don: I think that's true for an artist or something — you know, anybody, a musician. They just keep practicing, because it is who they are.

七、收尾

Lex: Well, I'm glad there's people like you at a place I admire like Fermilab — one of the many places in the United States and the world that is carrying the beacon of great science and great engineering forward. Don, thank you so much for everything you do — for all the teaching you do online, for all the incredible physics work that you do at Fermilab. And thank you so much for talking today.

Don: Thank you for having me.

观看原视频 →

大多数人会把 Don 的故事读作"逆境中崛起"——父母没受过高等教育、来自乡下、缺乏学术引路人。但仔细听他的叙述，会发现一个**反转**：正因为家里没有"学术地图"，他才不得不在科幻小说、Asimov、Sagan、Gamow 这些**通俗科学读物**里自己拼凑世界观。而通俗科普恰恰是最容易点燃**哲学性好奇心**的载体——它不教你解方程，而是让你迷上"宇宙为什么是这样"。
一个有教授父母的孩子，往往从小被推进结构化的训练里，问题被提前框定。而 Don 的"信息匮乏"反而保留了他**提问的原始野性**——他先是被宇宙起源、宇宙终结这些近乎神学的问题击中，然后才一步步走进物理。换句话说：他不是"克服了出身"成为科学家，而是**那个出身塑造了一个特定品种的科学家——一个带着哲学胃口、却又坚持要"做实验拿答案"的人**。
## 跨领域连接：Don 的"8 点到午夜"和顶级运动员、僧侣、艺术家共享同一套神经回路
注意他描述工作狂阶段时的措辞——"我无法想象做别的事情"、"宇宙不能赢我"、"东西坏了让我生气，所以我投入更多"

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Anthropic IPO, MSTR, HYPE, and Dimon vs. Armstrong
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"棘轮效应"背后的认知不对称：市场已学会单向遗忘

Sosnick 提到的"ratchet effect"——市场对波斯湾每一条积极消息都上涨，对随后的破灭却不下跌——表面看是乐观偏差，实际暴露了一个更深刻的结构性现象：当代市场的"情绪记忆"已经被算法和被动资金切除了下半部分。

意外的观察是：这与神经科学中的"享乐适应"（hedonic adaptation）原理惊人相似，但方向是反的。人类大脑对负面刺激比正面刺激更敏感（损失厌恶），但今天的市场恰恰相反——它对正面消息累积加仓，对负面消息选择性失忆。原因是被动资金的"无脑买入"机制让市场失去了下调的肌肉记忆。这其实是 ETF 化的一个隐藏副作用：当 60% 的资金不读新闻，市场就只剩下半边脸。

二、Anthropic IPO 与"反向 10-bagger 困境"：硅谷正在杀死自己的合法性

节目里有一段被快速带过但极其重要的洞察——当 IPO 估值从 90 年代的"十亿级"飙升到今天的"万亿级"，散户在数学上已经不可能获得 10 倍回报。这看起来是个估值问题，但本质是一个政治问题。

跨领域连接：这与 17 世纪荷兰东印度公司的命运高度相似。VOC 早期向公众发行股份，让阿姆斯特丹的面包师、寡妇、水手都能参与殖民贸易的暴利——这是现代股市合法性的起源。但当后期股份被精英家族垄断、新发行充满限制后，公众的"参与感"消失，最终导致了 18 世纪对"金融贵族"的政治反弹。今天的硅谷正在重演这个剧本：Series H、$2 万亿 IPO、私募市场吃掉所有 alpha。Austin 提到的"学生们的愤怒"不是情绪宣泄，而是合法性危机的前奏。当 Z 世代认为公开市场只是"VC 倾销给我的退休基金"的工具时，他们会用投票而非投资来回应。

意外之处在于：Hyperliquid 这种去中心化衍生品市场，可能反而是传统资本市场合法性的救命稻草——因为它至少给了散户一个 pre-IPO 的价格发现工具，把"信息垄断"这个最不公的环节炸开了。

三、MicroStrategy 的"三体问题"与货币史的镜像：Saylor 正在重演 19 世纪银行家的剧本

Saylor 卖掉 32 BTC 被嘲笑为"nothing burger"，但 Chris 指出的更深一层是：STRC 需要 11.5% 的年化股息，而 Bitcoin 作为 inert asset（惰性资产）无内生收益。这是一个经典的资产负债期限错配，Austin 直接点名硅谷银行作为类比。

但真正出人意料的连接是：这其实是 1830-1860 年美国"自由银行时代"（Free Banking Era）的完美翻版。当时州立银行发行自己的纸币，号称由黄金 1:1 背书，但黄金本身不产生现金流。当银行需要支付利息或赎回时，要么卖黄金（破坏背书叙事），要么发行更多纸币（稀释信心）。结果？大量银行倒闭，催生了后来的国家银行法案和最终的美联储。

Saylor 的 STRC/STRF 结构本质上是在"自由银行化" Bitcoin——他在用 Bitcoin 作为"硬通货储备"发行带息债务工具。历史告诉我们这种结构有一个隐藏的相变点：当主资产价格年化涨幅低于债务成本时，体系不是缓慢恶化，而是突然崩溃。Jeff Dorman 说"未来四个月内有人会输得很惨"——这不是悲观，这是金融史的均值回归。

更深的反讽是：Bitcoin 的核心叙事是"反对法币系统的脆弱性"，但 MicroStrategy 正在 Bitcoin 之上重建一个法币系统的所有脆弱性——杠杆、期限错配、信心依赖、社会共识赌博。Saylor 其实在无意中证明了任何资产一旦被金融工程化，都会重新发明它原本想逃离的问题。这是一个比"卖了 32 个币"重要得多的哲学事件。

to tourists, they can then say, you know what? I bought insert name of crypto ETF or ETP here. I bought this. It did well for a while and now it's not, or I bought it at the highs and I mistimed it completely. Let me move to something else.

That if it was bought by performance chasers in the first place, it's going to be sold by performance chasers as something else outperforms it. That's not me taking a view one way or the other on crypto as a long-term investment, but that's just that I do think that is a very important consideration.

You can't discount, you can't dismiss the importance of money flow and for better or worse, the huge success of crypto ETFs was such a boon on the way up. But now this is the flip side of that trade. I'm not attributing it specifically to that, but to many investors now, it's one more sector among their speculative momentum-based investments.

股市为何接近历史高点：盈利、guidance 与"棘轮效应"

So let's dive right in. I kind of set the stage here, but equities are still like near all time highs. How do you make sense of all this?

Well, there's a few things going on and they're working in concert. I will say to some extent we can thank good earnings. And that's always in my mind the best reason for a stock market to rally is the fact that in this past earnings season, we've seen a decent number, I would even say a significant number, of EPS beats and more importantly, positive guidance.

To the case in some situations, in let's say, semiconductor memory stocks, the guidance has been extreme. We can argue whether some of that guidance has been extrapolated maybe a bit more, maybe a bit further into the future than it ought to be. But I will stipulate that there is a solid base behind this rally. But the magnitude of it we can question.

Ratchet Effect：单向上涨的新闻反应

And to a certain extent, what we've noted over the past few weeks, let's say since the end of March, is what I've been calling the ratchet effect about news from the Persian Gulf.

By that I mean we seem to rally on each positive story about some sort of resolution to the situation, something that might reopen the strait. And so far, as we're taping this, yeah, there was another positive story this morning coming out of Axios. But for the most part, basically for however many there have been—I lose count of how many there have been—while oil and bonds tend to give back their decline (yields give back their declines and oil futures give back their declines), when the stories don't come to pass, stocks haven't really given back anything.

So that's why I call it like a ratchet. You move in one direction. One direction, yeah.

And so that's been a big factor. And what I'm wrestling with right now as we're speaking is stocks are reacting positively. I literally stepped off the desk to go to the bathroom and boom, we went from up to down to up very sharp. I'm like, what happened? Had to be a good story. We had a good story, but they're not ripping ahead, even though this is one of the more detailed and more substantial stories that we've gotten.

So I do have to wonder how much of this either A is priced into the market or B is traders' exhaustion at saying "you know, another one of these stories." They're not mutually exclusive, but how the market interprets them can lead to very different outcomes. Because if it's largely priced in, you have to wonder if it's a sell-the-news type of event. If the market's not reacting just because they're simply tired of reacting to these, that does leave room for further upside. So that's a huge unknown right now.

地缘政治与油价的潜在冲击

Yeah, it's hard to tell because people like to think of good things happening. I don't see much appetite from the US side to really renew hostilities in any way, any major form, despite what President Trump says from time to time. And as much as he claims to not care about the electoral calendar, that is something that I'm sure is on top of mind for him.

So maybe we're at worst case scenario and this is where it's at. But I've read, and I'm sure you have too, we may not have felt the full pain of oil prices if the strait doesn't reopen yet. I mean, $5 a gallon gas plus, et cetera, could have a damaging effect. We have to kind of see how that's going to fit.

Anthropic IPO：AI 估值面对公开市场的第一次大考

Now, let's talk about something that's relatively big news here, which is Anthropic has filed for an IPO. So I would say this is the first real test of AI valuations hitting public markets.

The news is that Anthropic has confidentially submitted a draft S1 registration statement to the SEC. They've said this gives us the option to pursue an initial public offering. The Wall Street Journal reported the same, saying it's setting it on a path to go public as early as this fall.

This sets up a race with OpenAI, which is reported to be preparing its own confidential filing in the coming weeks, also targeting fall, according to both Fortune and Bloomberg. And it's part of a white-hot IPO season because we also have SpaceX coming, targeting a $2 trillion valuation for their fundraise.

大规模发行对市场的影响

The big question will be, what does this mean when you have so much issuance hitting the market at the same time? The last time we saw a spate of IPOs was summer of last year. And then you had a cool off for several months with all the data hitting the market. Now you've got Anthropic and you've got a $2 trillion IPO coming also in SpaceX at something like a 90 times price to sales ratio. So it's going to have an impact on the market for sure.

The SpaceX IPO is June 10th, I believe. So we'll keep our eyes out for that.

24/7 市场与 pre-IPO 价格发现

Yeah, Chairman Atkins said he's going to make public markets great again. It seems like things are starting to work. Of course, you'd hope that in the future companies go public sooner. That would be helpful because again, you want people in.

But the thing that I'm looking at is again, the magnitude and size. If you look at the size of the IPO markets back in '25, I think there's about $44 billion that was raised. Then you have SPACs that raised another like $30 or so. And then direct listings have been very tiny.

But the one thing that I'm starting to see is this really sharp move to 24/7 markets. The ME just launched 24/7 markets today. They announced that. Only for crypto. But then you have like trade XYZ, the Hyperliquid guys pumping away at these pre-IPO derivatives markets.

And so now for the first time, you have this potential for real price discovery that I think people are still sussing it out right now and figuring out, wait a second, is this a good thing? Is it a bad thing? But I think it's going to be a very important tool, because IPOs tend to be mispriced in many cases. You're kind of walking blindly into the syndicate. And now with some of these tools that we're seeing, if they nail it, then issuers can better time their launch. They can better price their IPO, whether it's through a direct or even an IPO listing, and they'll leave less money on the table.

So I think this could be really interesting as you bring together crypto, and in this case, IPO, because those are the issuers, you can get to a much more efficient and frankly inclusive capital markets because retail has been boxed out of IPOs. Now, I think the good news here is that for some of these IPOs coming up, there's a lot more allocation to retail, which is, I think, when Ram comes back, maybe a departure from former syndicates.

But what I like is that it sounds like, of course, this is banned in the US—don't know, we'll have to get through that—but the fact that we're getting pre-IPO price discovery is pretty amazing. So hats off to the TradeXYZ guys, the Hyperliquid guys. Really, really interesting capabilities that they're now bringing to the overall. So it feels like the missing link in a way for inclusivity for retail and also for maybe more efficient IPO markets.

二级市场 ≠ 价格发现的反驳

Price discovery though, Chris—I think anything trading on a secondary for hard to access asset with a tightly controlled cap table, and the assets in high demand, it'll always trade at a premium and have inevitably compressed down once the issue starts trading. So I don't see it as price discovery. I think it's like a bad idea if you try to buy on these secondary markets at an inflated valuation.

I think we're talking about two different things, right? So secondary markets, I get it, but if you have really, really deep liquid derivatives markets, I think that could be much more interesting. And we're starting to see that for the first time. We just haven't had deep liquid pre-IPO markets in the past. And I'm kind of watching the SpaceX IPO. I mean, let's see if the TradeXYZ guys nail it. And, you know, last IPO is, I forget the name, Ciribus or something. It seems like it did all right. But that's what I'm watching.

IPO 时机推迟与"散户被排除"的代际后果

I think part of this comes back to a comment that you were making earlier, which is, we're in this regime now where IPOs happen much later in company cycles in the United States of America. Like imagine traveling back in time to the 90s and talking about a Series H. People would have been incredibly confused. And all of these, to me, are pointing to greater demand in public markets for companies to be going public earlier.

I think in some way, one of the things that we need to start considering in capital markets and maybe some unsolicited advice to the people in Silicon Valley based on what I hear from my students is if people don't feel like they're participants in these stocks and they instead feel like big company IPOs at extreme valuations are just dumping on retail and the price will just fall, fall, fall, you're going to have a pretty negative reaction to capital markets. Because if you look back at the last batch of call it mega IPOs, the price performance is pretty ugly across those blocks. And notably, they're changing index inclusion rules for SpaceX to get it in there.

So one of the questions this is raising for me is, on one hand, we have pre-IPO price discovery, interesting information is good for markets, I'm a big proponent of that. But on the other hand, we have much later IPO valuations. Right? Like put simply, I have a hard time seeing how somebody is getting a 10-bagger out of Anthropic or SpaceX if they're IPO-ing at a trillion-dollar valuation right out of the gate. I mean, Chris, do you disagree with me on that? What do you think based on that?

I guess over what timeframe? Yeah, okay. This is the law of big numbers. Let me start. In real terms, let's ignore inflation as well. No, fair enough. You're talking about the law of big numbers. And if you IPO in the trillions, a 10-bagger is a lot of trillions. Numbers don't add up.

Right. So I think you're right. Like IPOs shouldn't necessarily be exit liquidity for the rich private equity investors only. Like how do you make a more inclusive market?

私有化的根源：监管成本、SOX 与股东诉讼

Look, I think Chairman Atkins is very focused on this. And I've said it over and over again. 80% of companies in this country with over $100 million of revenue are private. That doesn't seem right.

How do you fix it? Well, you need to make it more accessible. You need to lower the cost for issuers. Like it seems to be better off if you stay private to date. Hopefully that's going to change. But it's really Sarbanes-Oxley. It really crushed issuers, the overhead, the compliance.

By the way, this is true with any market. When you introduce regulation, you increase fixed costs and then you need to drive scale to be able to be successful. And those fixed costs are generally the same for everyone, so the big get bigger. You're going to see that even with the Clarity Act and Genius Act. People are like, oh, Genius Act is going to be amazing. All these companies are going to start building. Well, they may start, but you're going to be only be left with a handful because that fixed cost is going to force consolidation. We see that over and over and over again.

So how do we lower the regulatory cost burden? And people say, oh, you're going to get rid of quarterly reporting. That's going to be terrible for investor protections. I don't know. Is it? Investors have amazing tools.

You're right on the mark. I mean, the reason they created Sarbanes-Oxley was in the wake of Enron and WorldCom. They had the fraudulent reporting. And then Arthur Anderson went away as a result and seed a brutal bear market, back-to-back bear market with a dot-com crash. You can solve a lot of that with technology and transparency, that you couldn't have solved before.

But I agree with all the points. When Amazon, Microsoft, and Netflix went public, they were sub—things went public in the billion-dollar market caps, not $2 trillion market caps. And people are extrapolating in a way they should not. You make your money on the buy. So these entry valuations are way too high.

股东诉讼骚扰与德州方案

I mean, one other thing, though, that I'm keeping an eye on, Chris, on the side of regulatory reform is I agree Sarbanes-Oxley has significantly raised the fixed costs. But the other thing that's been a creeping problem that people have paid less attention to, I think, is shareholder lawsuits, like the amount of nuisance lawsuits and claims and annoyance and just people extracting value out of public companies from the legal community has gotten surprisingly high.

So if you're a small to medium company and you're constantly going to be harassed about this, you know, somebody will be like, "I bought 12 shares and you did this thing wrong. So now pay my lawyers $50 million and I'll collect $12 of that." It just somehow doesn't compute.

So an interesting sort of fix there is what Texas has done. I don't know if anybody has paid attention to this, but they essentially took the Delaware sort of structure, copied and pasted it, and then put in some structural reforms. One of which is you need a minimum percentage of the shares in order to sue as a shareholder. So no more like, Chris, I buy eight shares of your company and then I go sue you over something because I'm just mad and my lawyers want a nuisance payout or like maybe a medium-sized payout. It becomes, you need to be a percentage points shareholder of this company to actually sue.

And I believe, and correct me if I'm wrong on this, we just saw a lawsuit dismissed against Tesla in Texas for this exact reason where you can't just sue on a nuisance basis. It's got to be the actual real deal in Texas.

What a great idea. Sounds amazing.

Yeah. And so I think there are like many areas where being a public company is annoying. And what I would say is, as we look at this from both the state and federal law standpoint, this is one of those where your opinion doesn't matter. The facts are what the facts are. If public companies are choosing not to be public until they are gigantic, something is blocking them earlier in the funnel. And if you want the American people to feel like they get to participate in the economy and reap those gains, we need to find a way to unblock that.

VC 锁定收益的隐藏激励

All right. Well, one other quick point on this, there's another reason they're staying private for longer. The management fees are higher in private markets. You're charging 2% management fee in private markets on a big amount of capital, that's one.

Second thing you can do in private markets is you can engineer your exit and control your exit price. How many VCs have unloaded Stripe shares in a very controlled manner at much higher prices than I can assure you of the multiples of payments companies in trading in public markets? So that helps them too. And then they get incentive fees on all of that.

So the VCs are in on it too. They're smart. They know what they're doing. They can keep growing and plowing capital on these names for longer and also capturing more fees.

But that also becomes a question ultimately of like who's buying it from the VCs, right? And here— Suckers. Suckers. Yeah. I was going to say here ultimately it becomes like retirement funds and pensions and things like that. So we do need a better market structure there.

MicroStrategy 与"Saylor 的三体问题"

All right. So speaking of things that might need a better market structure, let's talk about MicroStrategy. People have been describing this jokingly as Saylor's three body problem.

And what's going on is that for the first time since 2022 (and yes, that is correct for all of you who said strategy sold Bitcoin for the first time ever—you were wrong, they did this before), but for the first time since 2022, strategy started selling some Bitcoin. They sold 32 BTC, largely what looks like mostly a test transaction because that's $2.5 million, per their 8K, to fund distributions on preferred stock.

Now that is less than 0.01% of their holdings, but they chose to sell some BTC rather than drawing the cash reserve. And they sold as Bitcoin fell under 72K on the news.

资本结构与"垃圾袋"之争

Why is this happening? So their capital stack, I will remind everybody, has equity. It then has preferreds, which are the STRCs of the world. That right now, round numbers seems like they need about $1.5 billion a year in dividends. They have $900 million of cash. And the MNAV is basically at parity. So that's not really a good vehicle for issuing more to make the gains work.

Saylor is saying that our goal is to make STRC the best credit instrument in the world. He called the sale, quote, Ram, a "big nothing burger." Ah, yeah. However, you listen to the show. Michael, you should come on and join us. There you go.

Now let's talk about the bears on the other side of this though, who are saying maybe it is a problem. Jeff Dorman was saying $15 billion in prefs have a $1.5 billion a year dividend. He raised $2 billion via stock, then used a bunch of it to buy back 2029 maturity bonds. Someone is going to lose badly here and it will happen in the next four months.

Rich Galvin said that Saylor faces a three body problem. He can't indefinitely support all of equity, Bitcoin and the preferred debt stack. Eventually, they fly too close to the sun.

Bitcoin 作为"注意力资产"的脆弱性

So I'm curious. Let's start with the capital stack component of this. Do you agree with Dorman's point that now we're in a world where Bitcoin prices are not shooting upward? We have differential interests between equity holders, Bitcoin holders and the preferred holders. Like, is that conflict real?

I'll launch in there. I say, you have a three-body problem. I think he's right. Unless Bitcoin is coded to go up in perpetuity, which a lot of investors in that believe. And so they dismiss this issue. If, however, that does not happen, then you have issues like this.

And one of the challenges is that a lot of investors are in Bitcoin because of the velocity. It is an attention asset. I coined that term a few years ago, seeing more of that being used. But here's the issue. There are a lot of assets competing for attention now. Many, many assets are competing for attention. AI is an example of that. A lot of things are going up into the right now too. Biotech is going up into the right. So that makes it harder. That makes it harder for digital assets.

When you're in a momentum market, and this is a momentum market, where two standard deviations into a momentum market and you have an asset that's supposed to have momentum and velocity, and it's not running fast, then it cuts against you. That's what we're seeing now. So this is not good when you sell on, even if it's a small number, it doesn't matter. Perception is reality. It's an attention asset. It's not about the 0.1%.

DAT 的内在矛盾

Yeah, it's a social consensus asset. And one of the largest evangelists of that thesis just sold his assets for the first time. Now, a couple of things. First off, he's a DAT and he's acting like a DAT because a DAT has to act in the interest of its shareholders. And by selling, most stats will tell you that they don't like it. They'll grind their teeth, but the right thing for them to do is to sell their underlying tokens when certain conditions are met.

And I wouldn't say this was necessarily a sale. It was a symbolic, to say, look, I'm a DAT. I need to do certain things to optimize my capital stack. This is a source of funding when I need it. Maybe it's a quest for him to lower his cost basis because he's provided a degree of centralization into an asset that should be totally decentralized. And so by impacting the narrative, by impacting this theme of focus and consensus, you would expect for the price to go lower. So maybe he's, you know, maybe that's fine. Maybe he has some ideas about when he's going to go long again. And, you know, obviously he would never be manipulating markets, but maybe this is going to be an opportunity for him to lower his cost basis in time.

Bitcoin 作为"惰性资产"的根本错配

The other thing that's challenging with Bitcoin (and Ethereum is a little bit different, other assets, Solana, other assets are different, a little different) is Bitcoin is an inert asset. It is not a yielding asset. And so when you start offering to investors a yield on an underlying asset that does not yield, it's hard. And so that's another conundrum that people need to think about.

Now, is selling that asset a way to generate that yield that you're promising? It's one of many. Can you hit the ATM if you have a positive MNAV? Yeah, that's another way. But that underlying conundrum does result in some challenges when you have... 100%. Right? It's a very easy asset liability mismatch, right? Yeah. Like a coupon mismatch.

100%. Whereas like for some of the yielding products, at least you have that organic yield where maybe it's easier to structure cash flows or payments and maybe you have that dollar crypto mismatch, but you're getting a 3, you know, 2.8, 2.7% yield on ETH as an example, like Solana, Avalanche, Hyperliquid, you name it. I don't want to get anyone mad at me if I forget the name of their ecosystem, but it's just a fundamental mismatch in Bitcoin that you don't have in other places.

Now, those who can successfully drive sustainable yield on Bitcoin, that's very powerful. And how many strategies I've seen where people have been trying to generate yield on gold, not easy to do. It's a very similar type of strategy, but the extent somebody can deliver, and this is what he's trying to do, deliver a scalable yielding Bitcoin exposure, I think there's going to be demand for something like that for a long time.

STRC 的 11.5% 数学问题

I think the hard part is the mechanics of the yield though, right? Because if you look at STRC, you have an asset that needs to be paying an 11.5% dividend by Bitcoin. Buying Bitcoin. So the math on that is actually very simple. And to the point Ram was making earlier, it's that if I raise $15 billion of it and I just hold the Bitcoin, if that doesn't go up by 11.5% per year, if I start selling it back, I'm just reducing the amount of Bitcoin that I have, period, full stop.

And so fundamentally, what you're ultimately asking there is, is the price of Bitcoin going to go up more than 11.5% per year. And part of what's interesting about this as well is as Bitcoin has gotten larger and larger over time as a market cap asset, just as one would expect, you're seeing a reduction in the rate of gains, right? Back to the law of large numbers problem. It's a lot easier to get a hundred bagger with a $10 million asset than with a $1 trillion asset. And so I think a lot of the people who have been parameterizing this based on historical Bitcoin performance have not normalized that for the current size of Bitcoin.

I just wonder, like, if we're not staring down the barrel of very significant inflation, like, what is the game plan to continue paying that dividend and not sort of stripping Bitcoin off the balance sheet over time?

优先股折价与硅谷银行类比

Yeah. Both are selling below par now, STRC and STRF, and they sold off today. That's not good to see. Now, if you're selling Bitcoin to generate cash, the idea would be to create confidence for the providers of your debt financing. So one would expect that these would close closer to par, but they did not. So if those assets sold off, that means the cost of financing actually went higher. Christen also knows what that means. That's not good. Yes. That's not good.

You know, like if you're at a bank, right? What's a good analogy here, Austin? I would say a good analogy for a bank is that you have a bunch of loans on the balance sheet that are paying you 6% and you've watched short-term rates doing this inexorably while you're still getting paid 6. There comes a point where if rates go up enough, you just die. And if you don't believe me, the name of that bank was Silicon Valley Bank. Like this has happened in the not too distant past. You have an asset liability mismatch.

The other issue we have time and time again in our industry is this, the arbitrage between equity and token where it's hard to buy a token. It's hard for people to buy a token. And so they go in through an asset class that's easy. The trust, an equity, and that works sometimes when there's more demand, but when there's less demand maybe it can be sold faster as well. So it works in both directions. It's that basis, you know, last cycle was one of the core challenges with the bear market that we entered with that basis between equity and token. A lot of people died on that hill. You can go through some of the names, but interesting times, interesting times.

三大障碍：监管、安全、风险管理

Yeah. So you've got a couple issues right now. Number one, you've got a regulatory issue, because it doesn't have all the licenses on shore and therefore it keeps a lot of institutional market out. Second issue you have is security, right? When you have these open source, open protocols, bad guys want a piece of them. And so we've seen a lot of challenges. So that's the additional risk that's keeping institutions out that has to get solved. The way you solve that is twofold. There's a number of different techniques coming on side. There's policy things we can do. I've been talking about. I love my privateers. Got to get that in in this episode. I think AIs could actually help with that as well.

But I also think that we talk about the DeFi mullet, right? Where you have the business in the front and then the protocol in the back. The extent you have that business in the front who is a throat to choke for those institutions and can deliver that back in liquidity and take the risk in certain cases and get insurance for that risk, that's one way to do it.

So anyway, that's all coming together. The last thing that needs to be solved for these protocols like Hype, like you described, is risk management. So we cannot in traditional markets take counterparty risk on derivatives. Your hedge has to be there when the world blows up. We saw many of the crypto markets, sexes, indexes, literally like kill people with this thing called ADL. We've had long conversations on the show about it.

Well, guess what? That's something that you can fix if you're a Hyperliquid or Lighter or somebody else. You could definitely fix it. And you can build decentralized pools. And frankly, based on what I'm seeing, they're doing it.

And so now if you're Jeff Sprecher, you're stepping back, you're like, wow, they can really, they can probably create waterfalls as good as I can do, like meet those principles. By the way, you want to—it doesn't take a lot of capital to outpace the traditional exchanges and the amount that they put in the waterfall. They put tiny amount in, they send it to everybody else. So you can easily go, "Hey, here's my waterfall. Here's your waterfall. I'm actually better." And I think they're thinking about that right now because that's a big issue.

Licenses, you can buy them. Front ends can buy them. So if you're in traditional markets, it's a massive wake up call. You have a very, you don't have a regulator who's trying to attack you. You have one who's trying to keep the game level and letting innovators build. It's just sad for me, when I go to these websites and it's like, "Hey, you're American, you can't access it." It kills me because I'm like, I was there when we put these rules in place. And it's like, "Oh, these are designed to protect the world, protect everybody." But I don't know. ADL 与零售保护

I would say, I think if we're looking at hype in particular and trying to figure out what do they need to fix the most to get to the United States, I'm going to ignore, call it the paperwork aspects of regulation for a moment and say the big one is figuring out something better than ADL.

We can see what that did to crypto trading activity post 10/10. It's been ugly out there. But more to the point, if we have this like direct to retail model, going straight to ADL is never a thing that I think regulators are going to be comfortable with. And I don't just think the United States, Europe will have a negative reaction to that as well, because if like, let's say, Ram, rather than running a firm, you're just one guy. That means occasionally you need to, oh, I don't know, sleep. And so things go dramatically down overnight while you're out. You've got institutions managing 24/7, but you get liquidated and then ADL'd. Nobody's going to find that situation to be fair. So as Chris said, we're going to need capital. We're going to need better methods of doing this. We may need to, I don't know, use less than 100% X leverage at all times in crypto. So I think there are structural questions.

Before you go on to Cloud or OpenAI and try to figure out, ADL is—it's auto-deleveraging. So essentially you have a position—It is not the American Defamation League, that's correct. That's right. You have a position on, you're hedging your book. You need that derivative to be afloat. You're meeting your margin calls, whatever. When the exchange is just like, "sorry guy, I'm ripping it up." There's too much risk in the system and you've lost your hedge.

That actually exists in traditional markets. We call it recovery and resolution, but it's at the very bottom. It's like right before the tanks are on the street and the world blows up and there's massive pools of capital in front of it, risk-based pools of capital that really insulate you from that. So it almost never happens. When I was in trad markets, I would see in certain cases, you get into a couple of those tranches, like there's an issue in NASDAQ many years back, but I've never seen us get to that point, particularly post-financial crisis.

You need to be able to know that your hedge is going to be there for you to have those robust derivatives markets. I think it's coming together. I think it's coming together across DeFi. I think even in lending markets, as you're seeing what went on right now with Aave and those markets, you're going to start seeing like they didn't send, to my knowledge, risk back to the underlying holders. They're figuring it out. They're creating those pools that will help provide more confidence across DeFi. So I think that's happening, Austin.

银行业的"代沟"与代际敌意

I think one of the things that I'm noticing here is the bank lobby has gotten incredibly old and out of touch, because they don't understand the impact that they're having on the debate right now.

Like one, if you're in a large bank or most community banks in the United States, I want to be very clear, young Americans hate you. And I say that because if you look at this, SoFi has opened more accounts over the last few years than all of the big four combined, right? They don't like you guys. They view you as a bunch of paternalistic dickheads who crashed the system in 2008. I'm quoting one of my students when I say that, right? Just to be clear.

Two, you're in a position where you're arguing things that are factually wrong. And the people who are informed on finance understand this. Anybody saying that US dollar stablecoins cause deposits to leave the banking system in aggregate does not understand how bank deposits work. It is not mechanically possible for that to happen. Bank deposits are destroyed when you take money out of an ATM, when you repay a loan, when a bank sells an asset off the balance sheet, or a couple of things the Treasury and the Fed can do. None of those involve a stable coin. Unless you think buying a sandwich destroys a bank deposit, you don't think buying a stable coin or creating one destroys a bank deposit.

And then finally, and Chris, you'll laugh at this in terms of the boomerang coming back around on banks. And do we maybe need to go back to the way things worked before? I don't know that that's the answer, but I am going to warn the banks, you can't have your cake and eat it too. 存款利率为何如此之低：G-SIB 与隐性补贴

I think that's right. I think in time it'll—you know, there'll be stasis. And the banking market, the lobby is much bigger.

I think right now, if you know, apples to apples, the problem, there's a bunch of problems going on here. Number one, savings rates, checking rates are too low. Why should you get yield like zero on your checking account? That's a problem. And look at the bank earnings. Bank earnings are doing just fine. In fact, they're killing it. And so as rates went up, they didn't raise rates. And that's what's angering your students. They're keeping it all.

So why are they low? The reason why they're low, if you go to a small community bank, that's to compete with demand deposit interest rates, they offer higher rates. JP Morgan on the longer end of the deposit curve and say the CD market, they offer competitive rates with other banks. It's because large corporates that are well above the FDIC threshold and individuals that have deposits well above the $20,000 threshold, because they're not protected by the US government, they're trusting fortress balance sheets like JP Morgan and JP Morgan has a too-big-to-fail G-SIB status. And therefore the deposit rate is low.

Part of that is because they have G-SIB status. That's the issue. So the US is conferring the status. They're in the window of protection as opposed to having a competitive private market insurance offering like where Berkshire Hathaway say underwriting depository insurance at a higher level. So government's creating its own issue. Government is creating the reason why the top points are zero.

I think what you're saying is that you're getting those lower rates because you have a fortress balance sheet, because your money is safe, because you're depending on that. Because you're G-SIB, you're depending. But the fact of the matter is, it's a receipt on a fractionally reserved entity, but you think there's a guarantee, implicit or explicit. You think there's a guarantee, right? Correct.

But then you look at it next to a stable coin or money market fund for that matter, that's fully backed and paying a much higher rate. Here's your efficient frontier. Right. And that's the disconnect.

And so I hate when they also say apples to apples. "Well, we have more regulation, we have more cost." Well, that's because you're completely different business, right? You're not fully backed if you're fully—so it's, I found those arguments disingenuous.

But the biggest challenge is, dude, if you kill Clarity and you're so happy because stablecoin is not fixed, guess what? Genius is still the law of the land. It's going to take an act of Congress to unwind that loophole. So is that the solution?

I think you're going to see a number of other integrations that are going to be equally exciting. So it's great. But what does this mean? This means that, guys, I think Citi put out a paper today if you check it out. I think they said there's gonna be $5 trillion in tokenized equities by 2030 or something. I don't know. I think they're like way too light.

I think that we've talked about this—this is the next phase of markets beyond electronification. So we went from voice manual paper to electronification. Now we're going to go to blockchain-based finance. 24/7, 24/7 price discovery, real-time settlement. This is the obvious next iteration.

And as a fiduciary, which I am, if I'm going to trade a tokenized product or a non-tokenized product and there's sufficient liquidity, I got to trade the tokenized product, guys. Equity is $127 trillion market. I think DTCC has trillions, trillions, you know, that it safeguards and tracks. So I think it's going to be like kind of like steady, steady, steady, then all at once everything's going to go tokenized because we won't have a choice. So much, much more to follow. We're like in the first chapter of this thing. And I can't wait to watch it happen.

Ram：稳定币作为对国债的需求

Ram, what do you make of the fact that U.S. treasuries was in that announcement? Because as we think about backtracking to the global macro implications of this, certainly I've been surprised. I haven't heard news out of the US Treasury about tokenizing treasuries given the distribution possibility of stable coins, but I saw DTCC talking about it here and I assure you they can talk to treasury if they want to. I was curious what you made of that.

He's on mute. Ram, you're on mute. Oh, sorry.

So yeah, Scott Bessent rightly views stable coins as a demand for treasuries. So if he can create more plumbing infrastructure, that helps the US government finance its debt. So it's a smart move and makes sense that that'll go on chain.

稳定币的"效用解锁"难题

Can I jump into this one? Because one of the challenges right now with stablecoins is that they're, I don't want to say it in a bad way, but in a way they're like kind of a debt asset because they're not yielding. They're not money market funds. You have to deploy them into DeFi to get a yield. And in many cases, they lack utility.

Now, the real unlock for stablecoins is when you have utility, when you can buy stuff with them. I don't know how many times you've been speaking to your normal friends and you're like, we'll meet you down. If you could pay stable coins for that coffee, then you got something. But we're not there yet. We're missing it.

One thing I would love to see the Treasury do is to allow purchases of US treasuries—it doesn't have to be tokenized—with stablecoins. That'd be a really nice fundamental unlock where the US government's showing, hey, these things do have utility. We will accept them for the payment of either traditional or tokenized treasuries amongst primary dealers. I think that is the next foundational unlock that I'm looking for.

What do you think, Austin? We get it?

I think the US government is slow to do anything, but I think it's inevitable. Like if we're going to continue spending at this level and running a deficit, you need financing and you're going to need to go where the money is. So I do think it will eventually happen. The only other option is to dramatically cut the budget. And I just don't see much appetite for that.

结尾

So, all right. On that note, since we are at time here, let me say thank you as always for joining us for this episode of Bits and Bibs. We'll be back in one week to discuss more about how the worlds of crypto and macro are colliding.

Don't forget what we told you at the start of this episode about looking into our new channels and subscribing. So until then, everyone, take care.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、"棘轮效应"背后的认知不对称：市场已学会单向遗忘
Sosnick 提到的"ratchet effect"——市场对波斯湾每一条积极消息都上涨，对随后的破灭却不下跌——表面看是乐观偏差，实际暴露了一个更深刻的结构性现象：**当代市场的"情绪记忆"已经被算法和被动资金切除了下半部分**。
意外的观察是：这与神经科学中的"享乐适应"（hedonic adaptation）原理惊人相似，但方向是反的。人类大脑对负面刺激比正面刺激更敏感（损失厌恶），但今天的市场恰恰相反——它对正面消息累积加仓，对负面消息选择性失忆。原因是**被动资金的"无脑买入"机制让市场失去了下调的肌肉记忆**。这其实是 ETF 化的一个隐藏副作用：当 60% 的资金不读新闻，市场就只剩下半边脸。
## 二、Anthropic IPO 与"反向 10-bagger 困境"：硅谷正在杀死自己的合法性
节目里有一段被快速带过但极其重要的洞察——当 IPO 估值从 90 年代的"十亿级"飙升到今天的"万亿级"，**散户在数学上已经不可能获得 10 倍回报**。这看起来是个估值问题，但本质是一个政治问题。
跨领域

item.title: [Latent Space] Inside xAI: Building Grok Imagine in 3 Months, Videogen vs World Models, and Video Agents— Ethan He
item.dc:creator: Latent Space
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个让人意外的反直觉发现：视觉模型的智能，主要来自语言

Ethan 抛出的核心论断极具颠覆性——"视觉智能其实大部分来自语言"。这不只是技术细节，而是对整个生成式媒体行业的"黑色药丸"：

  • 视频扩散模型本身其实"很笨"，它会把"一只猫"逐字翻译成白底静止的猫。
  • 真正让 GPT-Image 显得聪明的，是那个在背后花 3 分钟"思考"、改写、规划构图的语言模型。
  • Cosmos 中的 prompt rewriter 比 7B 的视频模型本身还要大。

这意味着过去十年很多"视觉研究者"的护城河正在被语言模型悄悄吃掉。Ethan 自己作为顶级视觉研究者选择转向 LLM，就是用职业路径在为这个判断下注——这是一种用脚投票的诚实，比任何论文都更有信号价值。

跨领域连接：扩散前端 + 确定性后端 = 软件的"反编译"

最让我"啊哈"的是 Ethan 描绘的未来界面架构： - 现在：用户意图 → LLM 写代码 → 编译成二进制 → 渲染像素 - 未来：用户意图 → 扩散模型 → 像素

这与三个看似无关的领域形成了惊人共振：

  1. 认知心理学：人类输入带宽峰值在视觉，输出带宽峰值在语音——这意味着"talk to AI, AI replies in generative UI"恰好匹配人类 I/O 的生理极限。在 Neuralink 之前，这是带宽天花板。
  2. 编译器历史：我们花了 50 年把高级语言编译到低级机器码，现在 ML 用一步映射"短路"掉了整个中间表示层。这跟 AlphaFold 短路掉传统蛋白质折叠物理模拟、跟端到端自驾短路掉感知-规划-控制管线，是同一种现象。
  3. MP4 vs VAE 的悖论：MP4 是人类工程师为视频设计的最优压缩，但模型却学不会在 MP4 的潜空间里思考——必须重新发明一个"对模型友好"的 VAE。这暗示了一个更深的真相：为人类设计的抽象，对机器智能反而是噪声。这与"为什么 LLM 的 prompt 不是 SQL 而是自然语言"是同构问题。

隐藏的系统模式：所有"硬编码"最终都会被学习吃掉

把对话中分散的几个细节串起来，会看到一条贯穿整集的隐线：

领域 早期硬编码（启发式） 终局（学习化）
视频上下文管理 FramePack 手工金字塔压缩 模型自主选择性记忆
Agent harness Claude Code 的 8 种 pruning 规则 模型自我感知上下文用量
图像 prompt "ultra HD, 4K, masterpiece" 标签拼贴 自然语言意图
Diffusion 步数 固定 100 步 / 1000 步 Step distillation 学到 1-8 步
视频剪辑 人类用 FFmpeg 后期 Video agent 调用 FFmpeg 作为工具

这是 Rich Sutton "The Bitter Lesson" 的最新一轮验证——但有个惊人的反转：Ethan 暗示连"模型本身"也会被吃掉，被更大的语言智能 + 工具调用所吞噬。视频模型沦为 agent 工具链上的一个 callable function，就像 1980 年代的专家系统沦为今天 LLM 的子例程。

这就解释了他离开 XAI 的真正逻辑：当你看到护城河会被向上一层吞没时，最理性的选择不是加固护城河，而是搬到上一层去。这是一种"反向梯度下降"——不向局部最优，而向产业的 attention head 移动。

一个被忽视的隐喻：时间感知 = 智能的下一个相变

对话末尾那个看似随意的观察其实极有分量："LLM 估算任务时长，给你 12 小时的答案，自己花两天还没做完。" 这不是 bug，是缺失维度。

视频模型必须强制学会"时间"才能做音视频对齐（毫秒级），但语言模型完全没有内在时钟。一旦 LLM 通过 video/audio modality 内化了真实时间的连续性，可能解锁的不只是更好的视频，而是真正的长程规划能力——因为规划本质上就是关于时间的推理。

视频生成可能不是终点，而是 LLM 获得"时间器官"的训练场。这是一个出人意料的方向：robotics 不需要真实身体，因为 video LM 已经在屏幕录像里学会了世界的时间结构。

📝 转录文本

开场：一个颠覆性的论断

"I have a pretty big claim—the visual intelligence are actually mostly coming from language. Like these video models, especially from now since the diffusion model technology is more mature, every time you see there's some improvement on these models, I would say mostly the gain comes from language model, not coming from the video model itself."

主持人致辞

Before we get into today's episode, I just have a small message for listeners. Thank you. We would not be able to bring you the AI engineering, science, and entertainment content that you so clearly want if you didn't choose to also click in and tune into our content. We've been approached by sponsors on an almost daily basis. But fortunately, enough of you actually subscribe to us to keep all this sustainable without ads, and we want to keep it that way.

But I just have one favor to ask all of you. The single most powerful, completely free thing you can do is to click that subscribe button. It's the only thing I'll ever ask of you, and it means absolutely everything to me and my team that works so hard to bring Latent Space to you each and every week. If you do it, I promise you, we'll never stop working to make the show even better. Now, let's get into it.

嘉宾介绍与社区缘起

Okay, we're here in the studio with Ethan He, most recently of XAI. Welcome.

Yeah, thank you. Glad being here.

We're also here with Vibhu. You were first coming to us or joining the Latent Space world because you were working on Cosmos and Nvidia and you did a great paper. We loved it. You presented it as well. So thank you for doing that.

Yep. Ethan also presented the MoEs.

Yes. Twice at Latent Space.

How did you actually hear about us? Did we reach out to you?

No, actually I realized—oh, there's this online community where people talk about AI and also learn from each other through papers every week through the paper club. It's very nice. I learned a lot.

Three years non-stop. We haven't stopped even on Christmas and New Year's. Many weeks I want to stop.

No, good.

I think you had posted that you worked on a paper and I was like, "Oh, very cool." We have paper club presented, but I might have reached out to you after.

Yeah, because it's an amateur club, right? So it's very unusual, but we have sometimes paper authors come by and actually explain the paper. Today we just did the Poolside paper which is apparently very good. Came out yesterday. Pretty interesting, right? Fully open. They talk about everything—system. So it's a good one. We'll recommend people to read it.

从 Nvidia Cosmos 到 XAI 的转型

Bring us up to speed on your transition to XAI because I actually don't even know when you joined. Just tell us the story about the transition.

Before XAI I was working on Cosmos world model at Nvidia. So Cosmos is the giant video foundation model that aims to simulate the world and serves as a foundation for all of the roboticists to build on top of. Once I built Cosmos one, I realized this thing also has a scaling law similar to language models. We need to scale up the video models further. That's why I realized I need to move to somewhere with much more compute resources. That's how I—

Then Nvidia GPU came themselves.

Yeah.

And timeline-wise, when was Cosmos? It was pretty early, right? It was open world model paper.

It was like end of 2024.

End of 2024.

Yeah. Then at mid 2025 I moved to XAI. At that time I joined by the time when XAI was about to build video models and multimodal models. There were no infra, no data, and no model. And just as a few engineers, we built it in three months and released the first model, Grok Imagine 0.9.

Since then I keep working on video models and moved more from pre-training to post-training of the video models—for example, reference-to-videos kind of like the cameo feature, and video extensions. And now, before I left, I worked—uh, world model leading a small team to focus on real-time long-horizon video generation.

三个月从零到一：团队、迭代与小 bug

Can you give a rough roadmap? You're on a brand new team. Grok previously was only text, or they partnered with BFL for their image gen stuff. What are the building blocks? You have compute, data, you can procure somewhere—what's the sequence of things people should think about when setting up a new team?

I mean actually even deeper—not just data you can procure. You guys had to go through getting the data too, right? You shipped it pretty fast. 3 months is surprisingly fast.

One thing I'd say: thanks to my experience at Nvidia, the first time when we were building Cosmos together, we built it for about a year. So this is the second time I do it—roughly have an idea what to do.

I'd say the most important thing is talent. Everyone was very strong and clever, very close with each other towards a common goal. So that sped up things a lot. You reduce the communication bandwidth among people and everyone can work toward the same goal. Every day there's not that much meetings on the calendar—maybe like a sync a day, and after that it's just all building. It was pretty fun at that time.

Another thing is that XAI has very strong foundations of data infra, model infra, and the supporting infra. That can help the model development a lot. When I look at training models, the top important thing is how many iterations can you do per day. The more iterations you can do, the faster you can train the model. So if you have very strong infra and a lot of compute, you can train these models in a very short period of time. That gives you a much larger buffer for errors and also gives you the opportunity to spot more bugs.

What is an iteration? Is it like a few hundred steps?

Let's say just training the model—acquire new data and maybe design new algorithms and train a new model maybe at a smaller scale.

Cycle time for any hyperparam?

Yeah, cycle time end-to-end to evaluate this model: is this model better than my previous iteration?

So before, someone had already set this up so that you can iterate very quickly.

Yeah. The foundation there is extremely good for developing and researching models. And often I find this is kind of boring, but a lot of the improvements do not come from new algorithms. They come from finding small bugs here and there in the data pipeline, in the model training pipeline. Those gave the biggest boost to the model quality.
