It's interesting, right? You say it's a small team, less communication bandwidth, but also a lot of quality is finding little bugs. It seems counterintuitive—you have a lot of people, you can iron out more of those. But it's interesting to see the other side.

编码模型作为加速器，计算又重新成为瓶颈

I also wonder—did you try using LLMs to look for bugs? I remember at that time it was mid-2025, so the coding model wasn't quite there yet. I remember December 2025 it was extremely good.

I've been using it. At that time it was helpful sometimes, but it produced code that was kind of difficult to maintain. Even though the first time it builds something extremely fast, it gave like spaghetti code—thousands of lines that I couldn't maintain, and the LLM itself couldn't figure out what's wrong and how to improve on top of it. But now I find it much, much better.

I want to bring up another point: now coding models are much more efficient and can help us implement stuff much faster. Compute might become a bottleneck again. Previously, if you wanted to train a new model—say you want to generate new synthetic data and write a new algorithm—it might take a few weeks, and during that period you might not have experiments to run. But now you can build that thing within a few hours, then you can immediately train a model. Now you have to have enough compute to try all of the ideas. So compute might be the bottleneck of iteration speed again.

I actually honestly think it's kind of a stressful job because you're like, "Well, I should be trying everything, and if I'm not, then I'm not doing my job well."

I mean, there's also the stress of you're eating thousands of GPUs per hour, which is very expensive, and compute can go down. The researchers—

Daddy Elon.

It was. But there's still a finite amount of compute. You want to use it, you want to use it well, you want more of it.

That was quite stressful indeed. One thing is, with coding models now, a lot of these jobs can be automated, which is much better. Second, it's a marathon, so you've got to maintain good health and a regular schedule.

It's hard to hear that when you ship from zero to nothing in 3 months.

标准流程：从图像模型到视频模型

One thing I did want to dive into—in the notes you sent ahead of time, you had specific comments about the cost of videogen training. Presumably this is on Colossus 1, right? The 300 megawatt cluster.

There's three things you're talking about, right? There's videogen, there's also the image gen model that you put out. So zero to one, you have a few months—just what are the stages of creating image?

Oh yeah, maybe I got distracted. Sorry.

第一步：合成语言-视频对

I can't comment specifically on how I did it, but it's a quite standard process. I can draw some examples from Cosmos. Building a video model, you actually need to build an image model first. And building these two models, the data you need is 100% synthetic pairs of language and image, or language to video—because on the internet, videos don't naturally associate with text.

You can say, "Oh, on YouTube you have the title, description, and comments of a video, but usually they're not relevant to the video itself." Say the video is a natural scene of mountains, and the title is "I'm so happy today." They have no correlation at all. So the first step is you have to generate synthetic pairs of language with videos. You get videos from the internet and use a VLM to caption them.

Here's a question—how do you get the VLM to begin with? If there's no VLM, how do you generate the text to begin with? It's impossible.

In the beginning it's: you ask humans to describe the video as detailed as possible. For example, you ask them to describe all objects, all characters, and all interactions and dialogues. In the protocol of Cosmos labeling, the objective we gave to the labelers was that you have to describe the video as detailed as possible such that a blind person, sharing a blob of text, can reconstruct what the video is like from their head.

Video or image?

Either. This was pretty common when we went from CLIP and DALL·E—it's all training on really detailed captioning of images. Same applied to video, but instead of using multimodal models to pass in video or images and write rich descriptions—

I think there's this traditional perspective of supervised, very highly human-created. I feel like there's an unlock with unsupervised, where you have enough to bootstrap that you can just throw common corpus on it. Unsupervised vision and language pairing, where you just have interspersed image and text and it just learns. To me, that is the VLM breakthrough that is different from CLIP, different from the pre-LM era.

Yeah, it's interesting to see that you kind of need both data.

For example, for you to bootstrap it up.

For generative model training, there's also usually a small percentage of unlabelled data. So the model is instructed to generate a video without any text instruction. That can also help the model generalize.

第二步：训练 tokenizer / VAE

After this stage of synthetic pair generation, one important common step is to train a compressor or tokenizer of the image or videos. Theoretically you can train image or video models on pure pixels, but the problem is that's a lot of tokens. One image, 1000 by 1000, is 1 million tokens, 1 million pixels. It's impossible to train transformers on that. So you need to train a tokenizer which can go from image to latent space and latent space back to image.

That's why we named the podcast.

Exactly.

But basically you're talking about vocabulary size. A million is impossible.

In generative models, the vocab is continuous. It's a continuous space. You can think about it like you map an image to a vector—a fixed-length vector of like 16 or 48. And then you map that vector back to the image space. The mapping is patch-based. You have a 16x16 patch and you map that patch of pixels into this latent space.

We've covered this—VAE. You basically compress your input, do your generation, do your reasoning all in smaller dimension and then project back out.

VAE is a form of compression, but for me the patching thing is from ViT, right? Literally the paper is titled "16 by 16 is all you need," something like that.

And then I think also people make a lot of comparisons with this kind of patching with convolutions.

Yes. You are kind of reconstructing the old paradigm with the new.

Actually in VAE there are both convolution networks and transformers. You can actually do both.

第三步：训练扩散 transformer

After this, what you've got is latent space tokens and language tokens. Now the training of the diffusion transformer. Usually generative models use diffusion transformers. It's actually quite standard. It's very similar to how you train language transformer models. There's not that much difference—it's just visual tokens in, visual tokens out.

The only difference is there's a denoising process. You train the model to unmask some of the noise. You add random noise to the visual tokens and then train the model to remove that noise to generate clean tokens. In inference, the model can iteratively remove noise from 100% noise.

And then to speed things along on the tech tree of diffusion, there's CFG, and there's also I guess latent diffusion. Somewhere along the line, stability and all these other guys pioneered a lot of this architecture. I don't know if you want to get into that or just do the video side.

为什么图像模型是视频模型的基础

After you train such an image model, the reason it's a foundation for video models is that image models are cheaper to train and they have much denser connection between language and images. For example, you train on a billion images and there's a mapping from text to image. The cost to train the same—a billion text to a billion videos—that's much more expensive, because videos naturally have more tokens than images.

Because the diffusion models' understanding of language purely comes from this mapping—if you don't have enough mapping, if you only train on 10 million videos, you might not see enough language tokens in your training. So your model does not understand human intention enough. That's why you usually first train this image diffusion model and then bootstrap the video model from there.

视频压缩：MP4 vs VAE，帧压缩 vs 时序压缩

One thing I did want to ask—I think you're the first video model person I've ever talked to. We've talked to Luma and all those folks. There's all these tricks in video compression where basically frame by frame there's not that much difference. So actually you don't have to regenerate or resave the whole frame. I think MP4 compression or something else like that. Is it tempting to use that, or as far as I can tell everyone just treats it as "no, we will just generate every frame." Is that roughly the state of the art?

There are a few different approaches. First, you want to just directly use MP4 compression as tokens for the transformers to train. People have actually tried that, but the main challenge is the latent space for MP4 tokens was not very comprehensible for the models. It's extremely hard to train on that. That's why we created VAEs which create more continuous latent space so the models can understand and learn from it much easier.

Even within VAEs there are different difficulties of latent space. The simplest, most naive way: you have an image and you just shuffle all of the images into a vector. So you don't need to train any VAE—but that space is extremely hard for models to train on top of. That's why there's some debate on how do you compress the tokens.

You can compress frame by frame, or you can compress the temporal dimension. The difference is: if you compress the temporal dimension, you get a much higher compression rate, because there's temporal redundancy between frames—this frame and the last frame are mostly similar. There's only some small difference.

For example, in Wan 2.1 they have like 8 by 8 by 4 compression rate. So four temporal tokens are compressed into one token. That can save a lot of context length. If you do it frame by frame, you have to do maybe 8 by 8 by 1—your context length will be four times larger.

That being said, the benefit of frame-per-frame compression—we might come back to this later—is real-time-ness and interactivity. If you stream the output of the model frame by frame, the model can respond to any user requests immediately. If you have temporal 4x compression, then—

It might be laggy.

Yeah, there's a lag there in nature.

实时生成的前沿应用：Flipbook 与 Neuro-OS

So you're very pilled on this. Let's go ahead and bring it up. There's some frontier applications of real-time video gen. So Flipbook is one of the examples that went viral recently. What is Flipbook?

Flipbook：生成式网页浏览器

Flipbook is kind of like a web browser. It has the web browser UI on top. The difference is all of the UIs are generated by a generative image model in real time, and anything here is fake. But you can explore inside this imaginary world. Here we have "engineering the grid pyramid"—the model generated this for us to understand how it works. If we want to navigate around and understand further, we can click on some of the descriptions here and the model will generate a new sub-page describing the details we want to know.

So it's basically—we are playing a video, but it's pausing for our next interaction and then it just plays the next thing based on our interaction. Which is kind of cool.

Yes. You kind of decide your story.

The demo tweet had more animation between frames.

I think it's just skipping a lot of frames. They also have a video mode. There's a live video stream.

生成式 UI：未来的最终形态

This is an example of the kind of future you see at the extreme. We're obviously not in it today, but in a world where inference is completely free—

This is better than generating code in text.

Yeah. This is the final state of VR. Imagine internet doesn't exist and then you type in google.com—what should a model show you? The model can imagine something, and this is what the model imagines. These web pages completely do not exist.

I think as inference cost comes down, we're going to have generative UI for everything. If you think about how the coding model works—they write code for a web page, render the code, might be converted into binary, and the binary renders the pixels on the screen. In machine learning, every time we have some breakthrough, obviously it's more end-to-end. So why don't we have user instruction to the pixel directly?

The generated UI will be user intention to pixels directly. Even if I want email—let's say everyone has the same interface, but I want it slightly different. I want the email to show like a TikTok so I can swipe left and right for emails. Or maybe you want something else. Or I'm looking at Instagram Stories and I don't like the like button—I always misclick it, so generate the UI without it.

It's going to be a revolutionary replacement of the interface. In the future we might have much more powerful LLM and coding models running behind the scenes, and in the front end the diffusion model will actually be the front end to show stuff to you. That's how I imagine it.

Diffusion front end, deterministic back end.

Yes, something like that.

I find it very expensive but, you know.

I find it interesting—you called LLMs writing code on the back end deterministic. Okay, you write it once, compile, and then you execute.

If you think about the cost—say H100 costs $1 per hour, and if you use this 8 hours a day, 30 days—every month you're paying $240. You likely don't want to pay for that. That's even more expensive than Claude Code Max. But if you think about compute costs coming down 2x every year, I think that future will likely arrive.

Compute cost comes down, compute gets faster, model gets smarter, model gets smaller.

I don't know why you say 2x because I think it's like 100x. In language models, it is roughly 100 to 1,000 times every 12 to 18 months for the same given level of LLM ELO.

That's net of everything, right? Model performance alongside compute. Different than just compute cost coming down. But, a very interesting future.

For the web designers—shout out that accessibility is an issue, right? How do you deal with screen readers? But yes, this is higher bandwidth storytelling than anything you can possibly generate with code.

I'd like to add—humans naturally have the maximum bandwidth when looking at things, looking at videos. And we also have maximum output bandwidth when we are talking. So in the future it might be: we talk to AI models and the AI model responds back with generative UI. That will be the maximum input and output bandwidth to interact with AI models before Neuralink happens.

It's also very custom, right? Some people are very visual, some are not as visual—they prefer text. But the best thing about generative UI: it can also be text.

Neuro-OS：生成式操作系统

There's another project we wanted to highlight—the Neuro-OS. Kind of similar idea, but here you're literally simulating an operating system with a video model. You can play Doom, you can run Firefox. I find this like mildly less impressive obviously, because it's an OS that I can run. But here everything is imagined.

I was used to Command-W to close the Firefox tab. It didn't crash. It's too immersive. I wanted to close the tab. But yes, I can play generated Doom.

This is shockingly fast.

I remember there was a demo about maybe one or two years ago—someone tried to do a first-person shooter with an image model. There was no consistency, it was very slow. But here, realistically, this is Doom.

I think there's two sides to that. What is running a game? The heavy part of it is actually the game engine—all the lighting, all the graphics. This is just kind of video, right? We've solved consistency. This is still a few years old image generation. There's some temporal consistency, but it's kind of just images stitched together as frame video. But it's a good visual representation to picture the future you want to see.

This reminds me of how the video models get better and better. Neuro-OS, if you just look at it, feels like a crappy version of the Windows we could have. The difference is, this model is overfitted on the existing operating systems. It can generate nothing different than that.

But it's also similar to video models. When we're training these video/image models on the internet, there's no imaginary supernatural stuff on the internet. But once we train this model, you can prompt the model to generate something supernatural that has never existed in the dataset. So if you train your Neuro-OS or neuro-computer on the standard screen recordings on the entire internet, the model can imagine completely new interfaces to interact with a computer.

This is one of those things that is magical to me. Usually generalizing out-of-distribution is bad, but somehow we have learned some kind of internal world model.

You say "this, but it looks like rainbows and butterflies"—it'll do it and it'll kind of make sense.

视频模型训练的成本剖析

I do want to touch a little bit more on the model architecture stuff. One of the papers we covered—we've covered every annual Segment Anything release. I don't know if you follow—you're a computer vision guy, so you know. They did memory attention which is kind of interesting. I always think anything where you can across the temporal dimension keep some consistency is very fascinating.

Does the CV side bleeding into videogen side—I think it is underexplored. We talked about it for labeling, but you can borrow the architecture itself.

There are also completely different approaches. You brought up the term "world model." We went from video model to world model. There is diffusion, but there's also other approaches that people are doing.

One thing we should comment back on: we were talking about the steps to train image gen to video model. One thing we don't see as much of is, okay, you brought up the delta in training data. A video model might not generalize. But what is the cost of training a large video model?

For LLMs roughly, the Poolside thing that came out today is a Gemma-level model trained on roughly 40 trillion tokens at this many H200s over this much time. You can see the exact cost. How many GPU hours over how much H200 cost. How do we do the back-end math for video models, image models?

存储成本：被忽视的大头

I can share some back-of-envelope calculation. Surprisingly, video models—the cost is comparable to language models. Maybe like medium-scale language models.

Just storing the videos alone costs a lot. You can look up on AWS. Say if you have a billion videos and each video is 5 megabytes, then you need 5 petabytes to just store those videos. And remember we talked about you use a VAE to compress the videos—you also need to store those continuous features, which is also comparable in size with the videos themselves. So just storing these videos and features is tens of petabytes alone.

I just looked up the calculation. 5 petabytes on S3 Standard is $100k per month.

And then $200k. Even more expensive is you have the ingress and egress through the internet—you have to download those videos. I believe it's more expensive on AWS than just storing those videos. Each training run you probably need to pull them once. If you train multiple times, it's even more than that. So just storing on the network, the cost is a few millions per month—not to mention the GPU cost.

My side tangent: the compute rental, GPU rentals very efficient. But you can be XAI and build your data center. Should we not just build our storage compute as well? Cloud cost compared to—especially with egress and stuff—

That's a good idea, but it also comes to its own challenges.

Of course.

People who build GPU data centers might not expect this much storage. People who build storage typically just build it somewhere—there's just CPUs.

I just looked up: AWS only charges for egress, not ingress. Tier five for five petabytes is $230K. Even more expensive. But storing is per month, right? You check in and you cannot check out.

My math is larger than you think.

Yeah. My back-of-envelope math of GPU hours times GPU cost is also very much missing some storage. So you're basically also more I/O bound than normal training.

Yes. In Cosmos we did a lot of optimizations to make it not I/O bound.

GPU 训练成本与开源参考

Speaking of training—the GPU cost. If you look at the open source models, how big these video models are: LTX has 19B parameters, that's a dense model. People are also exploring MoEs—it might be like 20B active and 100B total. That's similar size as medium-size LLM models. If you look at number of tokens, we disclosed that in Cosmos it's tens of trillions of tokens on the visual tokens. Putting this together, the cost of training these video models is actually comparable with LLMs. Not to mention the infra is slightly different from LLM—it might be less efficient to train these models.

推理优化与 step distillation

Do you get the benefits of traditional diffusion speed-up? For images there's LCM, LoRAs for fine-tuning, flow matching—there's a lot of stuff that's been done. Is there overlap that applies to diffusion on the inference side?

The inference side is a completely different story. For the training side it might be a little bit hard to reduce that cost. For the inference side, the biggest gain is from the distillation of these models—step distillation, slightly different from knowledge distillation in LLMs.

Typically for flow matching models you need like 100 steps. Diffusion models even need 1000 steps to generate a good image or video. Step distillation tries to learn to generate few steps from the model itself. It's kind of like: now you use the full model to generate in 100 steps, then you take a model that only generates 10 steps and let that model learn from the perfect one.

Why does this work? Strong to weak seems kind of like strong to weak.

From the modeling perspective, the strong model—the teacher model—is trying to model the images and videos of the entire internet, and that distribution is extremely complex. But the step-distill model is just trying to learn from the teacher. The teacher is a model, and the size is fixed—the distribution is much simpler than the whole internet. That's the intuition I have on why step distillation can work.

Usually these models serve in production—they only run in a few steps. In Cosmos I believe we have 4-step and 8-step. If you do some simpler task like image-to-image translation, it can even run in one step, like Cosmos Transfer.

I think this is the same intuition that guides a lot of the consistency model work. I sent you a link for sCM—I don't know if you covered that. To me, that was actually one of the most impressive papers I've ever seen from OpenAI—the unifying grand concept of consistency models.

There are a few different approaches.

Two steps versus 20, 100 steps, whatever. It's already done.

GAN：被遗忘的原祖步蒸馏

There are a few different approaches—consistency model, and we actually shouldn't forget GAN. GAN was the OG step distillation, because it trained just one step to begin with. A lot of—for example, there's distribution matching distillation which uses GAN as one of the losses for distillation.

GAN just tells you "hey, generate an image" and it has a discriminator to tell: is this image real or not. So the model just needs to learn one of the distributions, not the full distribution. Because in training, the model is asked to reconstruct the ground truth image from the internet, which is extremely hard. When you're training GAN, it's a one-step process: "hey, you generate an image—does this image look as real as the image from the internet?" Which is a much simpler task.

Combining a lot of these approaches together—consistency model, distribution matching, GAN—we can get these few-step models.

音频与视频的联合生成

There's one step I wanted to add, which is audio. And video.

Grok Imagine 0.9 I believe is the first audio-video transformation model deployed at large scale.

And that was your first model.

Yeah, that was Grok Imagine's first model. It's audio-video joint generation. The hard part is the modality alignment. Before this joint model, we have text-to-video alignment—the corresponding text and video. Most of the VLMs understand images and videos. Very rarely do they understand audio. If you look at audio generation on the LLM side, you can talk to them perfectly fine, but if you ask them to sing a song, they're typically not very good. Also they don't have music either.

音频的两难：离散 + 连续

The hard part is that audio actually has two components: a discrete component and a continuous component. The discrete component is like language—when we speak.

It's an ASR issue.

It's text tokens with some characteristics, I would say.

But music—

I think the speech guys would disagree. It's like disfluencies and then—

The music is completely different. It's very continuous, and you cannot model it like discrete tokens in language models. This is the hard part for the model. Not to mention we have to align text, video, and audio together.

So some significant challenges: VLMs cannot understand audio—most cannot. So you have to have some way to do synthetic data generation for audio. You have to caption the model, and that involves synthetic data and human data effort a lot.

Not too surprisingly, most LLMs are very bad at recognizing the beat, tone, and details of music. They can give some general prediction of which song this is, but it's very hard to describe the details of music. Like we mentioned in image generation—you have to describe an image in as much detail as possible so that someone blind can reconstruct it. Here it's like someone deaf can reconstruct how the music sounds like without actually listening to it.

You've got to have all the details of the music and the dialogue.

时间对齐的独特挑战

Is the challenge typically stuff like music and audio, or is there a baseline where there's enough data we can understand narration, conversation, but the nuances in audio are where you hit data issues?

One important thing is alignment. The model has to know the video and audio—it has to have time-based alignment. At which timestep do the video and audio tokens correspond to each other? But we actually don't have these kinds of alignment for most of the other modalities. Text and image, text and video—they are loosely aligned. You can have a description of what's going on in the video, but you don't have exact descriptions like "at timestep 1 second this happened, 2 seconds this happened."

What was the ideal time step? You have to ablate it. Is it like four seconds or something?

That comes down to how you design the model—for the model to be aware of time. So the model is time-aware, and that's something pretty unique.

If you think about LLMs—if you ask an LLM to complete a task, they would say, "Oh, this task will probably take 12 hours." And they come back in one hour—they have already spent two days on this and have exhausted everything. So LLMs themselves don't have a sense of time.

I actually don't think that's just them not having a sense of time. I think it's somewhat based. You tell someone "go work on this feature"—there's a general understanding of how long that would take without LLMs, working LLM speed. Think back two years ago—if I tell you to build a new front end for Latent Space with a search bar, you'll estimate it'll take a few days. So you tell an LLM "go build this"—"it'll take me a few days." It's somewhat grounded. I'm not saying they have a great understanding, but you can see where it comes from. You're trained on all of the text.

They're trying to estimate what a human would say. That's what the data represents.

Then the core on the internet, people have an estimate.

Not even just in direct training samples, but just your world understanding of tokens of how long stuff takes. Go read a book—it'll take you a while. Even if you do nothing but read a book, it takes a few days.

Somewhat a tangent—this is a train of thought I haven't really expressed until now—which is basically that a full world model must also be recursive. The participant in the world model must also be aware that they have a world model. Which is this whole recursive thing down the line. And the world model can be wrong and they need to update it. We've argued this on the newsletter—that there needs to be sort of recursive or adversarial world models.

世界模型的定义

Just to ask: how do you define world models?

For context, we talked about video generation, and then there's a distinction between world models. What's your definition? How do you see the two?

Disclaimer: I'm not going to debate what is a world model—there are many definitions. So I'll just talk about my definition, since I came from the multimodal domain, mainly talking from video.

A world model is real-time, interactive, long-horizon videos. There are three parts:

1. Interaction

A world model should allow you to interact through keyboard, mouse, and maybe also voice. All of the modalities you can interact with, and the model should respond reasonably.

2. Real time

Once you move your mouse—say the world model generates a game—how fast can that game respond? Professional CS:GO players might say "you have to respond in sub-10 milliseconds or even less."

60 fps. Let's go.

300 fps. 500 FPS. Okay—300 FPS, that's 3 milliseconds. You have to respond.

Most of the video models cannot do that. If you have a video model that is say a digital human, the response time might be more generous—maybe 200 milliseconds for real-time voice interaction. That's much more generous, but even 200 milliseconds is pretty tricky.

Remember we mentioned this temporal compression coming from the VAE. If you don't compress the temporal dimension, your sequence length is going to explode. So if you want real-time-ness in your model, you have to deal with long context problems.

3. Long horizon

We are not going to just play with video games for just a few seconds. Most video models are only a few seconds. We're going to play for minutes, hours. The model has to be able to generate long-form content.

Putting these three together: real-time, long-horizon, interactive videos.

I think the final state will be a video version of Flipbook where you can interact with a neuro-computer. You move your mouse and you click on the generative interface, and it will reply through pixels generally in real time. But getting there is a very long way.

第一步：视频扩展 (Video Extension)

One of the first steps at Grok Imagine, where I led a small world model team, was to build video extension. So video extension is the first step of interactivity.

We have it here. Video editing.

The first step—because this unlocks long horizon. For most of the video generation models, you give it a prompt or an image as the initial frame, generate a video, that's it. That's just one time, done. Some creators try to use the last frame as the first frame for the second video. Sometimes it works, but if you do it a few times the quality degrades and it doesn't have context over the full video.

Because you only gave it the last frame.

Exactly. But it's actually a pretty fun hack.

Oh no, he has something better.

For example, Veo—I remember Veo 3 has a 1-second context of the last video. It's slightly better than using the last frame, but it has the same problem—the quality would degrade. If you extend a few times to one minute, the video quality would look much worse than the first video. Another problem: the model doesn't have long-range knowledge of what's happening before. So if they generate some dialogue, two people speaking, their voice might change over some time—especially if the 1-second conditioning doesn't cover the previous context.

These are the core challenges. So Grok Imagine video extension has historical context of all of the previous generated videos. It has context of who's speaking, what objects have appeared, everything—having that to generate the next video.

If we naively do this—put all the previous history video tokens into the context—the context length will easily explode, especially for video models. That can be a few million context, I would imagine.

For example, in Cosmos I think just 5 seconds of video is 50k or 60k tokens. If you do 50 seconds, that's 500k tokens. If you do longer than that, it easily explodes.

This long-horizon problem—with the first step we're trying to solve world model. It turns out people love video extension. A lot of creators love using video extension to create longer-form videos.

This is the part I like—that you have an intermediate step toward the final goal instead of just a straight shot to the final version.

But I can see you have a strong vision of where we want to end up.

工程视角：scale 还是新方法？

Does it seem like an efficiency issue? Okay, we're at a few million tokens context. If you draw the parallel to language models—we had very short context, 2,000, 8,000, then you scale it up to 1 million, 10 million. Sure, there's effective context, but at the end of the day, it's just what's it worth? In video, it might be slightly easier because we have 100-million-token videos—just take a movie with the full context there.

Is this efficiency from an inference standpoint where it's expensive but we know how to solve it? Or why is this not the approach?

My broader point: on your second point of world models, you say it needs to be interactive and live—you should be able to play a game and see the interaction live. One thing I see with research: a lot of what you actually serve is different from what you build. We talked about distillation—you train big models, you distill them, you do quantization, speculative decoding. We do all this stuff to serve it efficiently.

Should we not just have a solution—a world model that can interact well—and do inference optimization, serve it, distill it secondary? Make it real time after you solve it.

Another parallel: continual learning. What we need is someone to solve it and show it works inefficiently. Give it a few years, people will make it efficient. Same thing with regular attention—it worked over a few years, people have different forms of attention, and we've scaled it to be efficient at long context.

It seems like it works, you've scaled it—can we not just scale it a lot more efficiently over time? Do we need a separate approach if this works?

That's actually a very good point. In videos there's actually a lot of redundancies. We solved a lot of the pixel redundancy from VAE, but there's more redundancy in long-range and long-horizon videos.

Say if a character appears in the first clip and then it disappears—it only reappears at the end of the video. You probably don't need the context in the middle of generation. You only need that character where you need.

Reference-to-Video：另一种长上下文方案

That's why I helped build another feature—reference to video.

Is it the same model release?

It's a different one. You probably need to search "Grok reference to video."

Reference-to-video allows you to upload up to seven images as condition and generate a video. It can be characters or objects or even scenes. I want it conditioned on Sean's selfie and holding a blade.

You put the dog in the thing.

You can put them there and the video models will generate the video and copy the context over. That can solve a lot of problems like the long context problem. It doesn't need to have a very long context, but it's an intermediate solution.

The model should be able to selectively know where to draw references. Say if I want to generate a movie, I generate it autoregressively—10 seconds at a time. Now this character appears, I can look back to where it first appeared and bring that back.

I put the references—Optimus, Einstein, myself, Annie.

Oddly enough, I used Grok search to find it and it pulled your LinkedIn post, but we found it.

XAI 的沟通问题

Okay, this is a problem—not your fault, but XAI doesn't communicate all this work that you do very well. They just have the model release, and that's it. But actually these details are very, very good. As far as I understand, everything you just described is state-of-the-art. Like no one else has done it.

Thanks.

A lot of—and then you just put this blog post with the cookies. I'm like, this is not enough.

But obviously this is the high-level numbers that people want to know.

But some labs don't share research into what happens. No, but this is literally bragging about how good they are. Why would you not say that you are capable of extending with full context? This is not a secret sauce. This is "we did the work."

Different labs have slightly different communication styles.

Anyway, if anyone from XAI is listening, we are always happy to help you tell your story.

上下文管理：从启发式到模型自管

You did references, and I think the point you're making is like this is sort of a kludge. You can do seven, but what about 100? Then you need a completely different thing.

This is a mechanism to select context from the history—you might not put the entire history into the context. For example, there's a paper called FramePack which has a heuristic: the latest history, the last one second, I put the entire history. The history before that, I would compress and make the video smaller. They follow this pattern—a beautiful pattern that the maximum sequence length is fixed. The further you are from the current frame, you have a smaller image.

This is just a heuristic. I think it can be more automatic—the model is aware which part of the history can be selected. This part of research is actually being actively worked on by a lot of people.

This part of long context is a little bit ahead of the LLM part. For example, in LLMs, if the context keeps growing—let's say you call a tool and the tool call history is extremely long, that's still in context and keeps growing. Even if you switch the topic to something else, the whole context would be there.

There are some agentic harnesses that help you prune the tool results—when you query a file, only show the top 200 lines or something. But those were very heuristic-driven.

For listeners, we did a writeup on the Claude Code leak where there are eight different kinds of pruning. Including, you prune the tool results and all that. You can read up on that.

One breakthrough in context length might be a way to automatically manage its own. These are all heuristics and they will be replaced by machine learning. Interestingly, the same thing is being researched in both LLMs and video models.

The interesting thing is also—in the paper you showed, it's actually happening at the model level. Compared to language model—sure, we have base attention, but we'll do our own compression, we'll do our own pruning, which is separate from model error. Eventually it all just boils in, hopefully.

I think this is a form of attention, but also sort of reasoning attention. I feel like that's different than normal attention. Does that make sense?

Yeah. It's different in the sense that—attention, not to mention sparse attention—normal attention, you have to attend to all the tokens. You don't have a high-level mechanism to drop which tokens you don't want to attend to.

As humans, our attention span is surprisingly small.

You can only remember 11 digits of a phone number.

But I have feature detection. I can detect "oh, that's a sequence of 1 2 3 4 in a phone number that is 11 digits."

Very good pattern matchers.

But humans' attention can work because we can dynamically pull in context from different places. The same mechanism, I think, is going to happen for LLMs and video models.

RLMs is some of the recent work, which is not that crazy, but it's just recursive.

I think it's somewhat inherent in models too. Here's a nice example. Language models are also very good at slop parsing. I throw my typos in there—it doesn't matter. You have a transcript, you have whatever, just throw it in—it's very good at parsing through noise. That may be brute force—it can look over it, reason over it. But there's parallels to both.

XAI 文化与从零到一

I think it's really fascinating how you relate the world model stuff to video generation. I don't think a lot of people hear directly from people like you.

Any other work—did we cover video, audio, world models? Any other stuff in the omni team or other work at XAI you want to talk about? Any underrated stuff?

I feel the culture is quite interesting and a bit underrated. The culture is three sentences: move fast, build, no goal is too ambitious. And the first principle. Usually the goal set was very ambitious—it wasn't possible to achieve when first thinking about it.

Like for example, "I could build something in 3 months" was that "okay, we're starting a team, we want image, we want video, do it by this deadline"? How do you work back? Was it just "we have a rough date, we want something out"?

That's a very good point—it's from first principle thinking. People might say first principle thinking applies more to the physical world than the models. I would say, for example, if you think about limitations—acquiring data: how fast can we acquire the videos? If you think about training the models: what's the iteration speed for training a model end-to-end, and how would adding more GPUs accelerate that timeline? If you need human data: what's the turnaround time for human data to arrive?

If you put all of those together, that is first principle thinking—what is the minimum number of days possible to achieve something.

I think this is a lot of Elon's type of thinking. He's famous for saying that the only law you can't break is the laws of physics.

与 Elon 共事的体验

Just broadly, you worked a lot with Elon?

One benefit—working at XAI, you got a chance to interact more with Elon. I was very fortunate to get a few retreats from him, and that was quite fun. He also worked very closely with people—like people imagine online, he's very hands-on.

Grok Voice 与 Elon 的转推

Two things. One, I was actually looking up Elon retweeting you. I'll pull it up. He talked about you tweeting that you have a really good voice mode.

I don't—no, no, no. Him.

Oh, I also did it.

I would DM you feedback on voice mode because I was like, "Wow, really good." And then I'm like, "Oh, this sucks." But, I don't know. Anything you want to talk about your voice mode, building it? Was it a team you worked on?

That's actually not a part of the team I worked on.

Probably worked on more the video.

But Grok voice—actually, very good. This is one of those things where first of all you can speak at 2x, which is fun. I listen to 2x so I like to speak at 2x. But also I think the interruption was better than Gemini. I don't know how it compares to ChatGPT real-time now. But as far as driving was concerned, having Grok in my Tesla and driving—it's a really good experience.

He likes voice mode but also—just the crazy reach. 50 million views. Are just saying yes truth?

True.

Oh my god.

It's pretty cool how fast it came out.

视频生成的安全与水印问题

The other thing is the safety aspect of video mode. Anything interesting there?

Spicy question.

A lot of countries don't allow generative AI videos without watermarks. So in all of those countries, Grok Imagine had watermarks, and a lot of the takedowns of the videos were also happening extremely fast.

It's part of running a social platform, but also it transfers nicely to the gen AI side. Do you have a perspective on SynthID versus other kinds of watermarking?

It's going to be harder and harder to detect these things. SynthID—previously it was only Google, and now a lot of different labs are also adapting it. The limitation: the paper was out there and people can reverse engineer how to get rid of it. Even as it advances, it's still possible to reverse engineer it.

If you are interested, you can go on to Reddit and people have taken out the exact pattern that Google applies, and then you can apply it onto any Google-generated photo and you can reverse out the SynthID.

It's harder and harder to just judge by eyes. I remember a couple years ago there were six fingers or something—it's very obvious. My current way to tell is actually the audio. I feel like the audio is really lacking. My way to tell if something is AI-generated outside of—I think I've seen enough, I have a decent eye—the audio matchup, especially of Sora, is not great. It's all similar style.

Those are minor imperfections. My closest reference to this is also Ian Goodfellow, because he did the adversarial GAN thing where it's like, here's a picture of a zebra, then you change one pixel and it becomes a panda. Classic computer vision issue.

If you think about how these models were trained—like I mentioned, GAN was in the training process. The objective: the model generates an image, and there's a judge to tell if the image is real or not. The model is trained to make the image more real. As the model becomes more and more advanced, it's going to be harder and harder. For me personally now, I have to judge by whether these videos have logical sense.

Have a world model.

The audio is too nice, too studio quality. The lighting is too good. The skin is too clear. Basically the lack of imperfections.

推理与视频生成：核心论断

Do we have a good way to do reasoning in diffusion? Is that what separates video generators from world models? We really know how to apply it to autoregressive language models. Is there a parallel for diffusion video gen world models?

On that point, right, he has a thing on video agents.

大胆论断：视觉智能主要来自语言

That's a good question. I have a pretty big claim: visual intelligence is actually mostly coming from language. These video models, especially from now—since diffusion model technology is more mature—every time you see there's some improvement on these models, I would say mostly the gain comes from language models, not coming from the video diffusion model itself.

In Cosmos, typically these models have two parts: there's a prompt rewriter or prompt upsampler. In Cosmos we use Llama or Mixtral. The Cosmos video model itself is only 7B, and the language model that is the prompt rewriter is bigger than that.

The prompt rewriter's task is to take a user instruction and convert it to an extremely detailed description of the video. Because the video diffusion models, I would describe, are kind of dumb—they take the input instruction literally. In the training process, remember, we have to describe the video as detailed as possible when we are creating the synthetic text pair. So these models take that kind of instruction to generate videos.

When you're taking the user instruction—the user instruction is usually very simple, just say "a cat" or something. If you put "a cat" in the video model, they would take that instruction literally. They would literally show a cat in maybe a white background, because you didn't describe the background. The cat is not moving, because you didn't describe it. It takes the instruction quite literally. It's kind of dumb.

The prompt rewriter is actually a much bigger model—a language model that takes the user instruction and expands it. The "thinking process" you mentioned is from there. If you look at GPT Image—you generate an image in 3 minutes. It's not all pixel generation. A lot of time is spent in thinking.

Prompt rewriting 进化为 Agent

Prompt rewriting now has evolved to not only just thinking—it can also be an agentic model. For example, say you wanted to generate the image of today's news. So it's likely it'll go to fetch today's news online, then process them, dagger them, organize the layout, and generate it.

If I'm not mistaken these are no longer a diffusion model though, right? Autoregressively or—

There are different approaches. For example, like Qwen Omni—since they said it's Omni, I believe it's a single model. Maybe it's something like a language model with a diffusion head, where the language model does the thinking, the agent, the tool calling, and then it would use the diffusion head to generate the image in the end.

There were also approaches like Cosmos where you have a separate language model and separate diffusion model. And there are also purely language model approaches where you discretize the images and then generate an image as discrete tokens. So there are different approaches.

One of the claims I've seen for why these approaches struggle: a lot of the benefits for how we currently learn reasoning with language models is you basically iteratively generate, reason, you have your thought, and then you work on that answer. If you have an Omni model and then diffusion head, you can't feed that back in to continue reasoning. You can't go text-image-text-image. You can't reason on the output and then go back to diffusion. But I guess in the new Gemini Omni, you would be able to as long as you have diffusion.

I'm not sure if they have that process. It's definitely possible in the Omni paradigm.

If you think about traditional multimodal language models, they would have a ViT encoder that can encode the image. If they have a diffusion head, they can generate the image, put that back into the ViT encoder, encode that, and then do iterative refinement of the result.

I think you have to jointly train the ViT and the diffusion to make that somewhat reasonable. Otherwise you're kind of mismatching or feeding in slop.

I think it depends on your stage of training—you might be able to freeze it.

Also just on your earlier—I wanted to make explicit: we do know that Nano Banana and GPT Image are autoregressive language model with diffusion head. As far as I can tell from your description of Grok Image, it is not. It is end-to-end.

But I think there's different approaches, right? You started off saying prompt rewriter is a big part of the intelligence.

早期扩散模型的提示语贴标签风格

Even on that—I think everyone should try using an early diffusion model. If you've used Stable Diffusion 1 or whatever, you've seen the prompts: "ultra high-res 4K this style"—oh my god, the first time I tried one—you don't talk to them like language models. Your prompting is very comma-separated. Literally talking in the labels that were in the dataset.

But basically I'm just trying to make the point that prompt rewriter and then image is different from autoregressive language model with diffusion head. They're different things.

Yes. They're different.

The common part is the image part. So it's quite surprising that a lot of the improvement came from the language—the thinking, the tool calling.

I still remember in Cosmos I generated "a happy sheep" and without any rewriting it looks so CGI. After rewriting it looks so beautiful.

Without any joint training?

Yeah, without any joint training. With rewriting it's already much better.

Video Agents：下一波浪潮

A very interesting thing I guess will happen is video agents—mostly language models that call these generative models, either as a separate model or diffusion head, as a tool. This model can iteratively refine the results or even generate longer content through a very long train of thought.

It's actually very similar to how humans create art. We don't generate the pixels directly. We iteratively draw something. Through this process these models not only use diffusion as one of the tools, they can also use traditional tools—image editing tools from Photoshop, video editors, FFmpeg, whatever. Taking the combination of these and the generative AI technology as a set of tools, they can iteratively create much better video for production-grade quality.

If you look at existing professional creators, they don't end at generating a video from these models. They will take this video to their editor and edit here and there. So much post-production. Sometimes the reason the video is good is not really the video model—it's actually the editing.

Yes, we also are engaged in the same process as well. Would you love to use a video editing model?

Yeah, actually there's the Grok Imagine agent beta—that was the first attempt in that direction.

No blog post for it.

Maybe generate a one-minute video—which is not possible if you ask the same prompt to video models, but this model will iteratively call different tools to do that.

When we first released a video editing model, I saw on X some people try the video editing feature with "edit this video to be one minute." Because they didn't understand how video editing works—video editing typically is just removal, add, replace, transfer kind of thing. But that's actually a valid request under the assumption of video agents. These agents should be able to understand this kind of long-horizon, create a long-form video.

This is really fascinating because it's taking the same direction as: first you have AI-assisted coding, like tab completion and GitHub Copilot. From there you gradually evolve to Codex and Claude Code, where you do things fully automated. In Grok Imagine agent mode, you can still go in there and do stuff by yourself, but gradually as the model capability increases, it will be able to do everything fully automated.

I like that. Okay, so it looks like it's still generating.

Grok 推理速度的优势

I did notice Grok mid-gen was always very fast. I don't know if this is something you guys benchmark, but this is just a tangent. Compared to when I used the latest OpenAI image gen and same with Gemini Nano Banana, I would often use Grok just for—

It's in the benchmark somewhere in the imagine API blog post that they have all the speed things. It mostly combination of distillation plus inference.

There are a bunch of things—we talked about distillation. If you talk about thinking—if you don't have any thinking budget, the model can just think 3 minutes and then come back to you. And also, the inference infra team was very talented and they were able to accelerate a hell lot of these models.

Video Agents 的反高潮

My comment on the video agents thing—I'm trying to figure out: when people say video agents... when you initially told me about your bet on video agents, I was a little bit disappointed. I was like, "you mean models are tapped out now, we have to do agents?"

But I think you have to, right? The question now is: how much model training is really going to make a difference versus just building a better harness? Like you said, the models don't have to be jointly trained. You can just take an off-the-shelf frontier reasoning model, slap it on a harness, give it Grok as a tool—that's it. That's your video agent. Doesn't seem super satisfying.

Obviously you can co-train and get some more percentage points of proper performance. But if your central claim is that the majority of video or generative media alpha is actually coming from language intelligence and not image diffusion or video diffusion, then that is the future. It's primarily just wait.

If you pop back at the example—sorry to interrupt—it generated frames. It's been saying like "okay, I'm going to start stitching these frames together," it's using FFmpeg.

This is what GPT Image Pro as well is doing—it's also just writing code in the background and then stitching, doing an image pass on the final output.

It feels dissatisfying for the people who want to just train models.

It's interesting, right? It's also somewhat exciting like you brought up earlier—a lot of the gains don't come as much from the video.

I think you can see that in the language model space too. Anthropic—very, very good at coding. Their multimodal not the best. They have basic input PDF, but there's clearly a disconnect in the quality of their image, video processing, audio processing—yet intelligence very top tier. Other labs—Gemini, OpenAI, XAI—you can add modalities, but it's not like they're unlocking crazy capabilities.

It's interesting to see that the video models' capability increase actually comes from language models being more intelligent.

Video agents can unlock more stuff that you might imagine. One thing: when we're prompting these models, most people are actually not very good at prompting. Language models have a better sense of how to prompt AI models. AI models know AI models better. If you jointly train these models, maybe the model has a better sense of how to prompt each model.

It might not be as simple as "generate a few clips and slap them together using FFmpeg." There might be more image and video editing tools appearing in this process. Say if you want to exactly add a blob of text at this timestep—the video models might not get that intention very precisely, but these are possible using deterministic tools. Video agents can use all sorts of tools. So you don't have to put all of the capabilities into the diffusion model itself.

时间线预测

I think you're right. I think this will be a big category. Probably you are predicting the next one year in video is going to be all this.

Do you have a time prediction for when this stuff ramps up?

They already started. Is so good. I think the last one's just longer.

It didn't give me a minute, you gave me 36 seconds. But are we feeling it now? Is there going to be an inflection? Any timeline predictions you want to make?

I guess by the end of this year, this is going to be a big hit. The inflection point will be where the videos generated by video agents can get to production-grade quality—it can be presented and distributed in ads. Once that happens, I think the enterprise will have much more budget for video models.

The agents are inherently more expensive than the video models themselves, because they do this iterative process—they generate many variations. But once these models pass this usability threshold, it's going to be exponential growth beyond that.

I would fund a company right now based on this. So I think you're right.

机器人与世界模型

One thing I'm reflecting on the whole past hour conversation—I think you're into world models and video generation for video generation sake. There are a lot of other world models people. We've interviewed a lot of them—General Intuition and Phi, all those guys, and Moondream which I told you about. Moonlake.

Moonlake.

I keep saying Moondream. Goddammit. Moonlake.

A lot of them actually say robotics is the endgame—embodied robotics. You want real time, you want interactive. It is to interact with the physical world. You're not that concerned about it.

I think robotics will be a big part of it for sure. The process might happen naturally. My prediction on robotics: the problem of physical AI might be solved without actually needing to be in the real world. It might get solved by a video LLM with very strong video capability.

Remember we talked about the real-time interactive long-horizon video. Now these models are just training on screen recordings and computer screens. Once these models can use computers and understand the future state of computer extremely well, the robots might be one of the tools that a very powerful AI can use. The powerful AI might just be able to control the physical embodiment naturally.

I see that for sure.

为何离开 XAI

I know we're coming up on time. You had left one more spicy topic—why you left XAI.

For me, there's a lot of research I want to do that I cannot do as a company. Also the priorities and objective of a company typically can change very fast. It's also the same for XAI. Now is kind of the time—there's some research I want to do, especially more on the language model side, that I cannot do at XAI.

You're basically leaving—you had this whole transition from computer vision to world models to video generation, to now focusing on LLMs.

But it seems like you said focusing on LLMs—you really in the past hour described how it all ties together, right?

I don't know what you mean by focusing on LLMs. Is there—

I realized the fact that video models—even in the beginning the gain might come from improvement on diffusion technology, but this is a point where actually most of the gain comes from the language models themselves.

Is a huge black pill for anyone who has spent a career in generative media.

I mean, that's an extreme view. You still definitely need a bit of both, right?

There's just—it seems like more pressing, impactful work to do now on language model side.

下一年的 LLM 预测

Do you have any similar predictions—you predict video agents. I think you will be right on the language side. What are you looking for in the next one year?

One thing pretty interesting I think might be happening soon: language models will be context-aware and manage their own context.

From the video model side, we've been suffering from the long-horizon issue—we want to generate videos longer and longer, and we've been trying to solve the context length issues through various ways. One is just brute-forcing—train longer context length. Another is to manage the context better. I think the same thing in language models is also going to be happening soon.

For example, longish models—they're not aware of how long their own context length is. Once they hit 80% or something, the automatic context compaction is triggered, and the model is not aware of that when it's working. Maybe it's good for the models to know "oh, I'm approaching 80% or something."

In Open Claude—every time you type in something, the current local time is automatically attached to your message. So the model actually knows what time is it. This is making the model time-aware. Also, in tool calling, a lot of the intermediate tool call results are automatically pruned. So there's context removal, context addition, and context compaction. All of these are from the harnesses themselves.

But from our experience, the heuristic engineering—all of those get absorbed into the models themselves. So that's something very interesting to explore.

Infinite context.

Maybe. It's interesting—it is in the space of memory and continual learning.

I don't know. It's also like in the space of agent harness use.

He's saying he doesn't want to do it in a harness.

No, no, but models are also being trained on using harnesses. Some of it is implicitly leaking in. Part of post-training of language models is using it in coding harnesses, in which case when are sub-agents spawned, when is compaction going to happen? It's not explicit—you have this much token window, which I don't know if you want it to be is that'll change, but it's somewhat leaking in there.

I'm imagining what if the model has access to the whole code of the agent harness itself and is able to modify it whatever you want. Say if the agent harness is short enough, you can just put it in the context length in the system prompt, and then the model says "when I want to spawn a future version of myself, I can modify the agent harness." For example, if the agent harness is when I'm reading a long document, I can choose to read the whole thing in chunks and come back, smash the summary together. Or I can just read the first 200 lines and discard the rest. All kinds of choices, if they can be made by the model themselves, it might be very interesting—the model can program itself online at test time.

The self-modifying harness is also part of Open Claude and Pi. But I think there's a lot more work to do there.

职业转型的反思

I think part of me is kind of curious—you are part of a big lab and there's this career path of a researcher at a big lab: you train models, you get more compute, you train better models, you keep going. Somewhat I feel like you're opting out of that. If I were you I'd be like, "oh I think this is a bit of a career risk." You know what I mean?

I don't have any comment apart from like—you're very strongly convicted. I think a lot of people in your shoes would not be doing what you did.

Speaking of my career, if I look back—there were a lot of huge transitions. 10 years ago I was doing research with the ResNet authors—Shaoqing, Jian, and Jensen. At that time the research was completely different—mostly computation, like image recognition, object detection, object tracking. I was also doing neural net compression at that time. It was quite different from knowledge distillation these days.

At that time I wanted to be a professor. When I applied for PhD, I already had a few first-author papers at top conferences. So I confidently applied to top schools, but turns out I got rejected by all of the top PhD programs. So I had to go to industry. At that time I was at Facebook AI Research, FAIR, led by—

I want to talk about V-JEPA but it's different.

Anyway, we can leave it for another time. I switched to self-supervised learning at that time. It was quite different from what I was doing in computer vision.

After that it's Nvidia Cosmos. I realized scaling up was extremely important. At Nvidia I was mainly focusing on scaling. One thing is Cosmos—scaling the video description models to a few billion parameters. Another thing: I was working on Megatron, which was the first framework open-sourced to be able to train these at very large scales—like 100 billion parameters, even trillions of parameters, efficiently at like 40% MFU.

Switching to XAI was trying to work on even larger compute scale.

Looking at this trajectory, I actually worked on a lot of different things. I feel actually within ML, it's actually easier to switch. People might have manifested that "oh, I work on computer vision, I always have to work on computer vision, I cannot switch to language." But from my experience, at least at Nvidia, I worked on both language models and video models—it's actually not the case. A lot of the core principles of how to train large models are largely the same.

Right now, the bottleneck for video models is actually the language part—the agent—which is why I want to work more on LLM. One thing is it's a bit of a challenge, but I don't think it's a huge jump.

结语

Kudos to you. I think you have a lot of strong vision there. I think that was mostly everything that we wanted to cover. You've been very generous with your time, and it's really nice that you are able to share all these things now—we don't have to go through XAI to clear everything. But also we didn't get you in trouble.

It's a lot of good stuff about XAI compared to what you just see in the releases. You don't realize how many more levels there are to it.

XAI, please do more podcasts. Anyway, thank you for sharing. It's been very kind. I want to hear more from you. You're going to embark on your next phase—you haven't announced what you're doing next, but clearly you have more vision and more ambition on this path. You're basically kind of gradient descenting to whatever your final form is.

Thank you. I'll share more about my next chapter soon.

Okay.

Thank you for having me.

Thanks for coming.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个让人意外的反直觉发现：视觉模型的智能，主要来自语言
Ethan 抛出的核心论断极具颠覆性——**"视觉智能其实大部分来自语言"**。这不只是技术细节，而是对整个生成式媒体行业的"黑色药丸"：
- 视频扩散模型本身其实"很笨"，它会把"一只猫"逐字翻译成白底静止的猫。 - 真正让 GPT-Image 显得聪明的，是那个在背后花 3 分钟"思考"、改写、规划构图的语言模型。 - Cosmos 中的 prompt rewriter 比 7B 的视频模型本身还要大。
这意味着过去十年很多"视觉研究者"的护城河正在被语言模型悄悄吃掉。Ethan 自己作为顶级视觉研究者选择转向 LLM，就是用职业路径在为这个判断下注——这是一种用脚投票的诚实，比任何论文都更有信号价值。
## 跨领域连接：扩散前端 + 确定性后端 = 软件的"反编译"
最让我"啊哈"的是 Ethan 描绘的未来界面架构： - **现在**：用户意图 → LLM 写代码 → 编译成二进制 → 渲染像素 - **未来**：用户意图 → 扩散模型 → 像素
这与三个看似无关的领域形成了惊人共振：
1. **认知心
